posts.title: July 28 Quick Notes
posts.excerpt: By 7:20 PM, the most urgent tasks for today were mostly done. There were still a few non-urgent orders I simply couldn't finish, so I made a list and left it for Monday. I'd already braced myself for ...
posts.content:
By 7:20 PM, the most urgent tasks for today were mostly done. There were still a few non-urgent orders I simply couldn't finish, so I made a list and left it for Monday. I'd already braced myself for weekend overtime anyway—if I can take a day off, I'll take it. This whole week has been nothing but nonstop busyness, and every day I've stayed an extra hour or more. The only real break during the day was the short lunch break, and I'm exhausted. After sitting in the chair for over six hours straight, constantly thinking, my head started throbbing faintly as I walked out of the office. I'm genuinely worried one day I'll just drop dead at the keyboard.

Weige called me at six, asking me to bring some groceries home on my way back. It was his way of reminding me that I'd promised to cook for them tonight—something I'd completely forgotten. Around 7:40, I bought twenty yuan worth of cold pig ears and half a duck. The duck was only thirteen yuan and weighed more than the pig ears. Is pork really more expensive than duck, or is it just the ears? As expected, when I got home the rice cooker was the only thing warm—apparently if I didn't come back, they weren't worried about starving to death. Following their habit of always having soup with meals, I quickly made a pot of seaweed and egg-drop soup, tossed in a few dried shrimp, and served it.

I washed the dishes too, and I didn't mind. Their kitchen-cleaning habits drive me crazy, so I've learned to look the other way. They were very obedient though—when I told them to wash the dishes, they did. But for fuck's sake, who leaves a pot caked with egg and seaweed sitting on the stove? They never clean the drain strainer either; you could scoop out half a bowl of rice from it at any time. I seriously wanted to mix that leftover rice with seaweed and shrimp, roll it into sushi, and stuff it back into their mouths. On top of that, none of these guys mop the floor or take out the trash. They just complain about the smell while staying glued to their computers. I'm losing my mind. Last night I bagged up the garbage and left it right in the middle of the doorway. This morning I figured it would be gone, but fuck—no, the bag was still sitting there exactly where I'd left it. Did those two gods I live with fly out the window to work? Multiple reminders have had zero effect. Life is full of hardships; all I can do is sing my sorrows away.

After washing the dishes, wiping the table, and mopping the floor—and while I was at it, cleaning the fridge—I put two more bags of trash by the door…

posts.title: It's Still Shaking Today!!!
posts.excerpt: This morning, after breakfast, a few of us from the dorm were heading back when Aguo spotted a senior selling his old computer — a Celeron CPU, 80GB hard drive, going for 1000 yuan. We figured we'd go...
posts.content: This morning, after breakfast, a few of us from the dorm were heading back when Aguo spotted a senior selling his old computer — a Celeron CPU, 80GB hard drive, going for 1000 yuan. We figured we'd go check out just how bad it really was. After getting lost once, we finally found the guy. The moment our dorm's resident tech wizard touched the computer, he nearly bricked it. While he was working on it, the senior suddenly jumped up and shouted that there was an earthquake. Another guy poked his head out of the bathroom — probably mid-shit — and asked if it was really an earthquake. No one knew what was going on. Our tech wizard just kept tinkering with the computer. Zhang Shaoxia, who was from Mianyang, looked genuinely scared — he'd come to our school to escape the earthquake in the first place. I didn't feel anything. Maybe I'd become numb. I went to the window and looked down; people below were panicking. We just kept looking at the computer, ignoring the earthquake. In the end, our tech wizard declared it was absolute garbage, so we headed downstairs. Damn it. Everything felt off. On the way back to the dorm, I had to say it — I was really pissed. Why the hell did we have to live on the goddamn tenth floor, climbing those soul-crushing stairs? Our school was cheap enough to skip installing elevators and put the main entrance on the fourth floor. Just as we finally made it upstairs, Li was coming down. He had been in Xinjiang during the college entrance exam and missed the May 12 earthquake. He said everyone in our building had already evacuated — what were we doing going back up? So at his urging, I grabbed my stuff and went back down with them. At that moment, I was seriously considering just leaving for Chengdu. It felt like running away. Depressing. They went out with Li to buy speakers — yesterday he bought a computer but didn't get speakers, only a pair of those cheap hanging earphones.

posts.title: Looking Back — May 12 Anniversary Memories
posts.excerpt: On this day last year I was still in high school. It was a hot, stuffy day. My roommate and I were napping in the dorm. Classes started at 3, so my alarm was set for 2:30. The alarm is ridiculously lo...
posts.content:
On this day last year I was still in high school. It was a hot, stuffy day. My roommate and I were napping in the dorm. Classes started at 3, so my alarm was set for 2:30. The alarm is ridiculously loud—normally the second it goes off I'm awake. The instant it rang, I bolted upright to turn it off, but I felt the bed shaking. I thought I was dizzy. My classmate woke up too. I stared at him in confusion; he stared back the same way.

"Uh... do you feel the bed shaking?" I asked, unsure.

"It's shaking!"

My first thought was someone was using a washing machine, but I immediately dismissed it—how would there be a washing machine in the dorm? I jumped up and ran to the window. The window was shaking. People were rushing out the main gate. Shit—the mountain behind the building had collapsed. The building was going to fall. I spun around and started running without even fully registering it was an earthquake, yelling "xxx, run! Earthquake!" He was already sitting on the edge of the bed. I don't know why I noticed so clearly at the time—he was wearing gray-green flip-flops. He jumped up and took off, and I followed right behind. I yanked open the door and sprinted out of the room. A few meters later we hit the stairwell. Not many people yet, but everyone was rushing down. I saw one of our classmates from the other side of the building (our room was isolated around the corner from the rest of the class) also bolting. Later he told me he'd been shouting "Earthquake!" while running, but strangely I didn't hear it. Had everything gone silent? I was probably too panicked.

We flew down the stairs. We were on the second floor, so reaching the first floor and the exit took only seconds. Once outside we split up. I ran toward the slope beside the building—I figured it was just a small hill with a gentler incline on this side, so a landslide was unlikely. Running anywhere else would put us in the collapse radius of the surrounding buildings, which was far more dangerous.

I crouched on a small platform. The ground was still trembling. Below me the crowd was in chaos, all staring up at our building, probably still thinking it was about to collapse. I scanned the crowd for my classmates. People everywhere, but none of mine.

A few minutes later the earth finally calmed. I thought it was over—earthquakes are supposed to be one quick jolt, not something that keeps going—so I went back to the dorm. The door was still open. I changed shoes, locked up, and went downstairs. I ran into a few classmates and said I was heading to class. They looked at me like I was crazy: "Class? What class?"

I tried calling my grandparents—couldn't get through. Same with Chengdu. Then the school broadcast came on telling everyone to gather on the sports field. Half an hour later an old man on Central People's Radio reported the epicenter was in Wenchuan, over a hundred kilometers from Chengdu, magnitude 7.2. That's when the real fear hit me. Almost all my relatives and my parents were in Chengdu. I frantically dialed Chengdu numbers, but nothing connected. That night I slept on the field still trying to reach home. Finally, late at night, I heard familiar voices. Everyone was fine. Chengdu was barely affected, and neither was my hometown. My heart finally stopped racing.

I didn't sleep a wink. The radio kept updating the death toll in Wenchuan. Each number felt like a knife to the chest.

On the first anniversary of the Wenchuan earthquake, I'm writing down these memories. This post goes live at 14:28. May the dead rest in peace, and the living strive forward!

posts.title: The Thorn Birds
posts.excerpt: This novel is hailed in Western literary circles as "the author's most successful family saga and love story since The Thorn Birds." In this book, Colleen McCullough mercilessly lashes out at the evil...
posts.content:
This novel is hailed in Western literary circles as "the author's most successful family saga and love story since The Thorn Birds." In this book, Colleen McCullough mercilessly lashes out at the evils of human nature woven from ignorance, backwardness, and religious dogma a hundred years ago, while passionately celebrating the brilliance that erupts from trampled civilization and ravaged humanity. As one review noted: "This is a colorful story about colorful characters in a colorful time and place." That pretty much sums up the artistic achievement of The Thorn Birds.

Alexander the Great must have lived a glorious life, and the male protagonist, Alexander Ronan, is also someone who refuses to settle for mediocrity. The hands that built Ronan City were incredibly resilient. In that era, the Industrial Revolution was in full swing—anyone whose mind could grasp the steam age would rise rapidly. What is wisdom, really? I wish I knew. Then there's Nell, a miraculous girl who represents another tendency of the world after the turn of the century.

But a family with such extraordinary experiences—they're truly brilliant actors. Elizabeth stayed quietly in the background yet was everywhere. Ruby seeped into everyone's heart—some tragic, some joyful, some carefree. Ah, only from deep ravines can such remarkable people emerge. Nell is that brilliant, unmistakable Venus. Anna flashes by too quickly.

Sometimes I'm a little scared—Li feels like Ralph's shadow.

But the author is so masterful that once you're immersed in the book, all you want to do is absorb the miracle of the story. Those occasional first-person passages make you feel like you've become one of the actors in the tale. Cathartic, isn't it?

posts.title: Short Thoughts
posts.excerpt: It's raining in Chengdu. It stopped quickly, and now all I can hear is the hum of the air conditioner and the sound of raindrops dripping onto the awning outside—making the night feel especially quiet...
posts.content:
It's raining in Chengdu. It stopped quickly, and now all I can hear is the hum of the air conditioner and the sound of raindrops dripping onto the awning outside—making the night feel especially quiet. I rarely experience the night like this. Nights are supposed to be this way, but the occasional time it actually registers feels special. I've been so busy, so caught up in the rush, that I've forgotten to savor life. So many meaningful things slip right past me. No matter how hard I try to recall the past—what I ate, what I did—it's all a blank. This programmed existence has made me lazy about thinking and addicted to laziness. Any attempt to struggle feels pale and powerless. I try to change, but in the end I find myself helplessly standing back at the starting point.

I've been trying to read some poetry. I downloaded some on eMule—Shu Ting, Gu Cheng, Bei Dao, Xu Zhimo. Only Xi Murong really resonates with me. Bei Dao and Gu Cheng are pretty good too. Maybe because I'm twenty-one. Maybe because Xi Murong was also in her twenties when she wrote that poem. Twenty-one—the age of love.

I'm also a bit of a literary youth. I used to be more so; now not entirely. These days I only read occasionally, not with the same frenzy as before. But what I read now has more depth, more insight. As you live, you gradually understand some truths that others can't quite put into words. Only through experience do you truly get it. That's why life is the best teacher, and we should be its diligent students.

posts.title: Some Things About My Cousin
posts.excerpt: My cousin recently came to stay with us, so I can't use the computer as freely anymore. After a while I have to hand it over to her. She just finished the college entrance exam and didn't do well. She...
posts.content:
My cousin recently came to stay with us, so I can't use the computer as freely anymore. After a while I have to hand it over to her. She just finished the college entrance exam and didn't do well. She's feeling pretty down and plans to retake it next year. Coming to Chengdu is partly to clear her head. Unfortunately, I haven't taken her out much. There aren't really any places in Chengdu I particularly want to go to anyway. A few times I offered to take her somewhere, but she didn't seem interested. Maybe she came to Chengdu just to get away from my uncle. He had high expectations for her, and this result must've been disappointing. He doesn't say anything, but you can feel the disappointment in the air.

She mentioned wanting to check out some universities in Chengdu. I remembered the time I passed the main gate of the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China and thought, "If you can walk in and out of here, someday I will too." We all know how that turned out. I took her to Xipu to wander around Southwest Jiaotong University, and I meant to show her Xihua University too, but I'm not very familiar with Xipu and never made it there. In the end we just took the 5-yuan high-speed rail back to Chengdu. I probably shouldn't have let her ride that train—her first time on something that nice. If she ends up taking regular trains to study elsewhere later, the contrast might be too big.

She went home today to start her senior year of high school for the retake. Dad drove her. I really wanted to go back and visit too, but Mom vetoed it pretty firmly, so I didn't push. This is the first time Mom's opposition actually worked. She's opposed plenty of my decisions before, but it almost never stuck. I'll let her have this one. I'm not going back with my cousin. I think she'll probably do much better on the next exam, but there's one uncertain factor: girls' psychology. I can't figure out how she thinks at all, and I don't know what to say to her. I'm worried she'll overthink everything during the coming year and make things harder for herself.

Whatever happens, I hope she makes it through smoothly.

posts.title: First Post of Summer Break
posts.excerpt: Yesterday I finally made it home. Took the old green-skinned train with no air conditioning. When I was in elementary school I rode the green train once—from my hometown to Chengdu. It was my first ti...
posts.content:
Yesterday I finally made it home. Took the old green-skinned train with no air conditioning.

When I was in elementary school I rode the green train once—from my hometown to Chengdu. It was my first time on a train and it felt exciting. It stopped and started constantly, and during the stops vendors would crowd around selling snacks. I still remember the aunt or uncle sitting across from us sighing that my cousin and I had been eating nonstop from the moment we boarded until we reached Chengdu. He was impressed. Last year I took the green train again and met an old man carrying a bamboo basket to the market. First time I'd heard of someone taking the train just to go to the market. Also the first time I thought people living in those mountains were pretty badass. Anyway, this trip back to Chengdu was my third time on the green train. No vendors selling snacks at the windows this time, and no old man heading to the market.

After getting off I wiped my face with a tissue. The tissue turned completely black. I didn't even want to imagine what my face looked like. Too embarrassed to keep showing it in public, I kept my head down, took the bus home, took a shower, and ate an egg for breakfast.

Everything sorted. Alright, I'm a free man now. I have endless time to waste—hell, I could squat in the bathroom all day if I wanted. I choose to waste it online. I really like our home broadband—sixty yuan a month and the speed's decent. Perfect for downloading. I installed unlimited eMule. People say this connection isn't great for gaming. I've never noticed, because I don't play games! Updated the computer's software, checked new emails, installed my beloved Microsoft YaHei. Checking email has become part of my daily routine, and falling in love with a font is like falling in love with a woman—nothing else compares.

Finally sent my roommate a message: "Haven't had such unrestricted internet in a long time. Feels a bit weird."

posts.title: Mid-Autumn Festival
posts.excerpt: It's Mid-Autumn Festival, yet it feels completely flavorless. The sun outside is scorching. I had planned to go out, but the conversation just fizzled and that was that. In our lives there's nothing w...
posts.content:
It's Mid-Autumn Festival, yet it feels completely flavorless. The sun outside is scorching. I had planned to go out, but the conversation just fizzled and that was that. In our lives there's nothing we absolutely must do, and nothing we absolutely mustn't do—at least during university years we can say that.

As a modern-day Indoorsy Person shouldering the great task of building the motherland, when it's time to relax I naturally turn to one or two anime or dramas to pass the loneliness. I've got The Big Bang Theory in my collection. Whenever I'm bored or in a bad mood, that show is genuinely good medicine. Sheldon's antics have literally made the corners of my mouth sore from smiling. Someone with that kind of genius-level IQ and zero emotional intelligence is genuinely hard to talk to. I've also been unconsciously imitating classic lines from the show. In Season 3 Episode 16, when Sheldon uses a slideshow to explain to Penny that he has to go to Switzerland in her place to visit the Large Hadron Collider, he says those two strange lines to her: "AKA me" and "AKA you." Couldn't find them on Baidu or Google. The Chinese subtitles translated them as "就是我" and "就是你." Later I found out on Wenwen that "AKA" is short for "Also Known As." Checked it—yep, exactly. A few hours of entertainment suddenly felt meaningful just because I learned an abbreviation.

Tieba says people like me who spend the whole Mid-Autumn Festival online must be under a lot of pressure. I agree. When I went to the cafeteria for lunch today, it should've been packed at that hour, but it was completely empty. The atmosphere seemed to be hinting that I should go out for a proper meal. So yeah, tonight our dorm is going out to eat. Last semester our whole class had a Mid-Autumn meal together. I was hoping for the same this semester, but there's been zero movement. Probably the direct result of rising CPI. Can't really blame the decline of social morals.

Whenever any holiday rolls around, it's also the day China Mobile's heart aches. People like us who have hundreds of texts a month but almost never use them suddenly become active. You send one, I send one back, but it always circles back to "Happy Holidays." There's a certain melancholy in that.

posts.title: National Day Yunnan Travel Notes
posts.excerpt: 1. Boarded the train after 11 PM last night. Stood for two hours before finding an empty seat. Kept dozing off but kept getting woken up by the cold—shouldn't have only worn a T-shirt. 2. Played on my...
posts.content:
1. Boarded the train after 11 PM last night. Stood for two hours before finding an empty seat. Kept dozing off but kept getting woken up by the cold—shouldn't have only worn a T-shirt.

2. Played on my phone the whole way, checking the route. Phone was about to die. After getting off, used Baidu Maps to find an internet café near the train station. Immediately went in, showed my ID, got a card, charged my phone, and waited for both the charge and daylight.

3. Couldn't stop thinking of that line from On the Road: "I'm always on the road."

4. Planning to buy a backpack and wander around Kunming's busiest area to see how it compares to the Land of Abundance. Yesterday at Jinlang I saw a pair of New Balance shoes I really liked, but hesitated because I thought they were Japanese goods (PS: later realized I was wrong—not Japanese, so safe to buy).

5. On the first day I wandered around the city center. It's similar to Chunxi Road in Chengdu, but seems a bit bigger. Also spotted a ZARA that we don't have in Chengdu. Went looking for a New Balance backpack, checked every store big and small, only found one New Balance shop and they didn't have backpacks. Maybe I just won't feel like buying one later.

6. At noon on the 2nd I went to Haigeng Park. As someone who's grown up in Sichuan, seeing that endless stretch of water and rolling waves was genuinely shocking. I decided that soon I'd see an even bigger, wider body of water. You know its name: the sea.

7. After Haigeng Park we went to Xishan Park. There's a cable car—40 yuan per person. A broke student and hiking enthusiast naturally wasn't going to take it.

8. I assumed Xishan would charge an entrance fee—the itinerary said so—but somehow we ended up taking a small trail up the mountain. This path is probably the one locals use for their daily hikes—very low-key.

9. Xishan Park sits at 2600m (according to the guidebook). Dianchi Lake is around 1890m (I measured it myself). We spent over three hours exploring. From Xishan you can look down over the entire Kunming city area. It reminded me of how from Yangmingshan in Taiwan you can also overlook Taipei. I don't know how many big cities Chengdu has, but Chengdu is round. Kunming isn't.

10. Took a back-view photo by the cliff at Xishan, the same angle my Baidu friend Yue Xiaobei uses. That angle is really good—far away and thrilling. After coming down from Xishan my phone died, so no GPS. No idea how far the road ahead was. Ended up walking aimlessly for over an hour. Then took the A1 bus back to the city. Fell asleep on the bus.

11. Only after getting back to the city did I look for a hotel. Earlier I'd only found a guesthouse. Didn't go in—sounded sketchy, and I was worried about running into the kind of situation Andy Lau had in A Moment of Romance. Decided to find a proper HOTEL. Eventually found Home Inn—170 yuan for the night.

12. This morning after waking up I went straight to the train station to buy tonight's ticket. Recently all standing tickets to Dali in the evening have sold out. We hesitated. I looked at Xishuangbanna—over 200 yuan by bus. Speechless. In the end we decided to buy tickets for tomorrow to Dali. Then Zhang Shaoxia suddenly told me to buy for Lijiang instead. I said the ancient city entrance fee alone is 80, and it's only 15 yuan from Dali to Lijiang. Still ended up buying for Dali.

13. In the afternoon we wandered around Southwest Forestry University. The neighboring Kunming University of Science and Technology looked too old; didn't feel like going in. Took the bus to Yuantong Road, visited Yunnan University and the adjacent Cuihu Park.

14. That evening found a "Zhiqing Guesthouse" near the train station—80 yuan. Definitely not the same class as Home Inn. Proof once again that you get what you pay for.

15. Internet time's almost up, so I'll stop here. Day 3 itinerary ends.

16. On the morning of the 4th we rushed to the train station for the train to Dali. Arrived in Dali at 2 PM, grabbed some food, then took the 8 bus to the ancient city.

17. The ancient city was packed with tourists—completely expected during National Day. You can rent a bike here and cycle around the old town. Yangren Street and whatever other street are the liveliest, with the most silver jewelry sellers, though I doubt the purity. 

18. Also selling tie-dye and the "Eighteen Oddities of Yunnan"—milk-made whatever. Didn't taste good. The people drinking in bars seemed to all be foreigners. Chinese people were all in roadside teahouses. I also saw those two foreign couples selling stuff here that I'd read about before—their Chinese is actually pretty good.

19. Hotels during Golden Week are expensive. Even the crappy ones are fully booked. Starting to miss the Home Inn in Kunming already.

20. Spent the night in the ancient city.

21. At 8 AM we headed to Chongsheng Temple and the Three Pagodas. Chongsheng Temple is the Tianlong Temple Jin Yong wrote about in Demi-Gods and Semi-Devils, where Duan Yu learned the Six Meridians Divine Sword. We used student IDs. I was worried the ticket lady would check the photos carefully, but luckily she didn't. Half price—62 yuan. Our itinerary only allowed two hours at Chongsheng Temple. We ended up staying two and a half.

22. Coming out of the temple we caught the bus back to the city. Then took a taxi to Erhai Park. Still that endless water, though I think it's more interesting than Dianchi. Even went and touched the water of Erhai—never got to touch Dianchi's.

23. At 11:30 we arrived at Dali train station and boarded the train to Guangtong.

posts.title: The Story at Two in the Morning
posts.excerpt: I can't remember exactly when this happened anymore. That night I was reading "Demi-Gods and Semi-Devils" on my phone. Back in high school I'd decided I had to read this novel, but I never got around ...
posts.content:
I can't remember exactly when this happened anymore.

That night I was reading "Demi-Gods and Semi-Devils" on my phone. Back in high school I'd decided I had to read this novel, but I never got around to it, and of course I hadn't watched the TV series either. I was pretty engrossed and lost track of time when suddenly there was urgent knocking from the next dorm room. Annoyed, I heard Ce-ge loudly calling for people in the room to get up and go outside. Weird, why wake people up in the middle of the night? Then he used the same method to knock on the other adjacent dorms, "bang," "bang," "bang"...

I figured he'd come to our room next. I checked the time and thought, damn, 1:30!

Sure enough, the "bang," "bang," "bang" started pounding urgently on our door.

"What's going on?" I asked.

"XX, XX, XX, you three get up quick, come out and answer some questions, hurry!" Ce-ge replied.

I could already hear kids from other rooms coming to our doorway, grumbling and cursing in the middle of the night. I said I'd wake them right away. While getting dressed, I tried to guess what had happened.

We clattered downstairs as a group to the dorm supervisor's place. Our counselor was sitting there along with a few other leaders. The dorm auntie had naturally had her sweet dreams interrupted and looked completely fed up. Turns out one of our classmates had posted something inappropriate on QQ that night, and unfortunately it caught the attention of the higher-ups. They called our principal to handle it. Since we'd all added him as a friend, we got dragged into it too. The principal was apparently among those people—I'd never met him and didn't know who he was.

Later we were questioned. They asked if I'd logged into QQ that night. I said yes. They asked if I'd seen his QQ status. I said no—I couldn't be bothered to check my classmates' QQ statuses. We see each other every day, no need to look at their statuses. Then they rambled on about a bunch of stuff. Finally they made me write a guarantee letter, sign my name, and go back to sleep.

When I got back and checked my phone, it was 2 o'clock. I had no more interest in reading the novel and just fell asleep in a daze.

This is a legendary story. The deeper meaning is obvious to everyone, so no need to comment further.

posts.title: The Buddha on Line 1 – This Guy Really Suits My Taste
posts.excerpt: I just spent some time finishing Li Haipeng's "The Buddha on Line 1." I'd bought this book a while ago but had been too busy with exams to read it. Now that all my tests are finally done, I pulled a s...
posts.content:
I just spent some time finishing Li Haipeng's "The Buddha on Line 1." I'd bought this book a while ago but had been too busy with exams to read it. Now that all my tests are finally done, I pulled a stack of books I'd bought off the shelf and piled them on my desk to tackle one by one, starting with the unfinished "The Buddha on Line 1."

I bought it because I'd seen lots of positive reviews on Douban. I always trust Douban, so I found a TXT version online and put it on my phone to read before falling asleep at night. Just the first few essays had already won me over with the author's wit and sharpness. Continuing with the TXT would've been disrespectful to Uncle Li, so I immediately placed an order on Dangdang. That's how it went.

When I got the book and tore off the bellyband, it turned out to be a thread-bound edition. The real cover was deep blue, and the spine was left untrimmed, like an ancient book. I wonder how Li Haipeng agreed to this unusual binding—maybe it hints that this book is destined to become a classic.

Since the title of this post is "This Guy Really Suits My Taste," let me say that all my earlier complaints about the strange binding and somewhat critical tone were complete nonsense. I'm here specifically to praise this book, which also fits the Chinese writing tradition of criticizing first before praising. Can I compare this book to "Fortress Besieged"? I have a habit when reading: any book that makes me laugh heartily and gives me something to think about, I compare to "Fortress Besieged." Of course, this book can't hold a candle to "Fortress Besieged," but using it to reflect on society still makes it a good work. This book is a collection of Li Haipeng's articles from publications like "First Finance Weekly," "Southern Weekly," and "Think Tank GQ." "The Buddha on Line 1" is just one piece in it. In my opinion, that article's content is fairly ordinary, but the title is catchy. Using it as the book title plays on people's general curiosity. After flipping through and finding some good articles, I bought the book—bookseller, Li Haipeng, and readers all happy.

If Li Haipeng's writing is sharp, it's not because he inserts phrases like "your mom" or "go to hell" here and there. The real sharpness comes from his independent thinking as a writer, his deep reflection and criticism of social phenomena, rather than the mindless praise found in People's Daily articles. Of course, anyone who can publish in the Southern newspaper group isn't some professional propagandist—that's exactly why I like the Southern media group that's far from Beijing.

There's one sentence in Li Haipeng's writing that struck me deeply. I've used it in previous articles: "The most terrifying thing in the world is when people lacking common sense, in an environment lacking common sense, are full of confidence in their own abilities, firmly believe in their own value system, and feel deeply proud of their own moral passion." I'd been struggling to find the right words to describe a certain state of mind—similar to some love I could never quite express. His words perfectly captured some of my views.

He also said: "Many years ago when I was on a BBS, I didn't get along with some people. I can't entirely blame them—I was also the type whose cat claws couldn't stay idle, always wanting to point out idiots. This kind of thing isn't just boring, it has no end—idiots are like streptococcus, endless and inexhaustible." Reading this made me laugh. My internet experience is somewhat similar to Li Haipeng's, but this powerful metaphor was the first time I'd heard it. Okay, first come first served—if any friends say they've seen this metaphor somewhere before, I refuse to acknowledge it.

Such witty passages are everywhere in this book. Excerpting them and posting to Weibo would be great. I really wanted to underline them with a pen, but I'm someone who loves books and is a bit lazy, so I didn't do it.

I've seen a saying that the best-selling books nowadays are those written by "angry youth." According to the fifty-cent army's view, this book is indeed written by an angry youth because Li Haipeng didn't practice what Han Han criticized: "So many people are already singing praises, one more won't make a difference." I'm not a fifty-cent poster, so I think this book isn't angry youth at all—it's just the thinking a normal person should have.

posts.title: 2010 Year-End Summary
posts.excerpt: It's Christmas, but it has nothing to do with me. The Christmas atmosphere around me is just something the merchants have carefully packaged. If they could make money off Ghost Festival in July, they ...
posts.content:
It's Christmas, but it has nothing to do with me. The Christmas atmosphere around me is just something the merchants have carefully packaged. If they could make money off Ghost Festival in July, they wouldn't hesitate to package that too. In a few days it'll be 2011. Looking back at the entire past year, my 2010 didn't bring many surprises.

This time last year, things weren't much different from now. I was busy dealing with exams, spending every day on the sunny rooftop reading while sunbathing, then muddling through the tests. I bought a train ticket and, together with a high school classmate who'd come here to study with me, we couldn't wait to head back to Chengdu. After arriving, despite being exhausted from the overnight train ride, I called my parents to say I'd arrived, dropped off my luggage, and went with him on the bus to Chunxi Road to buy clothes. First, because Chengdu is much colder than the south, and second, because he was heading back to his hometown. I still remember the salesgirl at Gap looking at us in disbelief as we tried on clothes wearing just a jacket and a T-shirt. We quickly explained we'd just returned from out of town. A few years ago, a middle-aged couple was shopping at my mom's store. Seeing how lightly they were dressed, I said, "Uncle and auntie, you're so tough!" Their explanation: they'd just come back from Hainan.

After buying clothes on Chunxi Road that day, my classmate went home. Finding work during winter break in Chengdu is easiest. My plan was to do some temp work at Carrefour. Since my mom works there, it would've been easy for her to get me a job. But Carrefour only needed people for moving stock and cashier positions. I didn't have much strength and wasn't quick with my hands, so I didn't go. I stayed home surfing the internet, watching American TV shows, occasionally hanging out with relatives and friends. A few days before Spring Festival, I went back to my hometown alone, catching a ride with a relative of a relative. He'd just gotten his driver's license and bought a car, so he didn't dare drive on the highway. My uncle helped drive, and I got to see my cousin. I was speechless when he showed up in a military ambulance to pick us up. The remaining national highway had to be driven by this new driver relative. Sitting in his car made me break out in a cold sweat from nervousness, though he was even more nervous than me. Now, from my perspective, even though I haven't gotten my license yet, I think he doesn't drive as well as I do. Riding in his car, swaying back and forth, swaying back and forth, after a long time I actually made it home alive!

My youngest uncle and aunt, who'd been living away for years, chose to come home for the New Year—that's also why I went back. More people means more lively, and the grandparents would be happy. Actually, there's hardly any New Year atmosphere in the countryside anymore. First, most young people in rural areas work or study elsewhere. Those working in Guangdong far away don't have enough money for train or plane tickets, nor the energy to endure China's characteristic Spring Festival travel rush. Better to stay at the factory and get double overtime pay. People living closer usually take the elderly out for the holiday. These reasons leave the countryside without much energy. Second, the lack of people means merchants don't bother decorating for the festive atmosphere. It was better when I was a kid—I would never have complained about lacking New Year spirit back then. A few firecrackers going "pop pop pop" and the atmosphere was complete. Their New Year tradition is playing mahjong and Dou Dizhu. I don't know how to play either, so I felt helpless. I thought about going to the county town to find classmates to hang out with, but it always came back to mahjong and Dou Dizhu. No choice. Even though I say there's no atmosphere in the countryside during New Year, I'll still go back this year. This time I must bring my dad's laptop and wireless card.

After the New Year, I stayed in Chengdu for a while. One time—seems like it was the day China Heavy Industry listed—I was watching the economics channel with my dad. The news was reporting on China Heavy Industry's listing, and we started talking about the stock market. My dad said China Heavy Industry would probably rise. As we talked, he said, "How about I give you ten thousand yuan to buy stocks?" I'd never had that much money in my hands before. Afraid of losing it all, I only took five thousand. Together with Da Tao, I went to Guosen Securities to open a shareholder account. The next day, when Jinan Securities hit the daily limit down, I bought all in. In the afternoon, Jinan Securities was inexplicably pulled up to limit up. That day I made over nine hundred in profit. The third day it rose a bit more, with peak profit over a thousand. In the end I only made eight hundred. This was my first stock trading experience—completely accidental. Good thing I didn't buy the China Heavy Industry stock my dad mentioned, otherwise I would've lost badly. With the mindset that losing a small amount wouldn't matter, in nearly a year of stock trading, I haven't lost money, but I haven't made much either.

Soon I returned to school for the second semester of sophomore year, occasionally playing with stocks.

The second semester of sophomore year was as calm as still water, listening to teachers talk nonsense and classmates brag. Later I got obsessed with building websites. Although I'd registered my first .CN domain and second domain from GoDaddy back in '09, I didn't actually start building sites until April or May. I started with HTML, learned CSS, then after understanding a bit switched to the easiest-to-learn PHP. From there I moved on to CMS programs. Unfortunately, I still haven't accomplished anything. This blog of mine has lasted the longest and is the only program I somewhat understand. Saying "persisted" isn't quite right, because I've already decided to keep writing this blog indefinitely—whether I persist or not doesn't matter. Now I have four domains and can't bear to abandon any of them. This blog has been renewed until May 2012, and I'll renew the others too. I have a forum made with Discuz, domain PZHU.NET. With such a good domain and Baidu homepage ranking, having no energy to promote it is also a source of frustration.

After going through the exams I hated most, I took the train—no hotpot, no little tunes—and entered vacation overnight. My Summer Day.

My summer vacation experiences: went to Jiaotong University twice, had Da Tao take me around his Wangjiang campus at Sichuan University, went hiking Qingcheng Mountain with my cousin and wrote "Qingcheng Mountain Travel Notes," helped another younger brother and a younger sister buy computers, cooked dumplings and noodles with my brother at noon, helped my dad with budgets every night, watched "The Big Bang Theory," went to bed at 1 AM. Didn't go to Chunxi Road to shop, didn't visit any malls. The place I went to most was taking the 45 bus to Moziqiao Computer City. Near the end of summer break, using some money I'd earned plus some from my dad, I bought a phone—HTC HD MINI. Didn't expect they'd release a G9 with the same appearance later, otherwise I would've waited. The afternoon I went back to school, the ink cartridges I'd ordered from JD for my family's printer finally arrived and my mom picked them up. She had a lot going on that day but had to stay home waiting for the JD delivery, constantly complaining that I insisted on buying things in such a troublesome way. But she didn't seem to realize it was dozens of yuan cheaper than buying at Moziqiao Computer City.

Then came the first semester of junior year. Looking at the schedule, it seemed like I had quite a bit of free time, but that wasn't the case. There were fewer regular classes, but after-class assignments were more than any previous semester. Much of the textbook content and practical work needed to be completed after class—hours and hours of homework time, troublesome and exhausting. One memorable thing this semester was my trip to Dali—see "National Day Travel Notes" for details.

In the second semester of sophomore year, I'd already had the idea of buying one book per month. I actually started doing it at the beginning of junior year and will keep it up, hoping to have many, many books in the future. At the start of junior year, I wrote down several work goals for the semester on my blog. The semester still hasn't ended, but I've already failed at some of them.

That's about all that happened. My 2010 year-end summary ends here too.

posts.title: January 1, 2011 – A Beginning and an End
posts.excerpt: When I went to buy a computer for my counselor in the morning, I didn't help much. By the time it was sorted out at noon, he invited me to have a meal together. I felt a bit embarrassed—after all, "no...
posts.content:
When I went to buy a computer for my counselor in the morning, I didn't help much. By the time it was sorted out at noon, he invited me to have a meal together. I felt a bit embarrassed—after all, "no reward without merit," I should be the one treating him, hoping he'd look after me more in the future.

Around seven, a few people went to the train station to buy tickets. I asked them to get one for me too, but later they called back saying they couldn't get any. When I ran into them returning at the school gate, they gave me back my student ID and asked where I was going. "Chengdu," I answered. "Chengdu tickets on the 10th are easy to get." Damn, I specifically asked you guys to buy Chengdu tickets. Guess I'll have to go buy it myself. I put my student ID in my pocket and went to find my counselor at the Third Middle School gate. After waiting a while, they arrived and we went to the computer city.

While eating with my counselor, I realized we didn't have much in common to talk about. School matters weren't worth discussing, and our lives had little overlap. We were basically just making conversation while eating, which felt quite awkward. In real life, no one wears labels on their face. When we interact, we can only observe and guess someone's interests and hobbies. But in online life, I've already labeled myself—my likes and dislikes are clearly stated in the sidebar. If you like it, get close; if not, leave. So real-life communication is the hardest.

Before I woke up today, I had a dream. In a place full of broken coffins, somewhat like a mass grave, a soldier sitting in a tank was shooting a kangaroo with a gun. I rescued the kangaroo from their hands, let it jump onto my arm, then took it to the southernmost part of China, with the sea strait outside. I was about to take a boat to send it back to Australia when suddenly the kangaroo spoke: "I'm from Mianzhu, I'm not going to Australia." I thought, why does this kangaroo want to go back to the zoo? Didn't it escape? The dream ended there, woken up by the classmates who'd gone to buy train tickets earlier. Later when I thought about it, this dream had quite a meaning, so I specifically recorded it here. As for Mianzhu in the dream—that's the hometown of Jiannanchun liquor—I still can't figure out why that place appeared.

Logically, my blog should show "2010——2011" below today, but it still hasn't appeared. This is also why I randomly wrote this post—to test if it would appear after publishing this article, though it goes against my "better nothing than something mediocre" principle.

posts.title: About Dreams
posts.excerpt: There was a time when my dreams were at their most intense—every night was like performing in a large-scale situational drama, constantly switching scenes and actors. When I woke up in the morning, my...
posts.content:
There was a time when my dreams were at their most intense—every night was like performing in a large-scale situational drama, constantly switching scenes and actors. When I woke up in the morning, my head felt heavy and groggy. Later, after entering high school and then university, my dreams became fewer and fewer, and my ability to remember them grew worse. If it weren't for some accidental chance the next day hearing or seeing something that suddenly triggered my memory, I could hardly remember having dreamed at all. I don't know the general reason for this situation. My personal guess is that when I was young I loved fantasizing. Lacking ways to learn about various things, I was always imagining distant worlds and strange phenomena. But no matter how much I thought, there was no one around who could answer my questions, and I didn't dare ask teachers—they'd probably say I was daydreaming all day instead of focusing on proper studies, otherwise my grades wouldn't be such a mess. So when I slept at night, my brain kept pondering problems I hadn't figured out during the day, which created these dreams.

Through this explanation, I feel a sense of regret. How desperately I thirsted for knowledge as a child, how much I loved thinking about problems—yet it was crushed by harsh reality. I have a hundred reasons to envy today's children. For example, my younger brother, at an age when he's as curious about the world as I once was, now has sufficient tools to access the content he needs. The slight downside is that although there are many tools—like computers—he doesn't use them to their full potential. He completely treats the computer as a game console. One of my dorm roommates is the same. After several years of university, he can't even remember the school's domain name. Once when I needed to use his computer, I found his desktop had only one game and a few rarely used items—no browser. He had to go through "Start—Programs—Internet Explorer" to open a webpage. Completely speechless.

If I'd had these things before, perhaps I wouldn't have suffered from frequent dreams for so long. But those uncomfortable days have finally passed, and I won't return to that old time.

Early this morning, just before dawn, I had a long-awaited dream. I woke up quickly afterward, so I still remember the content. I think this dream is quite interesting. It seemed to be in a mass grave, with broken coffins and small burial mounds everywhere, and some strange expressionless, bloodless people—maybe ghosts. Suddenly a tank rolled up, with a soldier standing at the open top holding a machine gun. After driving through, it reached a place that looked like a forest. This soldier suddenly raised his gun and shot a large bird on a tree, then they went to chase a kangaroo that had jumped out from somewhere. I let this kangaroo jump onto my arm and told the soldier not to kill it. Then I took the kangaroo to the southernmost part of China, with the sea strait outside. I was about to send this kangaroo back to Australia when suddenly it spoke, saying it wouldn't go to Australia—it wanted to return to its original place. I guessed its original place might be a zoo. Just as I was at a loss, afraid it would go back and continue being harmed, I was suddenly woken up by sounds from outside. This dream tells the story of me helping someone escape a predicament, only for them to be ungrateful, thinking they were safe instead. Ridiculous.

After having this dream, I don't know when I'll dream again or remember a dream.

posts.title: The Moon and Sixpence – The World and Ideals
posts.excerpt: I finished this book on the train. I like calling it "The Moon and Sixpence." As I mentioned in the previous article, I was traveling back alone. On Douban, I Doumail'd my friend Yiyi—they were in car...
posts.content:
I finished this book on the train. I like calling it "The Moon and Sixpence." As I mentioned in the previous article, I was traveling back alone. On Douban, I Doumail'd my friend Yiyi—they were in car 8, I was in car 6. I said if I got bored I'd come find them, but by the time the train reached Chengdu, I still hadn't gone over. With just a few minutes left before arriving at the North Railway Station, I was busy finishing the last few paragraphs of "The Moon and Sixpence." I was sitting in a very comfortable window seat, facing the direction the train was heading. Before night fell, I could watch the scenery rushing backward outside the window. When tired, I could rest my head on the train's window frame. I was well aware of the strange looks from the several grade '09 classmates around me who'd been playing Dou Dizhu and poker all night, arguing over a few cents. When the three of them first got on the train, they wanted me to switch seats with them—I don't like watching scenery going backward, so I didn't switch. They then switched with the person next to me. Later events proved my choice was right. At Xichang Station, several Yi ethnic compatriots heading to Chengdu for work got on and directly crowded into that spot without leaving. One guy had to give up his seat to these Yi people and stood in the aisle all night. So I hid in a small corner and finished reading this book. Reading this type of novel on a train might not be appropriate—something like "Zhiyin" or "Story Club" would be more mainstream.

The first one hundred and forty-some pages I read in the dorm. Strickland was a stockbroker in London. Even now, being a stockbroker is still a decent profession—the stock investors' money all goes to the securities companies. Obviously Strickland's living conditions at the time could be called excellent. But one day, after returning to London from the countryside alone, he suddenly ran away, abandoning his wife and children. He went to Paris alone to start painting. He had no foundation whatsoever. The novel's first-person narrator "I" went to Paris on behalf of his wife to beg him to return, but this heartless, ungrateful guy Strickland remained unmoved. He believed he was now pursuing his ideal, and women were nothing more than stumbling blocks in his pursuit of that ideal, so he had to leave them. Strickland drifted around Paris for a while. There he broke up a family that had been helping him. Stroeve helped the sick Strickland, but later Stroeve's wife Blanche fell in love with Strickland and decided to follow him. Unfortunately, Strickland was a cruel guy. After satisfying himself, he made Blanche leave him. Blanche then drank oxalic acid and committed suicide. He later went to Marseille, then soon to an island in the South Pacific, where he devoted himself to painting and married a local woman. He later died of leprosy. During his lifetime, Strickland was unappreciated. After his death, his paintings gained recognition before World War I and became expensive, like Van Gogh.

Strickland was a cold-blooded animal from beginning to end. He didn't develop feelings for anyone. He thought women only talked about love all day, and love wasn't a person's entire life—people also needed to eat, live, and realize their value. Women were obstacles to him achieving his ideal, so he abandoned his wife. Later, when Blanche followed him, he only treated her as his model and a tool to occasionally relieve his desires. When he felt his tool had reached the expected value, to prevent Blanche from controlling him, he made her leave. Blanche felt her goal of firmly controlling Strickland had failed and chose suicide. Maugham has a passage in the book describing his view of women: "Because women can do nothing but talk about love, they attach great importance to love, to the point of absurdity. They also try to convince us that a man's entire life is love, when in fact love is only an insignificant part of life. I only understand lust, which is normal and healthy. Love is a disease. Women are tools for my pleasure. I find their demands that I treat them as career assistants or life companions extremely annoying." There's also a description in the book about Blanche's suicide. It should be the first-person narrator "I" comforting the anxious Stroeve, saying that when a woman fails to achieve her goal and has no other choice, she'll threaten with suicide, but the method she prepares won't actually kill her—it only needs to make the man change his mind. But in the end, Stroeve's wife Blanche still died.

There's another tragic character in the novel—Stroeve. He's a chubby person whose words and actions always make people laugh, even when he's telling others about his misfortunes. When we share happiness with others, even though they don't receive any benefit from you, they'll think you're a good person and respect you. But if someone frequently shares their troubles with others, the first one or two times listeners might shed a few sympathetic tears. After too many times, what you get is often not sympathy but ridicule—people might even curse you as an idiot to your face or behind your back. Stroeve was exactly this kind of person. He loved life and loved helping others, but he often told people about every trouble he encountered. Combined with his inherently comical appearance, everyone looked down on him. He helped Strickland, but Strickland disliked him intensely and even refused his help. Later, when Blanche left him, he cried and knelt begging Blanche for mercy, and waited for her on her route every day, only to be slapped by Blanche—a typical bitter drama role. "This person has no brain and has lost his dignity as a husband." I believe begging a woman who doesn't love you is useless. If a woman decides from the beginning that she no longer loves you, she'll never love you again. "A woman can be more cruel to a man who still loves her but whom she no longer loves than to anyone else." After Blanche died, Stroeve returned to his hometown in Holland. I also believe that when someone fails in their ventures outside and has nowhere to go, their hometown is always their best refuge, forever welcoming the return of its wanderers.

This is my first time reading Maugham's work. Of course, I rarely read more than two works by the same writer. As far as this book goes, I feel Maugham likes to explore the depths of characters' hearts. Human values are divided into several levels. The lowest level is satisfying one's survival, the highest is realizing one's value. Strickland is an anomaly—he skipped several important intermediate steps of gaining others' recognition and respect, going straight to just feeding himself (even then he couldn't eat his fill), disregarding everyone's opinions, pursuing his highest ideal. When a person no longer cares about their own body, would they still consider the world's opinion of them? I can't say whether I agree or disagree with Strickland. After all, he gained the world's recognition after death. But for ordinary people, if everyone acted like him, I think this world would be finished. If there were someone like Strickland around me—someone who disregards others' opinions, stands out uniquely, and purely pursues realizing themselves or changing all of humanity—I'd think he was crazy. The funny thing is, such a person would completely ignore my opinion too—after all, he's also a Strickland. My opinion doesn't count for anything. I also ask myself: what is my ideal? I can't answer. Probably I only have dreams, no ideals. Or as a friend said, my ideal is to realize my dreams—but does that count as an ideal?

**Appendix:**

A few brilliant passages casually excerpted:

"Love requires a person to expend enormous energy. It requires a person to leave their own life and specifically become a lover."

"A woman can forgive a man for hurting her," he said, "but she can never forgive him for the sacrifices he makes for her."

"Some say that disaster and misfortune can ennoble human nature—this statement is not correct. What sometimes inspires noble actions is actually happiness and success. In most cases, disaster and misfortune only make people narrow-minded and more vengeful."

"The difference between men and women when it comes to falling in love is: women can talk about love day and night, while men can only do this kind of thing occasionally."

"I very much doubt whether Abraham really wasted himself. Doing what one most wants to do, living in an environment one loves, being indifferent and tranquil, detached from worldly strife—is this really wasting oneself? On the contrary, becoming a famous surgeon with an annual salary of ten thousand pounds and marrying a beautiful wife—is that success? I think it all depends on how a person views the meaning of life, what obligations they believe they owe to society, and what demands they make of themselves."

posts.title: When We Talk About Baidu, What Do We Say?
posts.excerpt: In '07 I got my first QQ, but instead of using Qzone, I wrote on Baidu Space. Baidu Space was my first blog. Back then Baidu Space was still relatively pure—it didn't ask you to update your avatar eve...
posts.content:
In '07 I got my first QQ, but instead of using Qzone, I wrote on Baidu Space. Baidu Space was my first blog. Back then Baidu Space was still relatively pure—it didn't ask you to update your avatar every time you logged in, and few people sent spam through Baidu Space. It was easy to find people with similar interests or alumni there. I used this space until '09, made some friends, and had one annoying harassment incident. Because I didn't pay much attention to my personal information, I put my private QQ on there and a girl added me. From then on began my week-long nightmare. In the end I couldn't take it anymore—block! Blocking is the move I hate doing the most. It's too cruel. After that, I applied for another QQ—the one currently shown on the About page—specifically for communicating with net friends, strangers, and alumni I'd never met. No pressure at all.

After starting my blog, considering that the same article couldn't be posted on Baidu Space (otherwise Baidu would think I was plagiarizing from Baidu Space), I basically gave up updating Baidu Space. But because of a few popular articles, my space's visit count has hardly decreased. Probably tomorrow my Baidu Space visit count will exceed 50,000. Compared to Baidu celebrity Yue Xiaobei, it's nothing, but this visit count is still pretty good. It'd be nice if I could add ads to Baidu Space—maybe earn a few yuan, and Baidu Space's popularity would increase a lot. At least those bloggers who think they can make money would take root there. Yue Xiaobei closed her space today. She said she couldn't stand Baidu Space's awkward revision done out of envy for other spaces, and the constant avatar update prompts every time she logged into the space backend. I can't stand that either—why should I have to put my real information on Baidu facing netizens nationwide? Who can guarantee that everyone online is normal! But Yue Xiaobei's space had one or two million visits. No matter how you look at it, that's money. Closing it like this seems a bit wasteful. I won't close my space. I'll continue maintaining its popularity because I'm eyeing Baidu Search's friendly attitude toward my space. Making good use of it is also a promotion channel.

Baidu is a bit stupid. In '09 they completely shut down university tiebas. In '10 they relaunched a "completely revised" university tieba: "The new university tieba is divided by class units. Creating and joining classes uses real-name registration. While ensuring personal privacy and security, it provides you and your classmates with a more intimate and harmonious communication space." This irresponsible behavior literally confines everyone to their own class, and you need real names to create a class. The previously somewhat free big family is completely gone. Even an idiot knows this won't bring any benefit to Baidu, yet Baidu still did it.

posts.title: Some Things About the New Year
posts.excerpt: When the bus passed a mountain pass and a strange small town appeared on the right, I suddenly realized I had once again left my hometown, and couldn't help feeling melancholy. I've been living very r...
posts.content:
When the bus passed a mountain pass and a strange small town appeared on the right, I suddenly realized I had once again left my hometown, and couldn't help feeling melancholy. I've been living very relaxed these days. Returning from the noisy, bustling city to the quiet countryside felt like escaping from one world to another. All my troubles were thrown to the back of my mind. I no longer logged into QQ all day, no longer refreshed Weibo repeatedly, and stopped visiting blogs everywhere. I stopped caring about tech news, economic news, Egyptian politics, and such. I only cared about how many ducks the family was raising, how many acres of land were planted, how the neighbors were doing... There were no TV shows I liked. I went to bed around nine at night. In the pitch-black room, sleeping alone on a big bed, accompanied by music, I drifted off to sleep. Woke up at ten the next day. Well, more than half the day was spent sleeping—truly luxurious, truly comfortable. Living day by day in fragments, I seemed to forget the passage of time. Ten-plus days felt like the blink of an eye and passed in an instant.

The morning I came back, my dad asked me to bring my laptop. Originally that was also my plan, but when I tried the wireless card, it seemed broken. So bringing the computer would be pointless. I only casually brought some clothes and a few books—"The Devotion of Suspect X" and "Walden." "The Devotion of Suspect X," like "White Night," is a novel that doesn't withstand rereading. I read while listening to music in the courtyard and finished it in two or three days. Then I read "Walden"—progress was much slower. From the day I returned home until now, I've been listening to moumoon's "Sunshine Girl" on repeat. I like this singer's cute and playful voice. I've replayed it more times than I've listened to the national anthem from childhood to now. Because of this, my phone needs charging three times a day.

The family has raised a new puppy again. The previous dog ran away during summer while dragging its iron chain. It was hot at the time, and my grandpa, afraid the dog would suffer, splashed some water on it. Surprisingly, it scared the dog so much that it broke free from the chain and ran away. It wandered around the village at first, then was never seen again. The new puppy isn't familiar with me. As soon as it sees me, it hides far away. Ha, what a timid dog. I threw bones to it from far to near. It carefully approached with its tail between its legs, grabbed the bone, then ran away with its tail between its legs. After two days of this, the dog no longer feared me. I don't have clear likes or dislikes regarding cats and dogs. Some say dog people crave being loved by others, while cat people crave loving others—I think there's some truth to that.

We have a C-band satellite TV receiver at home. I like tinkering with these things, exploring unknown worlds. Our area can receive satellites from 78°E to 128°E. I've watched every free satellite program within this range that has receivable conditions. I especially like Phoenix TV on AsiaSat 3 and French fashion channels. I'd really like to install a Ku-band LNB, but I don't live here permanently and haven't had the chance to do it. If only I had my own house—I'd install several big dishes on the roof and crack encrypted programs with a networked setup, haha. I happened to see Chenghua Cable TV saying they want to legally install satellite receiving equipment and watch legal TV programs. I was curious what "legal TV programs" means—does Phoenix count as legal?

Grandpa is an old Party member. At lunch on New Year's Eve, he reminisced about the past and expressed gratitude for the present, and we had a disagreement about who brought us our happy life. He thought it was Mao Zedong, I thought it was Deng Xiaoping. I argued a couple of sentences then stopped debating. It's common for people to become more stubborn as they age—many views won't change. Let's go with Mao Zedong then. Actually, according to my five-cent classmate, we should also thank that bowl of egg fried rice. Later I heard family history and had never really understood this. I also don't know the ancestral relationships with nearby neighbors. Now I can roughly figure it out. If they hadn't told me these things, I would never have asked. I'm not very interested in these old relationships.

New Year's Day was quite boring. My grandma went up the mountain to ring the chimes for people worshiping Buddha. I also went up the mountain once. My cousin suddenly called saying she was going to town. My cousin and I immediately decided to go to town. We went to the temple in town. Although I didn't feel the lively atmosphere of the morning, the garbage on the ground indicated the previous scene. I have no religious faith—in fact, I have great aversion to one particular religion. Forgive me for not being able to directly name it online. No matter what Buddhist temple I visit, I only treat it as artificial scenery. People burning incense and worshiping Buddha I treat as temporary actors. In China, people with faith all have utilitarian motives. They hope the Buddha will protect their safety and bring them wealth. If the compassionate Buddha could save all sentient beings, wouldn't our great motherland have so many human tragedies? According to my understanding, burning incense, worshiping Buddha, and praying for blessings will eventually become just a ritual, like pasting Spring Festival couplets during New Year or eating tangyuan during Lantern Festival.

The New Year custom is to receive New Year's money. On my maternal grandmother's side, the uncles haven't followed this custom for many years, so I've gradually gotten used to not receiving New Year's money during the holiday. One of my aunts has a family that's not very well-off economically, but she insisted on giving me money. Last year when she gave me money, I quietly stuffed it back into her bag. We only called to tell her after we'd walked far away. This time when I left her house, she didn't give me New Year's money. I thought that was fine. After walking quite far, I subconsciously reached into my pocket and discovered she'd also quietly stuffed the money into my bag. I felt both touched and uncomfortable. Earning money isn't easy nowadays, let alone with prices skyrocketing. Once while walking alone on the road, I pondered whether returning to the gold standard economically would prevent economic crises. I made many assumptions—it seemed this could solve the problem.

My aunt also asked if I had a girlfriend. This winter break, I heard similar questions many times. My mom even asked me twice, almost making me angry. No means no!!!!!!! Why does it have to be so direct?

Went to my maternal grandmother's house. I feel my maternal grandparents have really aged. The food they cook no longer tastes as good as before, though I still ate with great relish.

The sixth day of the lunar new year was the high school classmates' reunion. I hadn't attended the previous few reunions. Now in my third year of university, it will be hard to have opportunities to gather in the future, so I must cherish this. Although we hadn't seen each other for a long time, that warm feeling still moved me. When we went to the teahouse to drink tea, I noticed many people had learned to play mahjong. I could only sit on the side chatting with classmates who also didn't know how to play cards. I like face-to-face conversation—this way we can understand each other better. Being able to see others' reactions during conversation is the best form of communication. It's very convenient for gauging whether what you're saying is reasonable and planning what to say next. Guessing the other person's feelings and responding appropriately during online chat is really mentally exhausting. In the future, let's go drink tea together. Coffee works too.

On the bus, I thought: this New Year is really over.

posts.title: Juliet Married Romeo Who Had a House
posts.excerpt: When I'm with Ce-ge, he always loves talking to me about major and future career matters. I don't know if he does this often with others too. He really likes considering life prospects, and exchanging...
posts.content:
When I'm with Ce-ge, he always loves talking to me about major and future career matters. I don't know if he does this often with others too. He really likes considering life prospects, and exchanging opinions with everyone has probably become his habit. I've had two recent opportunities to eat at the same table with him. Both times we talked about houses and marriage.

"In this current situation, can you get a wife without a house?"

"Don't even think about it. Today's girls are very realistic!" I answered loudly, same as last time.

"What about houses in rural areas?"

"Rural areas?! Who would want to marry into the countryside now? I don't even want to stay in the countryside myself, let alone ask someone else to come suffer with me. Before girls get married, they always ask a few questions: Do you have a house? Do you have a car? How many zeros in the bankbook? Even if the girl doesn't ask, her parents will definitely ask."

My judgment is based on some real situations I know about. These facts have somewhat influenced my views on marriage and put constant pressure on me to earn money. I have a distant cousin, over twenty years old, quite pretty. She had several relationships with marriage as the goal. Almost every time she was about to cross the marriage threshold, she was defeated by harsh reality. Her parents' condition for her marriage was that the guy must have a house, or be able to buy one immediately. I knew one young guy—I'd met him a few times. He worked at a landscaping company with a monthly salary of four or five thousand. He was very enthusiastic and proactive. I thought his conditions were decent. I thought their loving relationship would eventually lead them to the wedding hall. It wasn't until last New Year that I found out they'd broken up again. Because my aunt and uncle's only condition was that he must have a house—otherwise, don't even talk about anything. He couldn't meet it. His parents in the countryside couldn't even come up with the down payment money. Although his salary was slightly higher, with Chengdu's housing prices, he could only buy one square meter every two months. Can a girl in her twenties wait for you to save up the down payment? Who can guarantee she'll still love you then? In the end, this young guy just left. I wonder if his mood at that time was helplessness or anger? Resentment that his father couldn't step up, cursing his own incompetence, or complaining about skyrocketing housing prices? My cousin now has a new boyfriend who just graduated from university. I don't know how strong his personal abilities are, but his current conditions can meet all her family's requirements—he has one or two houses left to him by his parents. Now my cousin can get married, right?

Their marriage doesn't affect me much, but these experiences I've learned about have deeply touched me. I worry that one day when I'm about to bring the woman I deeply love into the church to receive the priest's blessing, her parents suddenly ask if I have a house—what would I answer? Maybe I'd say:

"Auntie... oh no... Mom, would a rented house work?"

"No! Don't get married for now. Young man, go buy a house!"

I might go find my dad: "Dad, buy me a house? I'm getting married."

My dad would say: "Son, your dad isn't surnamed Li!"

Then it becomes Romeo and Juliet... or maybe just me as Romeo alone, with Juliet running off to find Romeo who has a house.

Tying marriage to a property deed—I wonder if this is what China has always called "characteristics." Sometimes I think, if only the woman I marry were one of Goriot's two daughters, there'd also be a large dowry coming. Fantasy can't overcome reality. I fucking live in a heavenly dynasty where prices are skyrocketing! For example, when eating, I bought a pancake. The cafeteria staff pressed 1.2 on the card machine. I swiped, then he said it was wrong and pressed 0.3 yuan again on the machine. FK, you haven't even mastered pressing 1.2 yet? Now you're practicing 1.5 again! Can't you go slower? A small pancake, I can afford it. But most people can't afford housing prices that rise every day. In this era of nationwide property speculation, many companies doing real business have switched to speculating in real estate. They're not focused on their own enterprise development. Employee benefits and wages haven't improved. Housing prices are soaring into the sky. When employees' children want to get married and don't want their kids to continue suffering like them, they're all willing to find a son-in-law with a house and car.

posts.title: Reading List
posts.excerpt: Up to now, I've only read these books—maybe missing one or two. I'll just make a brief record. *Wolf Totem* — One of my favorite Chinese novels, highly recommended. *On the Road* — I love this group o...
posts.content:
Up to now, I've only read these books—maybe missing one or two. I'll just make a brief record.

*Wolf Totem* — One of my favorite Chinese novels, highly recommended.

*On the Road* — I love this group of confused young people who seem like madmen, highly recommended.

*Moment in Peking* — Yao Mochou is such an interesting woman, worth reading.

*The Golden Age of the Jin Family* — I like women like Leng Qingqiu, with a touch of sympathy, worth reading.

*Fate in Tears and Laughter* — I've almost forgotten it, average.

*Hawthorn Tree Forever* — Read it long before the movie, then watched the film too; equally flavorful, worth reading.

*Red Poppies* — The description of the fool feeling bubbles coming out of his bones is the most interesting, worth reading.

*In Search of Lost Time* — Didn't finish, too long, and couldn't get through it.

*Walden* — Rereading it, the kind you flip through when you have nothing to do, a keeper.

*Dream of the Red Chamber* — The second read was a bit more interesting than the first, but still didn't finish the second time, recommended.

*Romance of the Three Kingdoms* — Can't say I support any side, like Cao Cao's dominance and Liu Bei's kindness, recommended.

*The Catcher in the Rye* — Holden influenced me to some extent, recommended.

*Grass-like Youth* — Maybe read it again after college; regrettably I read it too early, worth reading.

*Return to Provence* — Didn't understand much at the time, just a record of Provence, worth reading.

*Ordinary World* — XX and XX's love made me sentimental; life in this world is not easy, highly recommended.

*Romeo and Juliet* — Not many people like reading plays, worth reading.

*Hamlet* — Same as above, average.

*Romance of the Western Chamber* — Just because it was in the textbook, I read the whole thing, average.

*Peony Pavilion* — You know the type, average.

*My Name Is Red* — Though it's a Nobel Prize winner, I'm unfamiliar with Middle Eastern culture, quite perplexing, average.

*Fortress Besieged* — One of my favorite Chinese novels, highly recommended.

*Border Town* — Pure love in western Hunan, average.

*The Old Man and the Sea* — The old man conquered the fish, though it was eaten clean by the time he reached shore; dreams don't die, highly recommended.

*The Adventures of Tom Sawyer* — Recommend it to the kids of people who visit my blog; I was young once, average.

*Gulliver's Travels* — Same as above, worth reading.

*Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Sea* — Same as above, worth reading.

*The Adventures of Robinson Crusoe* — Same as above, worth reading.

*Dong Qiao's Essays* — Forgive me, I didn't understand much back then, average.

*Historical Sky* — Actually, I don't like modern historical novels, really don't, average.

*Norwegian Wood* — Thinking back now, this book feels nostalgic: Watanabe, Naoko, Midori Kobayashi, recommended.

*Lin Qingxuan's Essays* — Love the wind chimes one the most; seems like Taiwanese people always bring Buddhism into things, worth reading.

*Zhou Guoping's Essays* — Lightly touched, no comment.

*The Unbearable Lightness of Being* — If I have time to read it a second time, maybe I'll fully understand it, worth reading.

*The Reader* — Forgot the main plot, only remember reading and making love, worth reading.

*Small World* — A bit like *Fortress Besieged*, but not as witty; reminds me of some beast, worth reading.

*The Horse Whisperer* — Almost forgot the main plot, but I remember it was quite interesting, worth reading.

*The Call* — After reading this, I had the urge to read McCullough's *The Thorn Birds*, but it's too thick, recommended.

*That Summer of Love* — Same kind of story about beast scholars, average.

*The Last Letter from Your Lover* — Some loves you'll never get, but never forget in your heart, recommended.

*Mansion Gate* — Another unfinished novel, no comment.

*Letter from an Unknown Woman* — I love you, you never knew, unless I died, highly recommended.

*1984* — No explanation needed, highly recommended.

*The Little Prince* — Forgive me, I was too childish when reading this, no comment.

*Père Goriot* — These days, who still reads classics? Quoted in a previous post, worth reading.

*To Live* — Unfinished, no comment.

*The Great Gatsby* — No explanation, recommended.

*The Bridges of Madison County* — Too sci-fi, worth reading.

*Tagore's Poetry Collection* — Every piece feels like a classic, recommended.

*Eugénie Grandet* — Forgot, no comment.

*We All Lost on Graduation Day* — Many people lost love that day, worth reading.

*Sadness Flows Upstream* — The only book by the Fourth Miss I've read; regrettably, in high school I didn't have such cool girlfriends, worth reading.

*Charlotte's Web* — That spider's name is Charlotte, recommended.

*Currency Wars* — Mysterious; I somewhat agree with the gold standard, think it could solve economic problems, worth reading.

*Message in a Bottle* — Like *The Last Letter from Your Lover*, a Nicholas Sparks bestseller romance novel; didn't have time to finish, no comment.

*The Kite Runner* — For you, a thousand times over, recommended.

*The Awakening of Design* — Professional book, don't read if you don't like it.

*Xi Murong's Poetry Collection* — Really like her poems, so beautiful, highly recommended.

*Deserted Village Apartment* — Actually not scary, worth reading.

*Half a Lifetime's Romance* — I want you to know there's someone in this world who will wait for you forever, highly recommended.

*Solo* — Out of print, just for collection; not many people regret it, the publisher wisely retreated, average.
