*Demi-Gods and Semi-Devils* — If you've seen the TV series, no need to read the novel, recommended.

*The Return of the Condor Heroes* — Same as above, recommended.

*The Heaven Sword and Dragon Saber* — Same as above, average.

*The Legend of the Condor Heroes* — Same as above, recommended.

*White Night* — I just want to walk with you under the sun holding hands; the first few episodes of the Japanese drama of the same name are pretty good, highly recommended.

*The Devotion of Suspect X* — Two books by Higashino, but I prefer *White Night*, recommended.

*Buddha on Line 1* — The true thoughts of a qualified literati, recommended.

*Lang Xianping Says: Why Are Our Days So Hard?* — One of the top ten garbage books of 2010, garbage.

*1Q84* BOOK 1 — Book 3 was spoiled, looking back book 1 feels interesting, planning to reread, no comment.

posts.title: Indifference
posts.excerpt: There are two reasons for being indifferent to people: one is unavoidable; the other is complete lack of interest. Just as I picked up my meal card and stood up to buy food, my phone in my pocket vibr...
posts.content:
There are two reasons for being indifferent to people: one is unavoidable; the other is complete lack of interest.

Just as I picked up my meal card and stood up to buy food, my phone in my pocket vibrated. I pulled it out and saw a local unknown number—probably that auntie dialing the wrong number again. Because numbers here are very similar and there are often scam calls, my phone frequently gets calls from strangers. The sensible ones hang up quickly. I counted silently: five, four, three, two, one. They didn't hang up, so I answered.

"XX, it's EH. My computer can't connect to the internet for some reason? How do I fix it?"

Damn it, it's you again! I thought to myself, while replying: "There are many reasons a computer can't connect to the internet, and it's hard for me to fix; just like audio problems, these are the two most troublesome issues. I usually just reinstall the system." I wasn't lying; those really are the two problems I find hardest to solve.

"Do you have time this afternoon? Come over and help me take a look, or reinstall the system for me..."

Hearing he wanted help reinstalling the system again, I got a bit anxious. Before he finished, I quickly replied: "I'm not free today, I have things to do all day." I lied—actually, my weekend had started Friday afternoon, and I was only doing homework tomorrow; the afternoon was quite free. This urgent reply plus my tone at the time already clearly implied I wasn't planning to help him. Afraid he still wouldn't get it and would ask me to come tomorrow, I started thinking of using the excuse of going to driving school for the weekend to decline.

Fortunately, he either understood my meaning or couldn't stand being offline all afternoon—in any case, he didn't ask me to come tomorrow. I felt much relieved.

"Do you have an XP system disk? I'll come get it," he continued asking. Normally he should have hung up right away.

"Uh... no, I don't use that system anymore," I didn't dare mention I only had Win7, lest it cause more trouble.

"Alright then, bye."

"Bye."

Actually, this person and I shouldn't have crossed paths. Even now I can't remember his name or face, but he remembered my phone number. I never expected his computer to have so many problems—I really regret giving him my number. The reason he found me was probably because he was a high school alumnus of my university classmate; they weren't particularly close either. Last summer his computer broke, and not wanting to spend money on a professional repair, my classmate mentioned my name to him, and so he found me. At noon while eating, I had no choice but to go install the system for him on an empty stomach. After installing the system, I installed antivirus and software for him, let him install games himself. He said he'd buy me a drink, I refused—that was hinting that helping him install the system wasn't for any benefit, and I didn't want to make money off him. As the saying goes, an upright person doesn't accept charity. If I helped him install the system once without any compensation, then next time he should sensibly not ask me again, out of embarrassment. And more seriously, if I had accepted that bottle of water, accepted his compensation, what would that mean? It would mean I'm someone who maintains computers for money, and from then on they could legitimately ask me to fix computer problems, dozens of yuan cheaper than a repair shop. Word spreads—one tells ten, ten tell a hundred—I couldn't handle that. I also didn't want to make that money; it has no real technical content and smells of freeloading. But in the end, he still dragged me out to eat, and I really couldn't refuse. He asked for my phone number, saying if there were any problems I could call him. Taking someone's hand makes it soft, eating someone's food makes it soft—I reluctantly gave him the number.

Later his computer had problems intermittently. The second time I went again, handled it a bit, fixed it. Looking at all the junk on his computer, it seemed he only knew how to play games and nothing else—I had no desire to befriend him at all. Gradually I got annoyed by his various uninteresting problems. I became more and more unhappy receiving his calls. Besides, I'd already said I didn't want to help ordinary people with computers anymore (helping Haifeng the day before yesterday doesn't count, that number 10 forward is okay), so today I gave him a very rude reply.

Excluding unavoidable circumstances, relationships between people are that simple. If you get along, there's something to gain, you share similar interests, or you're interested in the opposite sex, you'll be a bit warmer; otherwise, you'll dig deep ditches between various relationships with indifference.

posts.title: Deep Night, But I Can't Sleep
posts.excerpt: Although Zhang Henshui also has a novel called *Deep Night*, what I want to talk about is sleep, unrelated to that novel. Since April, no matter what I do or think, my reactions have been extremely sl...
posts.content:
Although Zhang Henshui also has a novel called *Deep Night*, what I want to talk about is sleep, unrelated to that novel.

Since April, no matter what I do or think, my reactions have been extremely slow—like an ordinary computer suddenly running too many programs, where you can move the mouse but clicking anything doesn't respond to right-click. Also, some things in life drew the worst lot for me; everything goes wrong. Compared to others it's no different, but my own feeling is particularly unpleasant. We're all in the same trough eating pig feed; a maverick pig suddenly can't understand why it has to root around in this trough every day, so it wants to fly out of the pen to take a look, but unfortunately it has no wings. Once that rare spark of thought is extinguished, it becomes even more dejected.

I started paying attention to my sleep issues. The main reason for being half a beat slow is that my sleep has been castrated by practicing the Sunflower Manual, wasting a lot of time.

Basically since second year of junior high, my sleep is no longer as deep as before. I start overthinking about the surrounding world—for example, what the houses around me should look like, where the highway at the school gate should lead, and so on. Probably haven't thought about topics related to "women, women..." yet. These wandering thoughts often keep me tossing and turning in bed past midnight. Some people in similar situations have even cried because of it. I haven't, but it must be very painful. Once I even went to a small clinic and told the doctor I couldn't sleep at night—he actually didn't prescribe me sleeping pills. Now of course I'm glad that doctor didn't give me sleeping pills back then; otherwise I might need medication to maintain sleep now, or I might have already swallowed a whole bottle of sleeping pills over something I couldn't figure out. Naturally, dear reader, you wouldn't see these rambling, worthless things I'm writing in a frenzy, out of boredom, with passion on office software.

Perhaps I'm already exhausted, but I've never practiced going to bed early. No matter how sleepy I am during the day, how my eyelids are fighting, I just don't want to sleep too early. Before midnight I'm usually online; when not online, I'm playing on my phone. During this pre-sleep time, I've added a lot of content to my read-novels list. If a novel is exciting, I often slowly go to sleep at one or two in the morning. When there's no novel to read, I listen to music. My phone has all kinds of music—Chinese, English, pop, classical, modern, nostalgic... Being able to listen to some music before sleep is a very pleasant thing. That day reading Han Han's *1988*, he said when he was young he liked listening to fast songs, later liked slow songs because he wanted to hear what they were singing. I had similar experiences. Freshman and sophomore year listening to Eminem, Green Day, Manson was my craziest time—I had to listen to a track before sleep too. Starting junior year, the songs I listened to gradually changed. In my phone I started collecting some sad, bright, gentle songs because I wanted to know what they were singing. So the memory card had songs by Chen Qizhen, Mayday, Liang Jingru, Stefanie Sun, Cao Fang. The saddest is listening to Liang Jingru in the dead of night, so I deliberately reduced her songs—occasional listening is enough. Then I transferred classical music transcoded from FLAC to my phone—Bach, Mozart, Vivaldi, Johann Strauss II, etc. They are neither restless nor sad, make me relaxed, and help me fall asleep the fastest.

Research institutions say people who sleep less have higher IQ, people who sleep more live longer. Choose high IQ or longer life? That's the question. What do I want? I don't want anything, I just don't want to sleep too early.

posts.title: All Things Bright And Beautiful
posts.excerpt: In high school, my homeroom teacher Lao Gao said he looked down most on people who keep pet dogs, because they can't find satisfaction in real life, so they need a loyal dog wagging its tail and beggi...
posts.content:
In high school, my homeroom teacher Lao Gao said he looked down most on people who keep pet dogs, because they can't find satisfaction in real life, so they need a loyal dog wagging its tail and begging for pity to soothe their vanity. I neither agreed nor disagreed with this view. Besides the guard dog we kept at our old home—the most common native dog, scientifically called the Chinese Pastoral Dog—almost no one in my life kept dogs as pets, so agonizing over the purpose of keeping dogs was meaningless to me.

These days I've been reading James Herriot's *All Things Bright And Beautiful*. This book was recommended to me by Roy (dearroy.com) in January this year. I bought it in February, and now it's May—I finally finished reading it completely. The beginning wasn't easy to get into; it took me two months to enter the story. I have to admit I'm very slow to warm up. A few days ago the power suddenly went out at noon. With nothing to do, I pulled out this book with a bookmark in the middle and continued reading. Certain emotions, after a long time, finally become fervent. I came to like this book. I read all the way until Harry enlisted, and the story ended. I think a person who loves life and loves living so much will have new stories wherever he goes.

From the opening *The Birth of a Lamb* to the penultimate story *The Old Man and the Dog*, the 38 stories are nothing more than accounts of veterinarian Harry treating animals for the villagers of Darrowby—pigs, dogs, cats, cows, birds, sheep, horses, even a person. But in these ordinary rural lives, Harry can write what looks to us like monotonous life in a witty, humorous, engaging way. This is precisely "all things bright and beautiful." A person without Harry's optimistic, positive, and humorous attitude toward life—like me—might never discover the beauty of life. Add this book to the list of healing songs, cute girls, handsome boys, and pictures. At least it will make people understand a truth that's been clichéd by middle school Chinese classes. Kawabata Yasunari explained in *The Unwept Flower* that "life is not lacking in beauty, but lacking eyes that discover beauty." I say life is so boring—every day you have to eat, go to work, go to class, sleep; every day face the same people you see every day, hear the same truths, log into the same websites, read similar tragedies in society, watch similar moods... I'm going crazy, I want to escape this madhouse without walls. Of course many people have the same thoughts as me, but their stories aren't my story. I counsel them with my second personality: "Life itself is like this." Now I must say I was wrong. Life isn't like this—it's just that we're not enthusiastic enough. Life is originally a shy little girl; a little teasing and she can be infinitely charming.

When reading *1988: I Want to Talk to the World*, when Lu Ziye and a woman traveled together on National Highway 318, from an aesthetic point of view, the image of Harry driving on a Yorkshire farm with a dog named Sam is a bit prettier. But the prettier it is, the lonelier its meaning. So Lu Ziye and the woman had endless stories driving all the way, while Harry could only discover the hidden beauty in life during house calls on the road—the valleys, the morning sun, the waterside—all worth stopping for, stealing moments of leisure in floating life. With beauty existing in repetitive life, everything becomes lively. The ordinary stories in the book often make me burst out laughing.

When Harry deals with animals, he never has an attitude of "just getting the job done." A person who loves animals must have a very soft heart; his life must be beautiful; he must also be beneficial and harmless to society. Even when Harry joined the Royal Air Force, he certainly wasn't going to kill people.

I also want to drive an off-road vehicle alone, with a German Shepherd, to some place.

*Errata: Thanks to classmate Guo Zheng Zhi Yu (edwardguo.com) for correcting: "Life is not lacking in beauty, but lacking eyes that discover beauty." This sentence was said by French sculptor Rodin; Kawabata Yasunari's original words were: "The beauty that humans perceive is limited. Precisely because humans' ability to perceive beauty is limited, the beauty humans perceive is limited, while nature's beauty is infinite."*

posts.title: Country Driving: A Chinese Road Trip
posts.excerpt: *Country Driving* was originally on my April book purchase list. Perhaps sales were too good—Dangdang and Joyo were both out of stock, and the printing house was busy reprinting. I suspect this was a ...
posts.content:
*Country Driving* was originally on my April book purchase list. Perhaps sales were too good—Dangdang and Joyo were both out of stock, and the printing house was busy reprinting. I suspect this was a hunger marketing strategy by the bookseller, so I only bought it back this month. Among the several books purchased at the same time, I read this one first, simply because its cover design is very beautiful—a two-way two-lane rural road stretching endlessly into the boundless distance, not knowing who has driven here or who will come here.

Actually, before buying it I already knew the general content of this book. Besides, many books' main themes can be guessed just from the title—social contradictions, economic development, etc. The subtitle of *Country Driving* is "A Chinese Road Trip from Farm to Factory." I think the author must want to reflect China's internal contradictions, similar to the book *China in Liangzhuang* from a while ago. Let's see how this foreigner understands China.

The author of this book, Peter Hessler (Chinese name He Wei), is a New Yorker correspondent in Beijing and a contributor to National Geographic. He obtained his Chinese driver's license in 2001. Over the next seven years, he drove around China's countryside and cities, then wrote these experiences into the book *Country Driving*, which was published in the United States in 2010. After Shanghai Translation Publishing House obtained the rights, they published this book in January this year. The copy I got is already the fourth printing in May. When I finished reading, I looked at other people's comments on Douban and found some deletions involving ethnic and religious issues—slightly regrettable.

The book is divided into three parts. It first narrates the author's journey of over ten thousand miles across northern China, following the Great Wall westward from the coast of the East China Sea. The second part focuses on the author's several years of rural living experience in a village below the Ming Great Wall in Huairou, Beijing, featuring the story of a peasant family's transition from farming to commerce. The last part writes about life scenes in industrial towns in Zhejiang. The author says he wants to explore the economy, trace the source of development, and investigate individuals' responses to change. But he doesn't do macro analysis; instead, he narrates the experiences of ordinary Chinese people to show the essence of China's transformation. Below is a speech by the author about this book. I didn't understand much, but I believe it will be helpful for some English experts.

I thought of some things similar to the book. During this period, rural areas in the west are becoming increasingly desolate. Young people have all left, and only the elderly and children remain in the villages. These children will likely be the last generation in these villages. My hometown is the same. When I was young, there were still young people in the village, and people were building new houses. Now when I go back every year for Chinese New Year, the village is cold and quiet. I can't even find peers my age—there's no New Year atmosphere at all. The village hasn't seen any newly completed buildings for many years. I often think this village will soon disappear. Villagers will all move to towns to settle, the houses left in the village will become more and more dilapidated, the land will be untended and overgrown with weeds, but it can't be sold because the land is collective asset—they only have usage rights. *Country Driving* quotes a researcher from the Chinese Academy of Social Sciences saying that China's land will eventually be privatized. But rural elders have very strong land concepts and often tell me the wisdom of "fallen leaves return to their roots." I agree with their thinking, but will those young people who grew up in more superior material conditions think the same? Probably not. Many of my uncles have bought houses in the city. Their next generation—my cousins—their hometown is the city where they live. My countryside—they don't understand it.

The fact that China's rural areas are declining or evolving in the midst of transformation is inevitable—this is one point narrated in *Country Driving*. But this book doesn't only talk about rural issues; there are also issues of personal development and urban development in China. There are many places worth thinking about, which readers can experience for themselves.

posts.title: End of June: Conversations and Walking
posts.excerpt: The last three people in the dorm all went back to Chengdu yesterday, but I took the train alone. Because those two were going to work at Foxconn in Chengdu for a month and the start date wasn't confi...
posts.content:
The last three people in the dorm all went back to Chengdu yesterday, but I took the train alone. Because those two were going to work at Foxconn in Chengdu for a month and the start date wasn't confirmed, I bought my ticket early. Later they were told everyone had to be in Chengdu on the 30th, so they hurriedly bought tickets for the 29th.

Next to me sat a sophomore girl from the Foreign Languages College, and across from her was a dark-skinned girl. I thought they were together, but they glared at each other for a long time. It turned out the dark-skinned girl was a local, not a student. Across from me, a rare empty seat appeared, and only after the train departed did a middle-aged uncle in his thirties sit down, carrying two cans of beer and a bag of braised chicken feet. The four of us in the seats didn't know each other. In a strange environment, a group of strangers contacting each other is truly a wonderful thing.

From the uncle's conversation, I inferred he worked at a public institution, but his parents had something going on, so he hurriedly took the train back. Later I learned he had served in the military in Tibet, stationed in Yadong. Perhaps this was the most unforgettable part of his life—his conversation was filled with countless senses of pride. He talked about how when he was guarding the border on a snowy mountain peak, old soldiers ordered him to stand punishment in the snow, how at night he used a quilt to cover snow water and boiled it for the old soldiers the next day, how on the mountaintop they ate ribs full of maggots and rotten vegetable leaves... In short, the hardships we've college students endured are nothing compared to his. I felt ashamed all over and deeply admired these people who had been tempered in harsh environments—possessing strong will and full of confidence in their future life. In one's lifetime, one should have life-and-death experiences like his, understand one's own potential, the preciousness of life, and the beauty of living.

Dealing with people is constantly finding common ground. Sure enough, we had some—all four of us were from the countryside. Even the dark-skinned girl across diagonally had brought two boxes of mangoes grown at home. Carrying two large boxes of mangoes was exhausting. I joked that if she didn't mind, she could sell them to me at market price. Of course, no one would agree to my request. The hardest part of the agricultural product sales chain is getting out of the mountains—one kilometer—and it's quite similar to the last mile in the express delivery industry. Once out of the mountains, it's a different price. So she should sell that box of mangoes to me at Chengdu price, otherwise it wouldn't cover costs.

The uncle said he really regretted transferring from rural to urban hukou back then. Now rural conditions are so good, and there's annual subsidy. Knowing no one would believe it, I still very stupidly said: now work hard to make money, and in a few decades when it's privatized, go buy land in the countryside. Then casually mentioned urban-rural hukou issues, like Chengdu's urban-rural integration development example. I noticed he couldn't follow the conversation, so I shut up. Talking to yourself in front of a group of people, others will think you're schizophrenic.

Utterly bored, I started flipping through the only *New Weekly* I brought home. For fifteen yuan, this magazine is really too thin. After two or three hours, I'd read all the inner-page ads word for word. Then I listened to a boy in the next seat loudly chatting with two girls traveling the same way, while secretly marveling at how the Foreign Languages girls had trained this boy to gossip just like them. He talked about some boy chasing a boy: "Do you have a girlfriend?" "No." "How about the two of us?" I almost vomited half a liter of blood. There are already few boys in Foreign Languages—what the hell are they doing! How can those girls bear it?

The laughter and chatter on the train accompanied the train's dull little logic. Moreover, the next day at nine o'clock, I dragged myself off the train exhausted.

That afternoon, after two and a half hours and changing buses three times, I finally arrived at Xihua University to pick up my two roommates' laptops. They were working outside, and the computers had become a burden, so they were temporarily stored at my house. In their decent zero-rent apartment outside Xihua campus, I found them. The air was stuffy and hot—unfortunately there was no fan in the room. I couldn't stand it, only stayed a short while, then carried the two computers home. While waiting for bus 310A, I encountered a girl wearing light brown glasses with long hair tied back. She was wearing a floral red short skirt and denim shorts, carrying a tote bag with the Jiaotong University emblem printed on it, looking very elegant. Later she also got on 310A. I kept looking back, paying attention, looking back again—she was gone.

At that time the bus hadn't entered the Third Ring Road yet.

posts.title: If the Heart Has No Evil, It Is Good; Staying Alive Matters Most
posts.excerpt: Luo Yonghao posted a multiple-choice question on Weibo: Walking in an alley late at night, there's a woman ahead walking in the same direction. This is a very awkward experience, especially when she s...
posts.content:
Luo Yonghao posted a multiple-choice question on Weibo: Walking in an alley late at night, there's a woman ahead walking in the same direction. This is a very awkward experience, especially when she seems a bit nervous and keeps looking back at you. If you maintain your original speed, it seems she'll stay nervous the whole time; if you speed up and overtake her, it seems she'll get even more nervous (at least more than before); if you simply stop and wait for her to disappear before continuing, she'll most likely think you're a hooligan who gave up on committing the crime at the last moment. I usually choose the third option. What about you?

Without thinking, I choose to overtake her. There was once, during the day—I don't remember the exact location, maybe by a river or outside a residential compound wall, the environment was quiet and secluded. Besides me and the girl in front, about twenty years old, there was no one else. In this situation, not only was the girl ahead nervous, I was also very nervous, wondering if this girl would mistake me for a stalker or robber. According to psychological research, such suggestions easily expose evil human nature. I can't continue with the premeditation—I must prove my innocence through action. So I brought out the supreme moral integrity in my personality, sped up, overtook this girl with great momentum, and left behind a magnificent broad back. Thinking darkly: girl, repent for your dark thoughts. How could I possibly be a bad person! Your unease is your own imagination.

Upon further reflection, I would rather stop and wait for her to disappear first. Choosing to overtake this girl is based on doing things according to my own ideals; waiting for this girl to disappear belongs to putting oneself in others' shoes against the backdrop of the social security environment. The approach is very gentlemanly—she couldn't possibly think of me as a hooligan who abandoned the crime. But if there's an urgent matter, I definitely won't wait for her to go first. Walking side by side is even more inappropriate. The best choice is still to overtake this girl, silently lower my head to watch the road, not look sideways, assume a contemplative posture and overtake at steady speed. If she sees I have no malice, she won't be nervous.

In my heart, one sentence keeps repeating: if the heart has no evil, it is good. As long as everyone has good cultivation and no dust in their heart, whatever they do won't have the slightest harm. Overtaking, walking side by side, waiting—none of it is inappropriate.

posts.title: The Ideal Afternoon: About Travel, Also About Wandering
posts.excerpt: Shu Guozhi's *The Ideal Afternoon* writes about his travel impressions in various places and his feelings and realizations in life. Though it looks like a thick book, there's actually not much text—yo...
posts.content:
Shu Guozhi's *The Ideal Afternoon* writes about his travel impressions in various places and his feelings and realizations in life. Though it looks like a thick book, there's actually not much text—you can easily finish it in two or three afternoons, as enjoyable as a travel guide.

But this book isn't a travel guide. It's an essay collection, more like a novel. Shu Guozhi is Dean from *On the Road*—he goes when he wants to go, stays when he wants to stay, relaxed and carefree, without any burden. His daily life is also extremely simple—no pursuit of brands, no pursuit of honors, not even a mobile phone, not overly bound by the outside world. This lifestyle is very rare among modern people. It's something I yearn for, yet something I fear.

There's not much to comment on about such a travel essay collection itself. I admire how Liang Wendao, the king of book jacket blurbs, could write such a long-winded preface for this book. As long as it pleases the heart, there's no need for clichés.

posts.title: A Few Things During the Day
posts.excerpt: Since coming home, I've barely gone out. Every day I do very little—nothing but going online, reading, and cooking. My daytime activities are casual and relaxed. Around nine or ten in the morning, tho...
posts.content:
Since coming home, I've barely gone out. Every day I do very little—nothing but going online, reading, and cooking. My daytime activities are casual and relaxed. Around nine or ten in the morning, though the dream has ended, my body is still lazy. No intention of getting up suddenly, I roll around in bed. After waiting a bit to wake up, I fumble for my phone, open Weibo, and dazedly watch the information loading circle spin round and round. After a long time, loading completes—no eye-catching news like someone's death confirmed, no plane crash, no bridge collapse—then I realize, another ordinary day...

I myself don't particularly like eating congee. Plus, due to the bad habit I developed at school, breakfast has always been convenient. There are many things at home like black sesame paste, instant soy milk, nutritious oatmeal, and instant coffee. After getting up, I make a cup, and that's considered having breakfast. Another reason for eating this way is that my parents often go out early. Even if they cook, no one eats on time. Gradually, breakfast is rarely made. Often it's steamed buns or bread with a drink. This perfectly suits my dietary preferences, and I don't have to be woken up to eat breakfast—killing two birds with one stone.

Among several things, going online is my main occupation. I don't browse the entire web. There are only a few websites I frequently visit. After a simple breakfast, after checking Weibo I browse Douban, after Douban I read blogs. Today's news scrolls by. Although I understand how many things in the world have nothing to do with me—they are actors, I'm the audience—even if I don't watch the world's laughter and play for a day, a month, or even a year, I can still live normally. But I can't control myself and only unconsciously check them; otherwise I feel empty inside. It seems I understand—my eyes are occupied by them because of emptiness. Only by closing them can I see myself clearly. Close my eyes, take a deep breath. My heart is full of anxiety; the air inhaled into my lungs desperately wants to erupt.

As for reading, it's often on summer afternoons. There's no expected silence—there's construction piling sounds, car engine roars, and children's fighting. Holding a newly bought book with only a shallow crease on the cover, I read very slowly, not meeting expectations. So I console myself: don't be greedy when reading, don't be eager for quick success, don't swallow dates whole, seeking thorough understanding is best. Though that's what I say, I can't fool my heart. Excellence comes from diligence; I waste time on play.

Because my mom has to work, alternating day and night shifts, sometimes lunch, sometimes dinner—my dad has entrusted it all to me. Washing green onions, cutting ginger, crushing garlic, steaming rice, boiling noodles, stir-frying dishes, washing pots, washing dishes, taking out trash—all these matters are numerous and complicated. My dad loves to eat, needs to add this, needs to add that—very different from my life philosophy. I like things simpler, don't pursue luxurious flavors, prefer boiling over stir-frying, add seasonings sparingly. If I have the chance to live alone, regarding food, it would probably be very simple.

It's midnight again, time to return to dreams. Time to sleep.

posts.title: One Hundred Years of Solitude: I Gave It Four Stars
posts.excerpt: My first encounter with *One Hundred Years of Solitude* was back in high school. There was a supplementary Chinese textbook at the time, and my fondness for it far exceeded the "Chinese Language" text...
posts.content:
My first encounter with *One Hundred Years of Solitude* was back in high school. There was a supplementary Chinese textbook at the time, and my fondness for it far exceeded the "Chinese Language" textbook full of cannibalistic stories. The excerpt from the first chapter of *One Hundred Years of Solitude* was included in it.

Many years later, facing my computer, I can no longer recall that stuffy afternoon reading the excerpt from *One Hundred Years of Solitude*. I only vaguely remember Lao Gao saying, "This is a great novel." But he also said that *White Deer Plain*, *Jean-Christophe*, and *Ulysses* were great novels. During one Chinese class related to rural China, he suddenly asked if anyone knew *White Deer Plain* and who wrote it. After a few seconds of silence, I recalled the novel in the bookstore about Erhei having an affair with a girl in the donkey grass, and answered, "Chen Zhongshi." His eyes lit up as he asked if I'd read it. I shook my head, and he seemed quite disappointed, suddenly raising his voice: "Hey, you don't even read such a great novel, what else are you reading!" As he spoke, his gaze swept over everyone.

So I approached the excerpt from the great *One Hundred Years of Solitude* with a classic attitude. "Many years later, facing the firing squad, Colonel Aureliano Buendía would remember that distant afternoon when his father took him to see ice." This sentence structure completely overturned my sense of time, and I regarded it as a classic. Recently, I learned that many Chinese writers have imitated this sentence structure. It seems their boasted classics only stop at this style, while their works haven't captured even half the essence of *One Hundred Years of Solitude*.

When I finally finished reading *One Hundred Years of Solitude* over three afternoons and evenings, I didn't feel the so-called shocking pleasure they talked about. This is a strange novel that reflects the historical development of Latin America. It narrates the process of the village of Macondo, which constantly explores and develops itself, ultimately declining and disappearing under the impact of modern civilization. Macondo's self-exploration had two stages. One was the gypsy stage. The gypsies traveling the world were just gentle facilitators. They brought the initial scientific products to Macondo but didn't teach the scientific principles. The people of Macondo were only in a curious enlightenment period. The second was the civil war period. The country's factional disputes gave people broader freedom and interest demands, but sometimes their war goals were unclear. Colonel Aureliano Buendía fought his whole life without even knowing why he was fighting. In the end, he just made small goldfish alone in a small workshop to pass the time. The modern civilization impact period refers to the banana planting period. The railroad-connected Macondo brought many foreigners to pan for gold here, but the people of Macondo needed more benefits, so the workers' movement broke out. The coalition government of conservatives and liberals shot more than three thousand group-intellectually disabled plantation workers to protect foreign interests. Afterwards, they destroyed the bodies to cover it up and announced to the outside world that the workers' movement had been properly resolved. It was a bit like *1984* or 1989—no one knew the truth, and the foolish masses only believed the official lies. Finally, after four years of heavy rain and ten years of drought in Macondo, Aureliano Babilonia, who fell in love with his aunt Amaranta Úrsula, deciphered the parchment, and the entire Macondo was wiped out in a hurricane, erased from the world's memory.

What is the root of all this? I think it's loneliness and selfishness. Because of loneliness, everyone was doing their own thing. Úrsula ran the candy store, José Arcadio Buendía explored the early world, the children were left to others to manage, Aureliano Buendía went to war because of loneliness, José Arcadio wandered the world because love had no place. Everyone was immersed in their own world, rarely communicating with others. Later, when victory was in sight, Aureliano Buendía didn't understand his purpose and gave up the war to compromise with the conservatives, abandoning all affairs including money and fame to focus on making small goldfish. His selfishness stemmed from loneliness, ultimately leading to foreign invaders colluding with the government. Macondo ultimately failed when accepting the new culture. However, the responsibility can't all be blamed on one person. During the war, the group worshipped the colonel; before the shooting, everyone was like a duck in the group collectively losing wisdom and being killed. Large-scale social ignorance was also one of the reasons.

In short, the story is wonderful. If I were Latin American, I would definitely give it five stars on Douban.

Honestly, I gave it four stars. Maybe I really should go read *White Deer Plain*.

posts.title: The Devotion of Suspect X: Who Can Understand Whose Deep Love
posts.excerpt: From the very beginning, this book is a grand deception. The only one truly in the know is the Galileo master: Yukawa's old classmate, Ishigami Tetsuya. He not only deceived the police and Hanaka Yasu...
posts.content:
From the very beginning, this book is a grand deception. The only one truly in the know is the Galileo master: Yukawa's old classmate, Ishigami Tetsuya. He not only deceived the police and Hanaka Yasuko but also every reader.

The middle-aged bachelor Ishigami Tetsuya works as a math teacher, a profession I dislike the most. His daily routine is walking from home to the place where Hanaka Yasuko works, using buying breakfast as an excuse to catch a glimpse of the woman he secretly loves, then heading to the middle school to teach. In the evenings, he does some math research on his own. Until one day, Ishigami discovers that Hanaka Yasuko and her daughter Misako have killed her ex-husband Togashi, who, though divorced, had been constantly harassing her. The story begins from there. Ishigami uses his genius intellect to dispose of the body and leads the police down the path he set, leaving them completely clueless. But in the end, Ishigami's university classmate, the equally brilliant physics researcher Yukawa Manabu, guesses the truth of the case from one of Ishigami's inadvertent details. So Ishigami turns himself in to the police. But why didn't Hanaka Yasuko turn herself in? Because Ishigami's surrender was also part of his plan from the very beginning. From the start, Ishigami designed everything with the premise that the police would identify him as the criminal, all to clear the mother and daughter's names.

That day, Ishigami was about to hang himself when suddenly someone knocked on the door. He had to open it to deal with it. A mother and daughter stood at the doorway—their eyes were so beautiful. Before that, Ishigami had never been moved by any kind of beauty. The thought of suicide vanished, and he regained the joy of life. Just imagining the life of the mother and daughter made him happy. He firmly believed that this pair was the most sublime beauty in the world. "For something sublime, being able to touch even the edge is enough happiness." So helping them was only natural. He didn't seek any reward; this was his repayment. He was willing to spend the rest of his life in prison for the mother and daughter.

To what extent can one love another person? What kind of encounter can make one willing to sacrifice their life without regret? Ishigami gave the best answer. This is the most selfless love—willing to dedicate one's entire life for another's good. But Yasuko was always selfish. She never loved Ishigami from beginning to end. Just before Ishigami was about to achieve all his set goals—clearing Yasuko of all charges—she ignored all of Ishigami's instructions and turned herself in to the police to ease her own conscience. But what else could she gain? Besides Ishigami's breakdown, both of them would spend the rest of their lives in prison. Would Yasuko's conscience then be at peace? One person only needs to live well to save another. If only Yasuko could understand that. Of course, as readers, we are also selfish. We hope Yasuko can live the rest of her life according to our wishes. But between people, there are always many unclear and inexplicable relationships. Who can understand whose deep love? Who can understand whose departure?

posts.title: Impermanence
posts.excerpt: Last time, after finishing my internship in Mianyang, I wanted to stay an extra day in Chengdu and decided to return to school the next day. That afternoon, after arriving in Chengdu from Mianyang, my...
posts.content:
Last time, after finishing my internship in Mianyang, I wanted to stay an extra day in Chengdu and decided to return to school the next day. That afternoon, after arriving in Chengdu from Mianyang, my classmates were all crammed into the Dicos at the North Train Station waiting for the train after 7 PM. Since I didn't have the key to my house, I went up too, ordered a juice, chatted with them, and took the opportunity to recommend fun places in Chengdu to my roommate from Guangxi. Maybe he wouldn't come to Chengdu again in his lifetime, so it was a good chance to show him around a bit. But he was too lazy to go shopping, and I didn't want to accompany him, so I called another high school roommate who was also a university alumnus to ask where he was. He said he was at my senior sister's place and asked if I wanted to come over.

This senior sister was also from my hometown. She was quite pretty, with a charming smile and a bit tanned. I usually called her Sister Lan. When I first arrived as a freshman, she treated me to a meal. Later, we ate together many more times, but our contact wasn't close—we only had each other's phone numbers, not many texts. Now I'm quite glad it was this way. I'm not a QQ fan myself and rarely take the initiative to message others to get closer. If I had, it probably would have gone cold long ago, and I wouldn't have this indescribably familiar feeling now. Seeing that the distance wasn't too far, I went over, planning to catch up with her and ask about employment prospects in our major.

Naturally, we ate dinner together. At a skewer restaurant near Xinhong Road, the owner pulled the table out to the flower bed by the sidewalk and invited us to sit. As sunset turned to night, the city's sky gradually darkened, streetlights came on, emitting a hazy glow. A few of us ate, drank, and chatted in this flower bed while cars slowly drove into the distant darkness. Sister Lan said she liked this feeling. I think no one would reject this kind of warm atmosphere. It seems like we're previewing our future days. Whether it's a few years or ten, when time flies by and life weighs us down until we can't breathe, having a few friends who can sit at a roadside small eatery, order a few plates of peanuts and a few glasses of draft beer, eating and talking—that's happiness, isn't it?

Many people have grand ambitions, but God won't arrange for all of them to be realized. The 80/20 rule exists, so most people can only be ordinary folks in this lifetime. Some people set their dreams too high and chase them too hard, ending up with nothing after a lifetime. It might be better to lower your stance, lower your dreams, and chase one small happiness after another around you. Conversely, how many people really want to do earth-shattering great things? Ambition is just ambition. Human innate laziness always stops their actions. Most people's days aren't lived according to life plans because they don't know when they'll die—maybe in the next second, maybe in sixty years. So it's better to say people live by miracles, grateful for impermanence's favor every second. Our well-being is nothing more than seeking a life without worries and a death without regrets. As long as we have a place to live, food to eat, some money to earn, a wife to marry, and children to laugh with, a plain and ordinary life is enough.

Yesterday, I watched Yu Hua's *To Live* with tears in my eyes. Life is impermanent. For some people, the peaceful years and stable present they long for are not easy things. Because we're just ordinary people, in the face of powerful external will and social turmoil, even if everything seems hopeless, we still have to keep living. Just being alive is good. Sooner or later, we'll transcend it all, and all suffering will eventually leave.

posts.title: How I Ended Up on the Irreversible Path of Independent Blogging
posts.excerpt: Two or three years ago, when I still wasn't used to using QQ, I was writing blogs on Baidu Space. Back then, I didn't have a computer, only a Motorola phone. Its functions look like a knockoff phone b...
posts.content:
Two or three years ago, when I still wasn't used to using QQ, I was writing blogs on Baidu Space. Back then, I didn't have a computer, only a Motorola phone. Its functions look like a knockoff phone by today's standards. When I was bored, I'd use its built-in browser to open Baidu Space and casually write a few words on the 2-something-inch screen. At the time, I didn't feel the slightest bit uncomfortable because I only wanted to record some joys and frustrations I encountered in life. When it rained, I'd write a couple sentences; when the sun came out, I'd write a couple sentences too. It was similar to writing Weibo today, and there were even over a hundred Space friends commenting back and forth. Back then, I was still a good young man, and the things I could encounter were all things I could see with my own eyes, so I never complained about any dissatisfaction with society. Those Space friends were the same. Now, whenever I think back, I feel that the hazy, vague time before was the purest period, and this purity came from my ignorance and dullness.

Later, when I got a computer, I'm glad I never got addicted to any games. After roughly experiencing their clearance modes, I abandoned them. But I was still depressed that I couldn't clear a church in Devil May Cry 4—I couldn't find the exit there and didn't want to look up game guides. After countless confidence collapses, I finally got tired of this game. When one window closes in life, God opens another for you. In my online life, I encountered some other things—the ubiquitous social chaos today. The ugliness they nakedly displayed to you couldn't help but make one angry. I wanted to record some of my views on these things on Baidu Space, which naturally couldn't avoid being extreme. But as you can imagine, according to relevant laws and policies, it was impossible to publish any disharmonious voices there.

In 2009, CN domains had a big promotion—one yuan each, and registration conditions were extremely loose. I followed the trend and registered a CN domain. At that time, I was still completely clueless about the internet. I had a domain but didn't know how to turn it into a website. Seeing some network links ending in .html, I assumed that all websites on the internet were written in HTML language. So I downloaded Dreamweaver, wrote a few HTML pages, found a free hosting service, and uploaded them via FTP. After carefully typing the domain into the browser, I saw the page I had written, and my mood was extremely excited—happier than finding a dime as a child. I wanted to announce to everyone that I was fucking starting to do IT too! I still feel ashamed now because I really did that later. I asked friends one by one to open this domain and take a look. I don't know what they were thinking at the time, but looking back now, it was really dumb.

Not long after, I realized that the internet was definitely not just HTML's world. Then I learned about ASP, ASP.NET, PHP, SQL, MySQL, and a bunch of other messy things. At the same time, I came into contact with open-source programs and understood that you could build a website without learning any language. In December 2009, I bought my first .com domain on GoDaddy. In May of the following year, I bought another .com domain and spent fifteen yuan to file it, pointing it to domestic virtual hosting. I installed WordPress via FTP. From then on, my independent blog officially began.

Like most independent blogs when they were first established, I focused my attention on the internet. I was the same, writing about Google, GAE, domains, hosting, open-source programs, and so on. Soon I started getting tired of these things, and I still feel the same now—I don't like independent blogs that are all about technology. Personally, I don't have much real technical skill worth mentioning. Besides, this kind of technology is everywhere on Google and not worth spending too much effort on. Therefore, it's better to return to the original intention of establishing an independent blog. It's precisely because one place doesn't allow you to write articles that you move to a free environment. Now it's time to continue the purpose that couldn't be achieved before—appreciating life, writing what life makes you think.

posts.title: Six Years of New Age Music History
posts.excerpt: The school finally understood the importance of the internet in life and gave each student an extra 10GB of data. Coincidentally, last month was National Day, and by the end of the month I still had o...
posts.content:
The school finally understood the importance of the internet in life and gave each student an extra 10GB of data. Coincidentally, last month was National Day, and by the end of the month I still had over ten GB left. So I took the opportunity to frantically download a lot of lossless music. Although I don't really understand the tricks of music—even now I only recognize do re mi fa—I have a drug-like love for them. If they weren't surrounding me, my life would definitely be very empty and boring.

My love for music started in junior high, but not the stuff in music textbooks. Even now, I still think that's a kind of torture—its ideology is greater than the knowledge itself. The knowledge conveyed by textbooks wants students to broadly understand them, not fall in love with them. However, real love for music should be to fall in love with them first, then understand them.

The first music style I liked was New Age. Back then, I didn't even know the name of this style. I just really appreciated these melodies that were comfortable to the bone—they integrated my emotions, my soul, my body, everything into them. It was a weekend evening. I was bored flipping through channels when I landed on CCTV-3 and saw an orchestra performing in the Forbidden City. An uncle was simultaneously controlling several electronic keyboards. His fingertips moved slowly, and clear, soothing sounds slowly flowed out from the electronic keyboards. Then he started conducting a large orchestra—I don't know how many people there were—everyone blowing or playing along with the uncle's conducting. The momentum was grand, and the entire Forbidden City was enveloped in this mysterious, heavy atmosphere. I don't know how much time passed before I finally found this uncle's photo in a music textbook. It was a scene of him performing at the Acropolis of Athens. It turned out he was Yanni.

I came into contact with Yanni more and more. I even specially bought his album from the Acropolis and recommended this person to my classmates. To this day, I still occasionally see some high school classmates listening to "With an Orchid" or "Nightingale" through QQ Music—these are his two most famous works, often appearing as background music on TV stations.

Bandari, as the background music prince, although also in the New Age music category, the tracks I like are only "Childhood" and "Annie's Wonderland." He's not as captivating to me as Joe Hisaishi. The first time I encountered Joe Hisaishi was while watching Hayao Miyazaki's animated film *Castle in the Sky*. His scoring was filled with endless ethereal quality. The melodious tunes seemed to take me to a distant sky. The more Japanese films and TV I watched, the more Joe Hisaishi I heard—every single one was a classic. In daily life, Joe Hisaishi always enters our lives intentionally or unintentionally. It's regrettable that many people don't know this and claim to be insulated from Japan. For example, our college radio station would play Joe Hisaishi's score "Summer" from * Kikujiro no Natsu* as the opening for a certain program at noon. With this kind of influence, Joe Hisaishi is undoubtedly Japan's undisputed music master, on par with Tanaka Ikko's status in the Japanese design world—unmatched. When taking stock of Japanese New Age representatives, another band that must be mentioned is "Peaceful Moon." The New Age music led by their company is also very famous. Even some Chinese films have had to imitate their style.

In the East, New Age music mostly exists as film background music, but in the West, New Age is a specialized music style that appears in music stores.

Besides Yanni, famous New Age music masters in Western countries include Bandari, Enya, Mike Oldfield, Secret Garden, and so on. I quite like Mike Oldfield, but he doesn't have many good-sounding pieces of music—perhaps related to the fact that he does too many music styles. Enya's music is more expressed through her heavenly voice. If I go a bit overboard, I'd say Enya's voice is much better than her arrangements. Her lyrics accompanied by her voice linger in the ears like poetry, making one unable to extricate themselves. Secret Garden's melodies are true to their name. When I listen to their music, it feels like religious music, conveying an atmosphere like some choirs in European and American films—solemn and reverent. There's also someone who must be mentioned. She became known to the nation at the Beijing Olympics opening ceremony. She's the Moonlight Goddess Sarah Brightman. She gives me a feeling a bit close to Secret Garden. I once found her shadow in the movie *The Fifth Element*—that blue alien singing once made me think she was Sarah Brightman.

Of course, foreign New Age music isn't limited to these. Among the twenty-something GB of lossless music I downloaded, there are also some I don't like in the New Age style, like the Pope's Choir—its seriousness seems a bit excessive.

Music has no borders. Whether it's the easternmost Japan or the westernmost Ireland, New Age has given me more personal spiritual enjoyment and emotional experience than any other music. I'm very grateful that there's still such a style belonging to all humanity in the world, and I hope China can produce a New Age music master, because Chinese film scoring is too weak.

————This is my music appreciation class paper, but it's not a paper.

posts.title: Mid-October: An Unfinished Cycling Trip
posts.excerpt: In mid-October, some classmates organized a cycling trip. With no prior experience to draw from, we just hit the road. In the end, underestimating the terrain, we turned back when we were still over t...
posts.content:
In mid-October, some classmates organized a cycling trip. With no prior experience to draw from, we just hit the road. In the end, underestimating the terrain, we turned back when we were still over twenty kilometers from Gesala. After the previous evening’s five or six hours of pure mountain climbing, no one was eager to push on to the Gesala scenic area at over three thousand meters elevation, much as we regretted it. The bikes were rented together—twenty yuan a day, and pretty heavy.

Setting Off

From the mountain top on the way down, note the altitude and time. It took everyone five hours to reach the summit of this mountain. I’d eaten through all my food, exhausted and thirsty, desperately wanting to collapse into the dry ditch by the roadside and sleep before continuing. But my companion, Brother Liao, stayed with me the whole way—I’m deeply grateful to him. On this journey I discovered for the first time the virtues of cigarettes: a piece of candy and a smoke, and all the fatigue vanished until we reached the top.

They camped in the rear while four of us pushed ahead to Qinghe Waterfall. From there it was still 26 kilometers to Gesala, with a 1,700-meter elevation difference. The stream and waterfall were modest, but the canyons on either side were extremely steep, with loose rocks scattered along the path. I kept worrying a falling stone might hit me—otherwise the classmates who came with us would face a real tragedy. I felt I’d gained some kind of realization here, something about life. Thank God, thank the Buddha for protection.

We camped on a riverbank for the night. I was anxious about flash floods, but an old man eventually came by and put my worries to rest.

On the third day we headed back, passing through Yumen Town, part of the Ertan Reservoir area. The environment and climate here already felt completely different from the city—moist and comfortable, very much like the climate of the Sichuan Basin.

The images below are in Google’s WebP format for testing purposes. Chrome and Opera support it natively and can display them directly.

posts.title: Rama's Bridge
posts.excerpt: While reading this book, I had countless moments where the thought "I must learn English well" flashed through my mind. Besides, not long ago I replied on my blog: "In this lifetime, I must learn a pr...
posts.content:
While reading this book, I had countless moments where the thought "I must learn English well" flashed through my mind. Besides, not long ago I replied on my blog: "In this lifetime, I must learn a programming language, PHP or Python; learn a foreign language, English or Japanese." I'm already 22 years old now...

The reason for learning English well is simple. If one day I suddenly have a little spare money, I can sling on a backpack and hit the road. Today on Tianya Community, I saw a girl who traveled all over Southeast Asia to South Asia with just ten thousand yuan. This shows that outbound travel isn't exclusive to the middle class. Basically everyone can achieve it as long as they're willing to take the first step.

In reality, *Rama's Bridge* is a story about a rich second-generation kid traveling in India. The author, Zheng Chen, online handle "Poison," spent three months traveling in India in 2010, then took another year and a half to write this novel. From the plot and accompanying photos, I feel this book has more real elements than fiction, and it also feels like the author himself is quite wealthy.

India is a magical country. Although it's right next to China, I know less about it than Brazil on the other side of the earth. I only know it's the world's largest democracy, but also a country with very low per capita income and a huge wealth gap. So every time there's an online debate about national systems, India always appears as a counterexample, indicating that democratic systems aren't suitable for China. The author repeatedly expressed his disgust for India in the novel—because the Indian government has low efficiency, officials are vain and extravagant, and the people are desperate, indifferent, greedy, and not clean. But India also has its lovable aspects. Darjeeling in the Northeast is a very famous tourist destination in India with beautiful scenery and simple, honest people. Unfortunately, the people there have no sense of belonging to the Indian government and often have separatist activities. When reading about all these bad things about India, shouldn't a trace of superiority as a Chinese person flash through my mind? Not at all!

In the novel, the person traveling with the author is a Chinese-descended woman, Miss Darjeeling. She grew up in India, completed university in Canada and obtained Canadian citizenship, then worked as staff at an international company's India office. But her feelings toward India are very complicated. She thinks of herself as an Indian, but locals don't see it that way. She wants to live in China, but China no longer has any connection to her. No matter where she is, she's someone who's not understood. I can feel her helplessness. Perhaps except for overseas Chinese in Southeast Asia, overseas Chinese from any other country have more or less the same emotional experience as her. Having no relatives in China means being forever disconnected from mainland China, becoming a middleman that neither country welcomes.

The novel ends with Rama's Bridge. Rama's Bridge is a series of limestone shoals between Mannar Island near the northwest of Sri Lanka and Rameswaram on India's southeast coast. The seawater in this area is very shallow, only one to ten meters deep, and some dunes are dry. It's said that before the 15th century, pedestrians could walk on it. Here they encountered an Indian policeman named Raghu. He was a survivor of the 2004 tsunami in Thiruchendur and was taken in as a godson by the police chief of Rameswaram. He was very upright in his work. While supervising Miss Darjeeling going to handle visa renewal, he was angered by officials who wouldn't accept money but also wouldn't do anything, causing the author's trip to have many unnecessary troubles. Miss Darjeeling therefore said: "But... a person's integrity... here... is fucking pathetic!..."

Perhaps Raghu was India's hope, but one night he disappeared. I guess he walked toward Rama's Bridge.

posts.title: Faith
posts.excerpt: Behind our village there's a mountain, the highest one for miles around. There's also a Buddhist temple on the mountaintop. It used to have grand halls and flourishing incense. During temple fairs, pe...
posts.content:
Behind our village there's a mountain, the highest one for miles around. There's also a Buddhist temple on the mountaintop. It used to have grand halls and flourishing incense. During temple fairs, people from villages within ten li would come here to worship. After the events of '98, although it was repaired multiple times, it became an occasional activity center for the elderly to hold temple fairs. But you can still see what it went through from the broken bricks and tiles scattered around.

After a countdown of ten seconds, with a series of "boom boom" sounds from detonators exploding, that year I personally witnessed this beautiful Buddhist temple collapsing with a crash amid flying stones and huge clouds of dust. That day, not many believers came to witness the disappearance of this piece of history. They said they couldn't bear to watch, but actually they couldn't get up there either—many people coming down from Santala had completely blocked the surrounding roads. A few kids and I, along with some young people, stood on another hilltop and watched all of this without any feeling, then excitedly returned to the dim classroom to continue our lessons. It wasn't until later that I could see a hint of terror from the headless land god's mutilated body that had been dug up. Maybe I really did treat him as a god, thinking he would bring disaster to our impoverished land.

The sun rose as usual. After the temple was blown up, those people disappeared. No one from above came to the village to promote religion versus superstition—just more bickering among the elderly. Fortunately, no disasters ever befell us. They said it was because of sincere hearts, and that the leader who dug up the land god had his hand broken, and another person had his leg broken. But no one had seen it—they weren't locals. Educated by books, I became an atheist. I've also been to temples in many places and seen fat and thin versions of Shakyamuni and Guanyin Bodhisattva, but I've never burned incense or donated money. In my eyes, they're just scenery, spiritual sustenance for some people. Or rather, gods are spiritual sustenance. If everyone has their own ideals, pursuits, and principles for dealing with the world, then everyone has faith.

The village where I grew up, in countless confused years of the past, generation after generation lived here, farming and tilling the land. There was no written history, no beautiful future to look forward to. Living was the entirety of life. They hoped not to get sick, hoped their families wouldn't get sick. But who would promise them that? Was it the government that forced people to drink pesticide and commit suicide because of high taxes back then, or the invisible charitable organizations? Naturally, this is an embarrassing joke. Hungry people can't establish transcendent personal faith. So religion, which can be used without thinking, became their best choice. As long as gods could bless them with food and clothing, what did it matter whether it was Buddhism, Christianity, or Islam?

Although the rise of that temple in our village stemmed from villagers' lives becoming increasingly difficult, the meaning of religion isn't about poverty alleviation and prosperity. Furthermore, religion wasn't originally born to solve humanity's basic needs. I've read a bit of the *Shurangama Sutra* and feel that religion's purpose is actually to cultivate believers' personal faith—teaching people to learn to endure difficulties, pursue an optimistic and open-minded attitude toward life, be charitable and benefit others. This is quite similar to the spiritual realm explored by some atheists.

The most frightening situation is when some people have neither correct religious faith nor appropriate personal faith. Some elderly people believe in Buddhism and donate all the family's money to the temple, buying countless oil lamps and burning them, seemingly forgetting that this money comes from their children's hard work and also includes their own food money. This kind of faith is very selfish in purpose—not Buddhism's pursuit. You could say the popularization of Buddhism locally has been a failure. There are also people who don't believe in religion and haven't learned the correct personal pursuits in life. Their life's purpose is entirely about pursuing wealth, satisfying their own preferences at the expense of others' health and interests—making fake medicine, fake milk powder, letting substandard products circulate on the market. But they haven't taught how to be a proper person. In *Those Years, We Were Chasing Girls Together*, Nine Knives mentioned that when they were in elementary school, they went to the mountains to study Buddhism. This kind of education method is very enviable. Regardless of whether they can learn personality development in class, at least they have one more religious choice.

On November 25th, in the waiting room of Taiyuan Railway Station in Shanxi, an elderly person suddenly died. A monk among the crowd of onlookers forming a large circle held the hand of this elderly person to perform a funeral rite. Although performing rites for the deceased has no meaning in the eyes of atheists, I saw respect for life and a proper view of death.

posts.title: To Live
posts.excerpt: If I had to name my favorite contemporary Chinese novelist, besides Yu Hua, I couldn't find a better answer. The reasons are, first, because I've read few novels, so naturally there are even fewer wor...
posts.content:
If I had to name my favorite contemporary Chinese novelist, besides Yu Hua, I couldn't find a better answer. The reasons are, first, because I've read few novels, so naturally there are even fewer works I can say I like. Second, because I like Yu Hua's understanding of human nature in his works. Although Wang Xiaobo's works are more inspiring and full of shining points everywhere, I feel Wang Xiaobo's personality is too different from mine. I don't know if he's a glib person—his language style always has a cynical feeling, funny but not likable to me, though still worth reading. Books are bridges for spiritual communication between authors and readers. I like works that resonate with my own spirit. They both strengthen my attitudes that I was half-convinced about and correct those erroneous views. There are also some works that, although not acceptable to people, their ideas may have already surpassed our current level and need to be read carefully—like a kind of crawling long-distance worship and cultivation. Only after many years will one reach the holy and sublime palace.

The human nature in Yu Hua's works is that most authentic human nature facing reality, the most ordinary and plain human nature. He doesn't conceal history, nor does he criticize history. He just places characters in a grand environment and lets them grow. Most people, when facing pressure, have an attitude of resigned acceptance yet unwilling to be lonely. In the end, they still achieve nothing repeatedly. Their own character and attitude are worn smooth by time, revealing a kind of inclusive warmth—without despair, without complaint, as if seeing through everything in life. This is the human nature in Yu Hua's works, and this is most precious. Speaking of this, I can't help but think of another novel, Lu Yao's *Ordinary World*, which I also like very much. Unfortunately, I've only read one of Lu Yao's works, and that was during high school, so I dare not comment rashly.

The reason I want to write about this is because, months after reading Yu Hua's novel *To Live*, today I watched Zhang Yimou's film of the same name adapted from it, and it brought me no less sense of desolation. It turns out Zhang Yimou was once so talented. It's a pity he's fallen to his current state. Starting from *Hero*, one film after another has been met with curses. Has he really exhausted his talent? I don't think so. Such a big country shouldn't be unable to produce a master who can make films. Iran, with a smaller population than us, has *Children of Heaven*. India, which is economically more backward than us, can make *3 Idiots*. There are many reasons for this situation. First is the despicable and shameless domestic cultural censorship system. Films, TV, and books are all trampled before being presented to the public. Therefore, authors and directors will castrate themselves once before submitting for review. Second is the prevalence of utilitarianism. To make more money, they make some incomprehensible blockbusters. And the second reason may be the evil consequence of the first point. A country doesn't only have main melody and colorful history—they can also have reality and future. But reality and future are precisely too sensitive. Those directors don't know what else they can film, so they have to use some lowbrow spoofs, time-travel, and other period dramas to fool the public. There's an even more tragic fact. The State Administration of Radio, Film, and Television says don't make too many ancient themes either. Since the Anti-Japanese War started in the Ming Dynasty as China's main melody, I guess in the future everyone will only be able to watch a sci-fi blockbuster where one Chinese person shoots a hundred Japanese people.

posts.title: Carrying a Book and Walking Around the Park
posts.excerpt: The driving school situation is getting more and more urgent. After 8 AM, I wolfed down a beef patty and ran over. Finally got a chance to swipe my card, but the time was in the afternoon. So I left t...
posts.content:
The driving school situation is getting more and more urgent. After 8 AM, I wolfed down a beef patty and ran over. Finally got a chance to swipe my card, but the time was in the afternoon. So I left the training ground with an empty feeling, saying: "Practicing driving is a waste of time—swiping the card is the real business!"

When I came, I wanted to bring a novel. After 7 AM in the dorm, I opened my Kindle, and a charging prompt popped up on the dim screen. I had no choice but to pick through a pile of books I'd never had the patience to read, went through them twice, and finally chose *Influence* to bring along. A classmate who saw me often bringing books to driving school and never saw me read much said: "You're bringing a book to pretend again." Of course, I didn't care at all about his opinion, let alone the opinions of the uncles and aunties studying with me. In life, there are always some unrelated people who like to lecture you, wanting you to embrace their deeply ingrained values and views on life. Arguing is useless— the only thing to do is smile with a kind of incurable pity for them. Reading, regardless of initial intentions, as long as the reader persists, their intellectual temperament will be like something finely carved, until even they themselves find it incredible. If a person is willing to read, as long as they have time and it doesn't affect others, then go read.

So after coming down from the dirt road in front of the driving school, I diagonally crossed a wasteland surrounded by walls, took a wrong turn down a dead-end road, climbed a few steps up a dirt slope without bushes, arrived at the park, and stepped onto the smooth asphalt road. On Saturday, the park had many people—there was even some government agency exercising in formation with red flags. I walked and read along this stretch of road until I reached a car surrounded by a noisy crowd. It turned out that agency was holding a climbing-with-prizes activity and was distributing prizes to participants. Suddenly, out of the corner of my eye, I spotted Teacher Zeng up ahead. We chatted for a bit. He said he was just here to climb the mountain. I really wanted to immediately turn around and walk with him, using my graduation project as an excuse to discuss my exam from the night before yesterday. But it felt unreliable, so I ultimately held back. I continued descending the mountain while reading, flipping through dozens more pages of the book. An hour later, I finished circling the park and entered the school.

At noon it was my turn to swipe the card. I brought my Kindle up and read a bit of *Eleven Kinds of Loneliness*.

posts.title: Rolex on the Liberation Monument — Chongqing
posts.excerpt: Originally I planned to visit Mount Hua, but since no one was going with me and I didn't feel comfortable going alone, I gave up the idea. Later, I went to Chongqing with Zhang Shaoxia. It was the end...
posts.content:
Originally I planned to visit Mount Hua, but since no one was going with me and I didn't feel comfortable going alone, I gave up the idea. Later, I went to Chongqing with Zhang Shaoxia. It was the end of August, scorching hot, with temperatures reaching a terrifying 41°C. Yet the furnace-like heat couldn't deter my determination to visit Chongqing.

The bullet train from Chengdu to Chongqing costs 98 yuan, about 20 yuan more than the Chengdu-Chongqing express bus, but it offers much better value. Besides, I'm not worried about bullet train rear-end collisions—the probability of two such incidents happening within 30 days is tiny.

Chengdu is a plain city encircled by ring roads. You can't appreciate its "waistline" from different angles like a mountain range, nor get the panoramic view from its peak. When I took a bus from Chongqing North Station straight into the city and crossed the Huangguayuan Bridge, the sight of Yuzhong District's layered buildings rising along the river gave me immense visual satisfaction. It's exactly the feeling you get seeing Manhattan from Brooklyn in American TV shows—except Yuzhong is built on hills, while Manhattan is flat.

Chao Tianmen, Chongqing's iconic area, was a bit disappointing at first sight. Walking down Xinyi Road, where the sidewalks were under renovation, the narrow streets were lined with old buildings and lacked the bustling commercial atmosphere I imagined. It's hard to believe this is near a landmark district. Chao Tianmen Square is small and looks its age. Unlike Chengdu's Tianfu Square, which gets torn down and rebuilt repeatedly, this one has stayed. Around 5 PM, there weren't many people—mostly out-of-towners, identifiable by their point-and-shoot cameras snapping photos everywhere, just like me.

I thought I could visit Chongqing University that evening to see Hao'er, but the distance turned out longer than expected. He said it would take over an hour; I had underestimated Chongqing's terrain. Since I was staying in Yuzhong District, round trip would take too much time. We went the next day instead. My rhinitis is acting up again.

Among pedestrian streets in Southwest China, Chunxi Road in Chengdu is the most famous, followed by Jiefangbei (Liberation Monument) in Chongqing, then Nanping in Kunming. Kunming's Nanping wins on size; Chongqing wins on beautiful women—Chengdu should rightfully claim the title, but Chunxi Road loses because it lacks benches under big trees where visitors can sit and people-watch. At Jiefangbei, the granite benches under the trees are prime territory for "wolf friends" (guys checking out girls). My classmate once said he people-watched from KFC at Jiefangbei—what an idiot. The current Liberation Monument was rebuilt in November 1949 after the 1945 version was destroyed. Now it has a clock on top, with a Rolex sign.

The most worthwhile place in Chongqing is the Nanshan Observation Deck, perfect for night views. Taking the bus up from Nan'an District, the nearly 10-meter bus winds along the two-lane mountain road, climbing over a hundred meters in elevation. With the engine roaring, that one-yuan bus ride felt totally worth it.

On the third day, we headed back. Across the street, various aunties asked if we wanted to sing karaoke—I had no idea what they meant.

posts.title: 2011 Year-End Summary
posts.excerpt: Time flies faster and faster. It feels like yesterday I was on the rooftop of Building 1, facing that massive paper transmission device, and tomorrow is already 2012, no matter how hard I try to resis...
posts.content:
Time flies faster and faster. It feels like yesterday I was on the rooftop of Building 1, facing that massive paper transmission device, and tomorrow is already 2012, no matter how hard I try to resist. As usual, it's time to write the year-end summary...

Overall, this year was neither good nor bad. Compared to last year's events, it felt quite dull. Almost no exercise, didn't learn much, didn't find the other half of my life—life's fluctuations were zero. Before I knew it, another fraction of my life slipped away in mediocrity. Looking at it from any angle, it's rather sad.

I counted my QQ status updates from 2011—exactly 40, maybe only a third of 2010's. If these statuses reflect my expectations and attachments to life, then clearly my mood was very down this year. On the last day, I tried to post a status. After racking my brain, I only managed "Happy New Year to you all." After sending, it somehow posted to my own message board. I mocked myself a bit, deleted it with two clicks. Time and distance aren't just killers of romance—sometimes friendships and family ties also perish at their blade. Oh well. I don't know what else to say.

This year on Weibo, I was obviously very active, but that activity only highlighted an overwhelming, irrepressible loneliness. Written expression amplifies the weight of one's beliefs.
