I just wanted to say something, no matter how faint the impact. Weibo delivers endless real-time information to netizens—some find it boring, some find it interesting. I think you have the kind of sky that matches your soul. What you follow determines how you feel, as you've seen.

In February of this year, I went back home for two classmate reunions. My junior high classmates were pure and warm, as if we hadn't been apart for six years. My high school classmates each had their own circles and mindsets. I guess the difference is that most elementary and junior high classmates have ventured out into society, experienced the preciousness of hometown connections after drifting elsewhere, so they were more enthusiastic at the gathering.

Near the end of the year, one afternoon my dad suddenly fainted. Friends took him to the hospital before calling my mom, saying he was unconscious. The sudden news shocked us! On the taxi to the hospital, I suddenly felt an unbearable weight. We got stuck in heavy traffic for what felt like forever. I ran through countless scenarios in my head, preparing for every possible outcome life might throw at me. I remember I didn't pray that day—life is just like this, and besides facing it calmly, there's nothing else to do. We even took a wrong turn and ran several extra blocks before finding the right hospital. I rushed into the ER and found Dad awake, tears around his eyes from the pain. Then I handled all the hospital procedures. Thankfully, Dad was fine. I guessed it was probably related to his diet. So during summer break, I ate a lot of rabbit meat—Dad said rabbit has only 3% fat.

Then in April and May, I met Xiaoxin—the lively one in our group. She was the catfish in the catfish effect. After she stirred things up, I met Xiaoluo, Minmin, and a guy from my department. We went from online to offline dinners together. Interestingly, the first person I met online from my department was a girl. Our department had so few people that once she revealed a bit of info online, I quickly identified her through elimination. Unfortunately, minutes later she also figured out who I was. That's how we met.

Academically, nothing much to say—university is like that. If you study well, you pass; if not, you fail. Some chase girls, some play games, some... well, you know. Each does their own thing, goes home to their own mom. I stayed home for a month during summer break, bought a bunch of novels to read, accomplished nothing. Mom pushed me to find a part-time job; I wanted to work with Dad, but it all fizzled out. In the hottest part of August, I spent a few days in Chongqing, met up with ZH classmate. First time meeting a blog friend, and I forgot to take a photo—kind of regrettable. Three days after returning from Chongqing, I went to Mianyang and stayed in its famous red-light district for over ten days, witnessing how fallen women, police, and other citizens coexist peacefully. At the Changhong factory, through interactions with employees, I discovered the company has very low efficiency. As one training center supervisor put it: "Before, you'd drive a BMW in and drive a motorcycle out. After the plasma screen production line started, now you can drive an Accord out." Watching their stock this past half-year, I think that statement was spot on.

After returning to school, in mid-October I went on a bike trip and completely fell in love with this mode of travel. Later, on a high school classmate's space who also loves cycling, I found a quote I really liked: "Even as a woman, one should have the heroic spirit to wander the world with a sword." As a man, what do I have to fear?

During National Day, I went home, visited Qionghai Lake, and the construction site of Guanghan Flight Academy. Back at school, I participated in campus recruitment and got nothing, but my views on employment changed a lot—I no longer want to work.

At the end of November, I went to driving school and began the daily commute life. Due to time constraints, I still couldn't take the test.

(Ended on New Year's Day 2012)

posts.title: Returning Home
posts.excerpt: China is a vast country with a huge population. Uneven economic development has driven many western residents to leave their homes and families to work in the developed eastern regions. Yet China is a...
posts.content:
China is a vast country with a huge population. Uneven economic development has driven many western residents to leave their homes and families to work in the developed eastern regions. Yet China is also a tradition-heavy nation. Every January and February, Chinese people working away from home return to their hometowns to reunite with loved ones and celebrate the most festive and significant Spring Festival of the year. This has given rise to the world's largest annual human migration. Naturally, trains have become the ideal choice for ordinary people with modest incomes.

Every semester end, the school sees a group of students running a business reselling train tickets. You hand over your student ID, pay a 5-yuan service fee, and they'll deliver the ticket to you. It's said that successful teams can earn ten to twenty thousand yuan in about ten days—a pretty good business. My roommate, after failing at romance and racking up debt, saw students' desperate need to go home and joined this trade. But because the team didn't manage accounts properly, some people borrowed from Peter to pay Paul and lined their own pockets. In the end, he only made a little over 100 yuan—basically wasted effort.

I have to say 12306.cn was the most successful e-commerce platform of 2012. Some uncles and aunties who never go online started shopping online, lured by the ticket rush. Around the 5th or 6th, my youngest uncle called me saying train tickets from Guangzhou back to Sichuan around the 14th were sold out. He heard you could book online and asked me to buy one for him. That morning, I struggled to log into the poorly designed booking site and searched train by train for a long time before finding a standing ticket. Thinking about how crowded trains get during Spring Festival—forget about using the bathroom, there's barely room to stand—I couldn't bear to let him suffer that. I hesitated about just telling him there were no tickets. But that would be cold, especially toward family. In the end, I told him the truth and let him choose. He decided to trade one kind of suffering for another and take the bus back to Sichuan. Still, it hurts.

They say a good man wanders the world with a sword. But what age does "good man" refer to? At half a century old, still having to venture far from home? Maybe "venture" isn't even the right word—they've long passed the age of youthful wandering. I don't believe that at this midpoint in life, my youngest uncle will have many more blessings coming. Getting rich overnight or even moderately wealthy seems unlikely. Time to return home.

posts.title: Mourning
posts.excerpt: I just got back on the 23rd when Grandma asked me to guess whether the family down the hill could have a peaceful New Year. When I asked why, it turned out the old man of that family was in very poor ...
posts.content:
I just got back on the 23rd when Grandma asked me to guess whether the family down the hill could have a peaceful New Year. When I asked why, it turned out the old man of that family was in very poor condition and probably wouldn't make it. Later I learned that two elderly people from the neighboring household had also passed away—one on the 7th day of the lunar new year last year. I didn't know. These past few years, every time I come home, I hear news of some elderly person passing. If I'm away, I wouldn't know, because their lives are so insignificant that even the whole village barely notices.

On New Year's Eve, the child from the family down the hill came to our backyard to move bricks. Strange. When I asked, I found out his grandfather had passed away at the age of ninety. According to our local custom, we say he "got old." Whenever someone mentions that a person "got old," we all understand what it means. As old age approaches and death nears, everyone has already quietly prepared themselves for other elderly people to pass at any time. They just never want to voice this thought that's already taken root. When someone dies, upon hearing "got old," they simply respond with a soft "oh."

Because of the holiday, the family didn't make any arrangements at first. One day I walked through their courtyard and saw all the children and grandchildren laughing and playing cards right in front of the memorial hall where incense and candles were burning. The atmosphere of death was completely gone. Grief and tears are reserved for formal mourning rituals. Only facing the self-generating and self-extinguishing nature of life with a smile is truly comforting. But custom dictates that when an elder passes, there must be people crying and mourning, otherwise it means the children are disrespectful. Since commercialization turned every relationship into something that can be bought with money, funeral bands now come with professional mourners who can wail to the heavens and seem utterly devastated—guaranteed to match the grief of the North Korean people when Kim Jong-il died. By the 6th or 7th, it was time for the burial. They hired chefs from out of town to prepare the funeral banquet and treated the entire village, relatives, and friends to two meals, with wine and tea served. "Happy New Year!"—the toast from the four sons of the deceased struck me as a bit funny, and I didn't know what to say, so I just raised my cup and drank it down.

The next morning at the burial, firecrackers went off all the way from their house to the graveyard. I didn't go watch—after all, one more person was gone, and one more grave was added. Later I heard that while stacking stones for the grave, someone got injured. He lives on the other side of the mountain, a bit older, and apparently needed money. He was paid 160 yuan to help carry the coffin, but a stone crushed and broke one of his fingers. If there was any compensation, I doubt it was much.

After the four sons kept the incense burning for seven days, they left, leaving behind an empty house.

posts.title: Celebrating the New Year
posts.excerpt: On February 2nd, I caught the 7 AM bus back to Chengdu. This year's New Year was over again. Unlike previous years, this time there wasn't that reluctant feeling of leaving home. The day before, I wen...
posts.content:
On February 2nd, I caught the 7 AM bus back to Chengdu. This year's New Year was over again. Unlike previous years, this time there wasn't that reluctant feeling of leaving home.

The day before, I went to town. At the village entrance, I looked at the row of locked houses above and felt a wave of dead silence. This bend has ten households, including my grandparents. Usually only five elderly people and one child live here. Those who haven't gone out to work mostly live in the township and only occasionally return to pick some vegetables before hurrying away. One day on the road, I ran into someone about twenty years older than me who went to school with my dad. According to family hierarchy, I should call him "brother." He talked about their childhood games, vividly describing the lively atmosphere when the village was full of people. But now, forget about children's noise—even dog barks are rarely heard. Though I don't want to, I have to foresee that this village at the county border, just two or three kilometers from the national highway or Chengdu-Chongqing Expressway, will eventually lose its vitality—not because of natural disasters or war, but due to the inevitable population flow from rural urbanization. I'm always worried this village will be blown away by a sudden wind like Macondo in *One Hundred Years of Solitude*, disappearing from people's memories.

Only those far from home can truly feel the imprint their hometown has left on their hearts. I grew up in the countryside, and its mountains and waters, winds and clouds, fields and lands are deeply etched in my heart. In dreams, I can still see the shadow of myself as a child chasing someone at the dumb mouth of the valley—I don't know who it was. Maybe it was Dad leaving home, or maybe someone on a motorcycle. But these fragments of memory are about to lose their roots, like duckweed floating with the current.

posts.title: Blind Date
posts.excerpt: Tonight I went to my uncle's house for dinner and saw my younger cousin and his blind date girlfriend all over each other. I was secretly amazed. Let me first talk about his family situation. My uncle...
posts.content:
Tonight I went to my uncle's house for dinner and saw my younger cousin and his blind date girlfriend all over each other. I was secretly amazed.

Let me first talk about his family situation. My uncle's family in Chengdu has a car and a house, no debts. They co-own a small clothing factory with friends, have several shops and some small investments. I don't know much, but from what I do know, their financial situation is pretty good.

My cousin is only one month younger than me, has a technical school diploma, and can repair elevators. Last year while working at an elevator company, he happened to be assigned to maintain the elevators in their neighborhood. He was in his element, playing games at home all day. In the second half of the year he quit and was sent by my uncle to a construction site in Xichang to work. The site never started, so he just played around in Xichang for two months before returning to Chengdu.

At this age where he's neither here nor there, having no job doesn't matter since the family is well-off. But at least he should have a girlfriend, right? Spending all day online—what kind of situation is that? Every Spring Festival, blind date season blooms everywhere, and my aunt is one of the main players. As long as she hears about some family's daughter still single, she drags my cousin off to a blind date. That day I was also at my grandmother's house. In the afternoon, my cousin called back and told my uncle to hurry up and clean the house—the girl's family was coming to see it. Our rural houses here are all brick houses, pretty much the same. You can guess what they look like with your eyes closed. And if they were going to look at a house, it should be the one in Chengdu, but they still wanted to come, which meant this blind date carried a hint of engagement. I've rarely seen my uncle so nervous. After the call, he grabbed a broom and started cleaning while directing others to do other things. It felt a bit like welcoming a bride. The whole room teased him. My mom couldn't stand it and went to help clean too.

I really thought this was an engagement, or maybe rural people are just straightforward. Besides, by relation we're distant enough that even if the deal doesn't go through, the courtesy remains. That evening, two tables of people played drinking games, ate and drank heartily. I drank quite a bit and even called that girl "sister-in-law"—only to be reminded afterward that I shouldn't call her that yet. Young people always have too much enthusiasm. After dinner we still weren't satisfied, so we took the girl and drove over the asphalt road damaged by heavy trucks from highway construction to the KTV in town to sing. My cousin isn't good at expressing himself—his emotions seem to wait for others to take charge. Sitting with this girl, I was really anxious for him. Meanwhile, others chatted idly, and we only left after finishing two cases of beer.

When the whole group returned to the old house, there wasn't enough space to sleep. My cousin and I shared a bed. I asked how he felt about this girl. He said: "She's short." I found it strange that up to that point, no one had commented on the girl's appearance. I guessed it was 50% because my cousin isn't particularly handsome either, so he had no right to judge others' looks.

The next morning I left. Later I heard the girl came to Chengdu with my cousin to play for a few days, but my aunt gradually worried that the girl's height might affect future children's genes. Hearing there was another promising girl, she sent this one back home and prepared for the next round of blind dates.

Last night, my aunt called to say this girl came to Chengdu again—this time at her father's instruction.

Hahahahahahahahaha...

Suddenly I feel love is so fucking complicated. At first meeting, they size each other up by conditions. After meeting, you can no longer tell what's true and what's false behind it all. This old monk is foolish and can't understand this play.

posts.title: My Childhood Herding Cattle
posts.excerpt: I bought *China in a Village* for two reasons: first, it was written by Xiong Peiyun, and second, because it writes about rural China. I believe few urban people are interested in this subject. My int...
posts.content:
I bought *China in a Village* for two reasons: first, it was written by Xiong Peiyun, and second, because it writes about rural China. I believe few urban people are interested in this subject. My interest mostly comes from my own noble rural bloodline. Yes, this identity is very noble. I wish I could stand alone in the lush green rice paddies, tilting my head to listen to the rumbling agricultural heavy metal blasting from an eight-star, eight-diamond phone. What pretentious pride—I have my own land, and I had a childhood herding cattle across the mountains and fields.

After so much time has passed, I only remember one incident of herding cattle. Our hometown area is hilly, and farming mostly requires animal labor, so cattle hold a high status in rural areas. Though not revered as gods like in India, at least they're not treated the same as pigs and dogs—you can tell just by comparing prices. Because cattle are expensive, rural families usually pool money to buy one and take turns feeding it. On the appointed day, you have to go to someone else's cowshed to bring the cow to your own. You feed it grass and water on time, and of course give it daily exercise. Since ancient times, herding cattle has been children's work. In poetry, there are misty rains and apricot blossoms, shepherd boys with short flutes. But in modern Southwest rural China, forget misty rains and apricot blossoms—even people who can play the flute are nowhere to be found. In the 1990s, adults were busy earning work points to offset the government's high agricultural taxes and levies. Who had time to teach children to mess with this thing that couldn't fill their bellies? The two teachers at our mountain village elementary school didn't know how either—they only taught Chinese and math.

One day after school—whether it was math or Chinese all afternoon—I came home and Grandma told me to go to the mountaintop across the way to herd cattle. She said I could only come back after the cow was full. So a few kids and I took the cow up the mountain. Somehow, they wanted to take the cow back home early. Damn, I had just brought the cow up and now they wanted to take it back. How was I supposed to explain if the cow wasn't full? I had no choice but to stay alone on the mountaintop feeding the cow. I didn't have a watch, and time seemed to be playing tricks on me. Night fell from the west like a blanket. Judging by the light, the cow should be full, so I started leading it down the eastern side of the mountain toward home. The sun was setting, and the eastern side of the mountain had no peak light. The further down I went, the darker it became. The rustling sounds on the ground grew clearer, making my hair stand on end. I quickened my pace, wanting to get home and escape this vague fear. Then the cow started messing with me. It got stubborn and refused to move. I was anxious—it was almost completely dark, and being alone was scary. I pulled with all my strength, but back then I wasn't as tall as the cow, and no matter how hard I tried, it pulled me backward. I gave up struggling and stopped pulling. Holding the rope, I stood halfway up the mountain and desperately called for Grandma to come help. I shouted for a long time with no answer—probably they hadn't come home yet and were on another mountain, so they couldn't hear me. But night had come, and I couldn't overcome that desperate helplessness and fear of the dark. I was afraid of being eaten by ghosts, and only by shouting loudly could I feel my own existence. With each shout, I grew more terrified and cried harder. In desperate situations, time stretches endlessly. After a long while, my grandma finally heard my calls and answered from far away. Only then did I feel I had found my direction.

This childhood incident left such a deep impression that I can never forget it. Later, thinking back, I believe what terrified me most at the time was my own crying. The more miserably I cried, the more frightened I felt. If I hadn't cried, maybe the situation wouldn't have been so bad.

posts.title: Us Under New Technological Progress
posts.excerpt: Two years ago, my phone was a MOTO Z3 with a 20-yuan Dynamic Zone plan—only 30MB of data per month. By the end of the month, I usually had over ten MB left and felt very satisfied. In 2010, I switched...
posts.content:
Two years ago, my phone was a MOTO Z3 with a 20-yuan Dynamic Zone plan—only 30MB of data per month. By the end of the month, I usually had over ten MB left and felt very satisfied. In 2010, I switched to a Windows Mobile phone. The screen got bigger and pictures were clearer, but 30MB wasn't enough, so I spent another 10 yuan to upgrade to 100MB per month. I still had data left each month and felt quite content. Later I switched to a China Unicom 3G number, and my monthly data jumped to 300MB. Although the phone was still running WM, the experience changed because the base conditions were different—I could do more things, and 300MB barely lasted the month. Now, although I haven't changed phones, I flashed it to Android. It's fast, smooth, and has rich apps. Compared to Android, WM feels like an aging ox, so I completely gave up the idea of flashing back to the original system. However, after using it for two consecutive months, I exceeded my data limit every month. I feel that under new technological progress, we keep becoming slaves to technology—whether actively or passively. Our desires are controlled by the operators. The more data they give you, the more you can experience. But looking back, we used to be more satisfied. So from time to time, I miss the era of color screen phones, even black-and-white screens. Back then, phones were purely for chatting and communication. We only made calls when necessary, unlike today when we silently stare at screens whether eating, walking, or sleeping. Admittedly, there are many more ways to communicate now—Weibo, WeChat, QQ—but are we happier than in the black-and-white era? I don't think so. Phone apps have turned everyone into a broadcast station. Every moment we want to say something, but very few people are tuned to the same frequency. Yet we'd rather let information flood than admit our loneliness. We keep posting updates for people we've never met, but we still won't lift our heads to talk to the person across the dinner table. Eating alone really is lonely.

Technological progress is never a bad thing, but we must be careful not to lose ourselves in it. The British TV series *Black Mirror*, though only three episodes, each feels like a technological parable. I especially like the third episode because I once fantasized about Sheldon's high-definition memory implant—storing every formula and scene I see in my brain so I wouldn't need to read or think. I could just retrieve knowledge when needed. But when I saw the male lead digging out the implanted storage device from his body, my unrealistic fantasy was shattered. See, foolish humans—technology without moral distinction brings convenience but also side effects. We don't need to remember everything we see and hear. Some illusions and lies won't destroy life; on the contrary, they help things go smoothly.

If I had to choose one point as humanity's eternal state, I would definitely choose now. I'm also not interested in humanity's technological level fifty or sixty years from now. All of this is because I live in the present with an unknowable future. I just want to live well in the now and not be troubled by technology.

posts.title: Life
posts.excerpt: Sometimes when I see someone walking listlessly down the road, I tend to treat their current state as eternal and assume they'll be this listless for their whole life. Then, sentimentally, I lament th...
posts.content:
Sometimes when I see someone walking listlessly down the road, I tend to treat their current state as eternal and assume they'll be this listless for their whole life. Then, sentimentally, I lament the meaning of their decades of existence—as if they're just an empty shell.

A few nights ago while eating with some girls, I probably said that once people enter society, many live only for houses, cars, and girls. One night after working overtime, drinking slowly with my boss, I repeated this sentence. It's not philosophy, but it's the truth.

Forgive me for seeing life so bleakly. As a veteran mover, when delivering goods to clients, I can't help feeling sad. It seems a person's whole life is just constantly doing things for others, who then do things for still others, cycling on and on. We stand in the middle as movers. Isn't humanity's endless labor just turning entropy to 0.5? Green Day has a lyric: "When masturbation's lost its fun, you're fucking lazy." When even jerking off loses its pleasure, you're truly fucking bored. When entropy reaches 0.5, humanity is truly fucking bored.

I hate boredom, and I also hate being constantly busy. Maybe I can only slowly work toward houses, cars, and girls.

Life is meaningless. Time to drink.

posts.title: Master, Slow Down
posts.excerpt: The bus driver stepped a little harder on the gas, and the world immediately became ugly. I would have preferred sitting on a slow-moving bus, watching the people and scenery outside gradually recede....
posts.content:
The bus driver stepped a little harder on the gas, and the world immediately became ugly.

I would have preferred sitting on a slow-moving bus, watching the people and scenery outside gradually recede.

Ideal life should be like this—I don't want to be overwhelmed.

But the one driving isn't me.

The company's little sister often doesn't bring money and can't eat lunch.

After meals, I have no choice but to treat her.

She says, "I'm so sorry, you're always treating."

Heh.

Internally roaring.

MLGB!!!

At first I thought if I treat you once, you'd at least treat me back once. But every meal ends up being on me.

Actually I'm also short on money, but still have to pretend to be generous. Bitter smile.

—You just can't bear to watch others go hungry in front of you.

It must be because summer has arrived.

Feeling a bit restless inside.

Eyes can't help but wander.

Accidentally saw something.

Sin, sin.

Sister, wearing such an ultra-short skirt and sitting so MAN-like...

Sometimes I'm also cheap.

Knowing there's nothing to see, I still look.

You can't dig my eyeballs out, can you?

I said, I've already started feeling bored. Using a 17-inch screen for design is an insult to this industry.

Can't take it anymore, want to change jobs.

Change to what?

I want to work in front of a 27-inch monitor.

Pfft~~ That's just hope, not reality.

Seeing my own designs being happily played with by others—

a bit of a sense of accomplishment.

Sudden realization: the purpose of design is to make clients ignore the design.

Clearly doing things without edges or corners,

but getting better and better at talking.

Like usual, a brick flew past.

Probably because the city is too clean—dirt is nowhere to be seen.

And the phone's airplane mode can only be used once.

In high school, the girl who sat behind me always liked to write on my back.

Guess what she wrote?

Don't know.

I'll write one more, guess again. Your back is so fleshy, it feels good to write on.

Actually I enjoyed it too, like a massage. Class was boring anyway.

Later we were separated into different classes. Didn't speak for five or six years.

Last week, my classmate said I can design websites and gave her my QQ.

Ding ding ding...

It's you! Asking if I still remember her?

Of course I do—the girl who wrote on my back.

"Hehe... I forgot."

...

Ten thousand whys ask me one question,

then I Google one question.

Then I become an encyclopedia.

Everyone knows how we should live our lives,

but I've never worked toward that direction.

posts.title: The Road of Life is Long, Cherish It While Walking
posts.excerpt: I moved out of school and am truly facing life. I've never had such a clear sense of severance before. For many years, no matter how things changed, I still had to obediently return to the classroom t...
posts.content:
I moved out of school and am truly facing life. I've never had such a clear sense of severance before. For many years, no matter how things changed, I still had to obediently return to the classroom to listen to courses I had no interest in. But this time, I felt like I was hanging from a beam, someone swung a knife to cut the rope, a flash of white light passed, and I fell into an unfathomable black hole. I wasn't scared by the unknown—maybe there's a pool below, or it's filled with soft fallen leaves, or it might even pierce through the planet and drop me under another blue sky. So I was too lazy to even let out an "ah" like bungee jumping, and forgot to experience the feeling of weightlessness.

I've been working for three months. At first it was a bit tiring—there was so much I didn't understand, and I relied on Google for everything. As the things I didn't know were resolved one by one, I gradually forgot my troubles and began adapting to the 9-to-6 work routine. We're a small company, so I can leave at 8:40 and being late is common. Recently I've been wearing shorts and flip-flops to work every day. This is what life is supposed to be, damn it.

But human laziness is deeply rooted—once adapted to an environment, people are reluctant to move. I've always been wary of stable, long-term jobs, worried about hanging myself on one tree and losing the whole forest. The other day while watching *Floating and Sinking*, the little driver explained to Wang Guilin why everyone wants to go to Shanghai: "In Jiangzhou, at twenty you can already see what you'll look like at eighty." I've said similar things. When a classmate went to Jinchuan County as a civil servant, I told him I could already see what he'd look like in the next few decades. I bet that around fifty or sixty, he'd run to Chengdu to buy a house and settle down—because that's basically what civil servants from Tibetan areas do. If they don't get promoted, after working half their lives, they quietly retire in that city where they have few connections until they die.

I once solemnly told Juan: "Don't ever take the civil service exam—one foot in the door and it's as deep as the sea." Recently she said she wanted to take the exam again. I didn't try to dissuade her this time—instead I supported it. Who says everyone likes a life of ups and downs? Time flows like water; what matters is mindset. But I definitely won't go. I don't even want to see what I'll be like in five years. Speaking of which, poor Doraemon—drilling into a drawer and seeing his adult self. What's the point of struggling in life then?

Last night while drinking with Lei Ge, he said no matter where he is, after staying five years, he plans to buy a house there. So now he hopes to have a down payment within five years. I was embarrassed to tell him face-to-face that he thinks economics too easy and life too boring. If it were me, I wouldn't consider this issue within ten years—I'd rather buy a car that can take me to see all kinds of scenery. Recently, my father discussed buying a house with me, saying at my age I should think about these things. Being from the working class, I'm a bit worried about how many years of freedom it would cost to scatter a fortune. I haven't lived according to my own wishes yet, so I told him: take it slow, no rush. I don't know what decision they'll make after I go back.

I have my own ideas. Anyway, I don't want to be a rural migrant worker for long, and I don't want to put down roots in the city for now. I'll make this clear to my dad when I go back.

posts.title: An Ordinary Day
posts.excerpt: A rare weekend. The day started like this. My phone was out of battery in the morning, so I didn't know what time it was. Bright light was streaming into the room through the not-quite-closed curtains...
posts.content:
A rare weekend. The day started like this. My phone was out of battery in the morning, so I didn't know what time it was. Bright light was streaming into the room through the not-quite-closed curtains—this was intentional, hoping the daylight would wake me. But I didn't turn off the computer until 1 AM last night, and today I wanted to sleep in, so I turned on the computer: 9:17. Not as hoped, so I tried to sleep more. This morning I didn't want to sleep too deeply. I wanted music to accompany me. Remembering the pleasant experience last time, I turned on Pandora Radio again, chose the Mozart channel, then lay back on the bed. I don't know what kind of "Like" and "Unlike" would make the Mozart station play only Bandari-style light music, but anyway, in my hazy consciousness there were only lake views, mountain colors, and birds singing on branches—I didn't hear a single classical symphony. Later I fell asleep. I only woke up when Lei Ge called me at the door—it was already 10:40.

Today only one person went to work, leaving three people in the room. Around noon, Wei Ge's high school classmate arrived, nicknamed "Lei Bi." We've eaten together several times. He seems extremely unreliable but has excellent eloquence. When five or six people eat together, he can talk non-stop from the moment he sits down until everyone leaves, without needing any response from others. His witty banter and scolding form their own style. Thanks to this skill, he's dated quite a few girls—not counting one-night stands, he had at least a dozen girlfriends during university, and naturally spent quite a bit of money. In his sophomore year, he borrowed someone else's student ID to get over fifty telecom phones, then sold them for 500 yuan each. This year telecom started auditing accounts, and it's about to reach him—a loss of tens of thousands of yuan, and he can't pay it back. So he's been uneasy, worried someone will report him for fraud. But worry is worry, and he still lives carefree, spending money like flowing water. Today, as expected, he came to mooch food. Wei Ge asked me what to eat for lunch. Since I'm so familiar with Lei Bi, I skipped the "friend from afar" formalities: "Too much trouble, noodles it is!" So Wei Ge cooked two huge pots of noodles at noon, plus last night's leftovers. We ate quite well. After eating, Lei Bi left, but not before arguing with Wei Ge about ten yuan. Who would make a special trip just for ten yuan?

In this era, some people, apart from necessary activities they force themselves to do, only have the internet to pass time. But after playing for a long time, they also get disgusted. Some people's artistic nature comes from boredom. Thinking there was still a book I hadn't finished, I couldn't help but get artistic. But conditions limit that lazy afternoon dream—brewing a cup of light tea, leaning by the window quietly reading—that scene can go to hell. Poor bastards like me only deserve to lie on a straw mat with a cheap Kindle reading an out-of-copyright *The Brothers Karamazov*. After years of practice, I've determined that reading in bed has only one effect: it makes you extremely sleepy, better and stronger than sleeping pills. Before long, drowsiness came like mountains collapsing and seas roaring. I put down the Kindle and slept.

I didn't get up until past 5 PM. There was still something I forgot to do—I needed to go to the JD.com pickup point to return something, but time didn't allow. The few guys living together have all cooked and made meals, but I'm the only one who can't do anything, though I've accumulated rich dishwashing experience. Doing one thing for too long makes both body and mind tired. I got tired of my own role. To become a creator, tonight I'm going to cook! And to gloriously complete this qualitative-to-quantitative transformation, I installed the "Xiachufang" (下厨房, "Down to the Kitchen") app to handle possible challenges. The slogan "Practice is the sole criterion for testing truth" has been shouted for decades. When it actually needs to be applied, you suddenly realize that leaders are the sole truth of practice. Wei Ge wanted to be my military advisor. I said when stir-frying meat, add Pixian bean paste. But he directed this and that, and only after the dish was almost done did I add some bean paste to the pot. After that, the color of the chives didn't look good, but fortunately the taste wasn't much different. They said as long as it was edible. Sparrows don't know the ambitions of swans—my goal is commercial-grade cuisine.

At 10 PM, I turned off the computer to read. After midnight, I turned it back on, took a shower, then came back to the computer to record these things.

posts.title: The Sound of Rain at Two in the Morning
posts.excerpt: The house I live in is inside the Third Middle School area. To be precise, it's rented by my classmates. Because I was too busy with work to find a place, I moved in with them without really knowing w...
posts.content:
The house I live in is inside the Third Middle School area. To be precise, it's rented by my classmates. Because I was too busy with work to find a place, I moved in with them without really knowing what it was like. Before arriving, I had fantasies—floor-to-ceiling windows, good lighting, a large balcony, and so on. It wasn't until the day I moved in that I woke up to how real life actually is. There's no chance of lazily brewing tea on a flower-filled balcony while quietly reading a book. First of all, the entire apartment has no balcony, not enough flowers, let alone a comfortable chair. My bedroom only has a computer desk. In my ideal home, there should be a 140cm wide, 80cm deep, about 72cm high Manchurian ash desk with a laptop on it, a few books casually placed beside it, and a plant I've been growing for two years. I should be sitting at the desk seriously flipping through two books... Even though this ideal doesn't exist, I've accepted the current situation. I can't strongly protest to anyone that life shouldn't be this crude, because I could have chosen differently but gave up the opportunity. If there's still dissatisfaction, it must be that my brain is playing tricks on me.

An elderly couple lives downstairs. They seem to specialize in collecting scrap. They often pile several bags of waste in the corner downstairs, and whenever heavy rain comes, they always cover the scrap with tarps. Sometimes when I come back from work, their door is open, and the room inside is cluttered with all sorts of things. A 21-inch TV sitting on a small box is constantly playing news programs. In many households, TV sound exists as background noise, just like how a sports meet can't go without the Radetzky March. No matter how major the national political news is, pragmatic Chinese people can completely ignore it. For the elderly, besides serving as household background music, news programs might also remind them that this is a real world.

A few days ago, when I came back from hiking, I happened to run into the old man downstairs sorting through junk. I told him we probably had dozens of plastic bottles upstairs and asked if he wanted them—I'd give them all to him. The reason I offered wasn't out of pity, but because some time ago I heard someone downstairs shouting "Scrap collection~". I leaned out the window and asked if he collected dozens of plastic bottles. He very straightforwardly said, "No!" I felt my confidence was hit. In this era that emphasizes economic efficiency, even scrap collectors aren't as diligent as before. Indeed, times are progressing, and every industry is undergoing earth-shaking changes. If you're not careful, your economic mindset falls behind. How inspiring. Since I couldn't get rid of those bottles, the first thing I thought of was giving them to the couple downstairs—do unto others as you would have them do unto you. When the old man heard I wanted to give him the bottles, he immediately pulled out a snakeskin bag and followed me up to the third floor. He stuffed the bottles into the bag one by one. Some still had unfinished purified water inside. He quickly unscrewed the caps. Before I could tell him to pour the water in the toilet, he had already stuck his hand out the door and poured the water gurgling into the stairwell. I didn't say much. I just watched him pour another large bottle of water in the hallway—"It'll evaporate," he said. Luckily there was no sweet and sour juice inside. Maybe he considered that water evaporates, but he didn't think someone might slip on the puddle or dirty their shoes and track it onto the floor. He seems like a rather ill-tempered old man. He said this apartment only cost 120,000 yuan in 2007. He thought it was too expensive so he didn't buy it. Our landlord bought it, and now it's worth over 300,000 yuan. He feels it's a pity. I've heard similar stories more than once. Betting on housing prices is gambling with the government—who can guarantee they'll always win? If you had over 300,000 yuan in hand right now, would you still dare to place a bet? I really wanted to tell that old man, "Stop complaining, just focus on collecting garbage! And remember not to pour water in people's hallways." In the end, I only said to him, "Alright, take care! In a couple of days I'll have more for you!"

An old lady who sells things lives upstairs. Sometimes we buy beer from her. We haven't interacted much, but a few days ago when we ran out of electricity, she passed by and gave me tips on buying electricity—always keep a few hundred yuan worth of credit on your electricity card for emergencies. This is the art of her living, I guess. I suspect she has at least two children.

Speaking of no electricity, it's indeed a very annoying thing. We delegated the responsibility of buying electricity entirely to Weige, but Weige lives in another time zone—we have a time difference. Every morning when I wake up to wash, Weige is about to go to bed. When I get off work in the evening, Weige gets up. But the electricity department uses Beijing time, so our refrigerator hasn't been running for almost a week. The ice melted into water all over the floor, and the water turned into steam spreading throughout the entire room...

If young people say their days are "boring," it naturally means they've been online until their eyes hurt, their phone data is used up, and Hunan TV is running ads. So during this period without electricity, I was very "bored," but actually lived quite fulfillingly. When night fell and the house was pitch black, I lay in bed with nothing to do. Suddenly a bright place appeared in my mind, and it had sufficient 24-hour lighting. After hesitating for a moment, I grabbed the recently purchased *River Town* and went there. On the way, I chatted with Juan'er on my phone for a bit, seeking some comfort so I wouldn't get kicked out by KFC. Sure enough, they didn't kick me out. Was it because I bought 9-yuan black tea that actually cost less than 1 yuan to make? Thinking this way, I suddenly felt righteous— if it weren't for the fact that I could sit here for a while, who would bother spending 9 yuan here to buy a cup of water? Later I told Juan'er, "Actually, I quite enjoy this period of time. Besides work, everything else is very comfortable." I couldn't help but think of a book title—*A Perfect Day for a Single Person*. I don't intend to live forever in a city without friends, but now I have this opportunity. Setting work aside, the rest of my time I can live entirely according to my own wishes. It's much better than being in a city with parents who criticize "this isn't good, that isn't good," or having relatives and friends who trouble you with "help me with this, let's eat there." But no matter how much I like living alone, no matter how much I show how comfortable I am being alone, when the entire city is submerged in the sound of rain at two in the morning, I know—I can't leave them.

posts.title: Staying Awake While Reading
posts.excerpt: Ordinary life most easily devours our thoughts. Every day you handle almost the same things. Clients ask you to make the logo a little bigger, the content a little more eye-catching, the colors a litt...
posts.content:
Ordinary life most easily devours our thoughts.

Every day you handle almost the same things. Clients ask you to make the logo a little bigger, the content a little more eye-catching, the colors a little brighter, the layout a little fuller—it's exactly like some male compatriots' fantasies about women: bigger breasts, sexier clothes, fuller figure. Then you modify according to the client's requirements. The result isn't something you like at all, but the client says it's great. "Not bad, young man, you've worked hard~" "Sure thing~" While silently chanting "Logo bigger! Content eye-catching! Colors brighter!" you painfully complete several more layouts. After doing similar work for several months, you find that your body and mind are completely occupied by work. All day long you're doing things similar to assembly line work—no need to think. Ignorance is strength!

In the end, you always find yourself exhausted. After getting off work and eating dinner, you sit in front of the computer, worrying about the whole country like you're in Zhongnanhai, reviewing the day's Weibo posts, afraid of missing any trace. You browse classmates' and friends' spaces to see how they're doing today. In reality, you haven't seen them for a long time, haven't heard their voices for ages. It seems like you care deeply about the existing world and the friends around you. However, after aimlessly closing the webpage, you can't remember a single thing you saw ten seconds ago, one minute ago, or even ten minutes ago. The feeling of premature aging flashes by, and immediately even this awareness of sorrow disappears without a trace. Even though there's the consolation that people who are good at forgetting always live happily, the uneasy thought never truly leaves. You may not know your dopamine levels, but your own emotions are always clear.

So you indulge in the negative effects that low mood brings you. You clearly know you're unhappy and know what to do, but you just don't execute it immediately. You have to wait until you finally feel bored to the point of collapse before you'll miss the things you used to do, before you'll turn your thoughts into action.

I finally picked up a book again, immersing myself in the author's thoughts, forgetting the temptations of the internet, TV dramas, cars, and women, feeling the author's reasoning. Only after closing the book did I use my own consciousness to make my own judgment on the author's reasoning. Although I couldn't reach high-level conclusions, feeling the difference between good and bad writing was especially satisfying, because during that time I thought, I felt my own existence as a human being.

posts.title: The Last Drink
posts.excerpt: The guys in the neighboring dorm often bring several cases of beer to their room to invite people over for a small gathering. The number of people varies, and the drinking snacks are only peanuts, bro...
posts.content:
The guys in the neighboring dorm often bring several cases of beer to their room to invite people over for a small gathering. The number of people varies, and the drinking snacks are only peanuts, broad beans, and beef jerky—no delicacies to feast on. So these gatherings are mainly for chatting, with eating as secondary. Last night they brought up seven or eight cases of beer again and called everyone who stayed behind to drink together. This dorm gathering was the largest in four years, and also the last one. The next day, several people in the class were going to Guangdong.

After lights-out at eleven, I walked into their dorm. Before going, I specifically put on shorts because they had also called the auntie from our building. Actually, it would have been fine not to wear them—I was often in just my underwear surfing the internet in the dorm when the auntie would run in asking me to find things for her. She was long used to it. But the occasion was different, so wearing pants showed respect.

They had set up five or six rechargeable LED lights in the dorm—not very bright, but the atmosphere was nice. A dozen or so people crowded around two small mahjong tables, which were messily covered with snacks and crookedly placed beer bottles. Their chattering conversation was somewhat like the chaotic noise of a street-side snack shop under dim streetlights.

They had already been chatting for half an hour. When the auntie saw me come in, she made me drink three big gulps of beer as punishment for being late. Fine, fine, fine—no wine glasses, so I'll punish myself with three big gulps. Gulp, gulp, gulp it down, the bottle is half empty. Actually, I'm not very familiar with the auntie. She's a passerby to me, and I'm a passerby to her. But the auntie has a particularly good relationship with several people in the class, so they called her over. Also to thank her for taking care of our dorm over the past two-plus years. The group effect at the drinking table makes any one person's opinion easily accepted. As an individual, it's best to follow their opinions and not act rashly.

Graduation drinking and eating—thousands of words boil down to three themes: reluctant to leave; where to develop; don't forget to help out when you make it big. They like everyone to toast in a circle. I can't avoid the cliché either. But after four years as classmates, there are still some classmates I don't interact with much. Just like in high school, people with similar interests formed their own circles: the flashy ones in one circle, the gamers in another. Originally there wasn't much overlap. At the drinking table, I could only put on a perfunctory show of courtesy. It's not that I dislike them, but there's really nothing to say, so I could only say: "Come on, nothing more to say. Our feelings are all in the wine—bottoms up!" After several rounds of drinks, the polite talk finished, and we started speaking from the heart—no risks involved.

I really admire people who venture out alone into the world. Brother Ping is one of them. He told everyone about the cold shoulders and hardships he encountered going to Shenzhen alone. When he got excited, he slammed a beer bottle onto the table, sending broken peanuts flying. With great heroism, he said, "In the future, half of Shenzhen will be mine! Jobs is nothing! Li Ka-shing is nothing!"

I suddenly understood that some people's exaggerated ambitions are intimidating. Probably from watching too many TV dramas—behind huge ambitions lies either darkness or hardship. I used to desperately want endless money to buy endless things. Later my ambitions gradually faded, perhaps extinguished by reality. I no longer crave having a lot of money. I only ask for enough to get by—contentment brings happiness. I don't seek to prove my abilities to others. As long as I have a job full of fun that earns just enough money and I'm with someone I like, that's enough.

posts.title: National Day Travel Plan
posts.excerpt: While preparing to write this year's National Day travel plan, I tried to recall where I went last National Day. I probably have amnesia, or remembering dates is just a difficult thing. Many past outi...
posts.content:
While preparing to write this year's National Day travel plan, I tried to recall where I went last National Day. I probably have amnesia, or remembering dates is just a difficult thing. Many past outings, besides the story framework that can still be recounted, those precise and detailed scenes have long become blurred. I remember I laughed, I got tired, I felt the release of seeing all the mountains below. But I can't remember which day and with whom I laughed and got tired together. In the end, I had to search the article archives. It turns out last National Day I rode a bike with classmates to Qinghe Waterfall. The photos interspersed in the travelogue made the past vivid again. A bit like an old man, trembling as he takes out a well-preserved photo album, staring at them with tears streaming down his face, as things sealed away for decades gradually become clear again.

I was truly unwilling to accept it. Last National Day, because there were too many people, when facing challenges everyone acted independently. Under the influence of team consciousness, not a single person reached the destination. So not long ago, when deciding on the 2012 travel plan, I straightforwardly told them I wanted to retrace Gesala and called them to come "relive our youth" together. When Liao Lin was celebrating his birthday, I asked who wanted to go. I also asked in the Q group. Until now, no one has said they'd go again.

Later, just last week, a friend asked me where I was going for National Day. I still firmly said I was going to Gesala, going alone with a tent and sleeping bag on a bike to that place I couldn't let go of. She said she didn't know where to go for National Day. Having one or two people accompany you on the journey would be such a wonderful thing. I decisively forgot my oath from a minute ago and immediately asked her to come with me to Tengchong and Shangri-La that popped into my head.

Later she didn't come to Yunnan. I still continued my travel plan. I couldn't extinguish one dream and then pour cold water on another. I resolutely headed to Yunnan. Tengchong, like Dali and Lijiang, is nationally famous. Going there alone during National Day, besides looking at people, there's nothing to see.

I decided to head to Shangri-La, then further west to Deqin to see Meili Snow Mountain. That place won't have the crowds and extortion by unscrupulous merchants like Dali and Lijiang. Rather than saying I'm going to see scenery, I'd rather believe I'm going on a pilgrimage. In the movie *Riding the Mountains*, there's a master riding along Route 214 to Lhasa who said he comes here every year, always hoping to see the sun illuminating the golden mountain, because it's said that those who see it will have good luck for a year. Unfortunately, accompanying Zhang Shuhao on his bike ride, we still didn't see it. He was so angry he jumped and stomped on the mountain. I want to go see the sun illuminating the golden mountain. I like the vigorous momentum it brings—the sense of existence that makes one feel small in the world.

At the foot of the snow mountain there's also a beautiful village, very suitable for and only accessible by hiking. Going in and out once takes about three days. I looked at photos of this village. It's not as beautiful as I imagined, but I feel that going there once, the feeling of being cut off from the world isn't something you can experience just anywhere.

For this trip, I bought some equipment online: tent, backpack, sleeping bag, moisture-proof mat, trekking poles, flashlight—these alone cost 800 yuan. Additional equipment needed includes a survival knife, windbreaker pants, first aid medicine. My past one-way transportation fee was 130, and the Meili Snow Mountain ticket package was over 200. I estimate this trip will cost close to 2K.

While I'm still young—no mortgage, no car, no wife, parents don't need support—go out and explore. Otherwise when I'm old, I won't have the chance.

posts.title: Journey to Western Yunnan: Deqin
posts.excerpt: **D3: Feilai Temple — Yubeng Village** Last night I stayed in a room with a couple who came from Shenzhen. This room had three beds, each at 100 yuan. Although I had already prepared myself not to see...
posts.content:
**D3: Feilai Temple — Yubeng Village**

Last night I stayed in a room with a couple who came from Shenzhen. This room had three beds, each at 100 yuan.

Although I had already prepared myself not to see the sun illuminating the golden mountain, when it got noisy outside past seven in the morning, holding onto a sliver of hope, I still quickly jumped up. Without even putting on my jacket, I walked to the guesthouse balcony to check the snow mountain situation. Obviously, as always, disappointment.

The accommodation conditions at Feilai Temple are very poor. All along the way it's 100 yuan a night, but only Feilai Temple made me feel like I was back at my rural hometown. But that's not wrong either—Feilai Temple's administrative name is "Feilai Temple Villager Group, Jushui Village Committee, Shengping Town, Deqin County, Diqing Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture." It's originally just a small village. Usually not many people come here. Only during National Day do accommodation prices here rise by ten yuan every half hour.

I went to the viewing platform for a walk. The railing area was crowded with tourists who came early to try their luck. But Meili Snow Mountain didn't give face—the clouds and mist quietly stationed on the mountainside without any courtesy, as if they had never left. I hoped a gust of wind would come from the north and blow away these unreasonable water vapors! Roll them off to sunny Tengchong!

Since the thirteen peaks of Meili rejected me from a thousand miles away, I didn't stay long. I found a car to go directly to Xidang Hot Springs to hike to Yubeng.

There are two routes to Yubeng: one from Ninong along the Lancang River, and one from Xidang Hot Springs. The minibus from Feilai Temple to Xidang Hot Springs isn't far—the village is visible from Feilai Temple at a glance. However, the car winds around the mountain for 2 hours, and the fare is 20 yuan. I thought only private car bosses would drive while making phone calls, but I didn't expect the bus driver to do the same. On the cliffs on both sides of the unfathomably deep Lancang River Grand Canyon, they can steer with one hand while dialing numbers with the other. Besides reverence, I was also nervous. They drive on this road every day, transporting tens of thousands of passengers. May that low-probability event never happen to me.

It started drizzling when entering Yubeng. Luckily, the Shenzhen couple who shared the room with me last night lent me a plastic raincoat. They originally wanted to go to Yubeng too, but afraid there wasn't enough time, they changed to Mingyong Glacier instead. Seeing I didn't have rain gear, they gave me theirs. It was a very thin one, but a thousand times better than having none.

The only way to Yubeng is by hiking or riding horses. A considerable portion of tourists get off at Xidang Hot Springs and ride horses straight there. The horses' path and the hikers' path are the same road. The muddy surface is mixed with horse urine and horse manure. I told my companions that this road must be great for growing wheat.

I don't want to elaborate on the hardships along the way. When you're carrying twenty to thirty pounds of gear on your back, walking for six hours on muddy rainy roads, you'll experience that kind of hardship where you feel like you're about to collapse but can only keep going forward. Along the way, you'll also meet many impressive people—wearing flat shoes, breaking off a tree branch, carrying no gear at all, still going into Yubeng. But later they all fell behind the hikers wearing hiking shoes.

It took me about six hours to reach Upper Yubeng. My clothes were almost completely soaked—half of it was probably sweat. The weather upon arriving at Upper Yubeng was still overcast. The snow mountain peak above showed no trace. Quite boring.

**D4: Sacred Waterfall**

Also due to time constraints, I gave up the trip to Ice Lake and went to the Sacred Waterfall with a few girls from Guizhou. The Sacred Waterfall is a waterfall formed by snow mountain meltwater. From Lower Yubeng, you can reach it by following the small stream formed by many waterfalls. Both sides of the road are dense primeval forest. Some fallen thick tree trunks are covered with fresh green moss, and some trunks are also dotted with mushrooms of various shapes. I hoped to encounter a living matsutake here, but until I left Deqin, I never saw a single one.

**D5: Deqin — Shangri-La**

Departed from Yubeng at 7, arrived at Xidang Hot Springs around 12. Paid 25 yuan to get to Feilai Temple, then hitched a ride from Feilai Temple to Shangri-La. The driver who picked us up was a Tibetan uncle. His Mandarin wasn't good. I sat in the passenger seat. He often muttered for a long time and I couldn't understand what he was saying. It wasn't until we passed Baima Snow Mountain that I understood he was chanting: "Baima Snow Mountain &%#@\*#&......" Because every time we crossed a pass, he would chant roughly the same content. It must be out of respect for the great mountain, praying for it to protect the safety of everyone in the car.

We arrived in Shangri-La county town around 11 PM. A student from Dali College and I found a standard room in the old town to stay in. Each person spent 90 yuan. Then I thoroughly washed up and fell into a deep sleep. Since the 1st when I came out, I hadn't slept in such a warm quilt.

posts.title: Now
posts.excerpt: Now I'm 22, almost 23. I don't know what my grandfather and father were doing at this age. I guess at that time Grandpa was eating from the big pot with production team villagers, smashing pots and ma...
posts.content:
Now I'm 22, almost 23. I don't know what my grandfather and father were doing at this age. I guess at that time Grandpa was eating from the big pot with production team villagers, smashing pots and making steel to catch up with Britain and surpass America, and was courting Grandma. A little over a year later, my dad was born. When my dad was twenty-three, it was already many years into the reform and opening up. Society was diverse, population was flowing everywhere. After my dad graduated junior high at sixteen or seventeen, he followed a carpenter from the village and became a carpenter, going around repairing houses for people. Later he fell out with Grandpa and went to Yunnan to do carpentry work during the "up to the mountains and down to the countryside" movement. The reason seems to be that Grandpa didn't approve of him dating another girl from the village. Later he went from Yunnan to Guizhou. Even now he often mentions drinking with Miao village people in Guizhou. He said Miao people drink boldly. To show respect, they drink from evening until dawn, one vat of rice wine after another. In the end, he drank all the Miao hosts under the table. It took them several days to recover, so those people greatly admired him. Without a doubt, this was the most glorious drinking occasion of his life. People like to show off their most glorious deeds. He rarely mentions the Yunnan stories. Only when I went to Kunming earlier did he mention a few place names in Kunming, like near the University of Science and Technology. I went there and felt like I was following in his footsteps from back then. Later he returned to Sichuan. The villagers introduced him to a matchmaker, and he met my mom. Then there was me.

But I haven't reached 23 yet, so at 22, I'm working. People at this age must go to work, otherwise others will think there's something wrong with them. But now I don't want to work anymore. That's the truth. If someone brags that they like going to work, we'd also think there's something wrong with their brain. Liking something and having to do something often can't both be satisfied. We try to choose things we don't hate. In a few days I'll resign and return to Chengdu, because I don't hate Chengdu. What can I do when I get back? I haven't decided yet. Some people always set themselves two paths. When it comes time to make a choice, they say they've reached "a crossroads in life," as if one side is hell and the other is heaven. One wrong step and you're doomed forever. I try to avoid this situation, so I've always stayed honest—don't steal, don't rob, don't traffic drugs. There's no possibility of a dark gun barrel being pressed against my head. Besides this extreme situation, I think there are no more "crossroads."

I have many choices. Continue doing advertising I don't like (advertising isn't design, it's just providing cheap goods to customers. As long as the client is satisfied, it's fine. Design should be led by designers using a series of plans on the client's products). I can also switch to home renovation design. I also have ideas about engaging in website design and development. Although I haven't touched the latter two, I always believe I have the ability to master them—it's all about perseverance and interest.

Right now I'm at the advertising company, sitting next to the intern guiding him. Indeed, fresh graduates don't know anything, just like I was back then. It makes me think about society's attitude toward young people. Those uncles and aunties can all look down on us for having no work experience, but they absolutely shouldn't reject us because of no work experience. People will eventually die. If you die, there'll be no one to inherit your work. How regrettable that would be.

(Work was boring. The entire article was written on a mobile client.)

posts.title: Second Job
posts.excerpt: In November I interviewed at five companies. In the end, I still ended up doing work similar to my first job. This reminded me of someone who said you must be careful choosing your first job, otherwis...
posts.content:
In November I interviewed at five companies. In the end, I still ended up doing work similar to my first job. This reminded me of someone who said you must be careful choosing your first job, otherwise you'll be stuck in this circle for life. Of the five companies I interviewed at, three were graphic design related. Two companies gave me job offers, but only this current gold investment company made me a formal employee. However, the workload is much less than at the previous advertising company. At the advertising company, I had to complete several drafts a day. I felt exhausted and there was no salary compensation, so I resigned (I believe the fundamental reason classmates complain about being tired, exhausted, and wanting to resign is that the salary is too low). This current job is unusually relaxed. I can work on one draft for three days. There are only a few drafts a month. Time still passes the same way, work is still done the same way. I only need to provide multiple different proposals for the manager to choose from. Most importantly, I don't have to deal with clients anymore.

I'm still in my probation period. My salary isn't much, but my actual disposable income is more than before. Actually, I should feel ashamed—room and board are all provided by my parents. I considered moving out later, wanting to live closer to the company so I could sleep an extra hour and have more freedom. But our family is quite traditional. I need to consider my parents' opinions.

*Attached: One-week work summary, Design for customer.*

This is my first week at the company. During this week, I roughly understood the company's organizational structure, business types, and met some new colleagues. I've been doing design work at the company for a week. Compared to the previous complicated work, the current work is more organized and directional. The design style is relatively stable, so the current work makes me feel a bit more relaxed. Under this premise, when designing works, I'm more meticulous and rational. Besides viewing from a designer's perspective, I think more from an audience's perspective about the visual experience of the work's information. I believe that when we examine our own works from others' perspectives, our works can be more easily read, and the information the company wants to convey can be more easily spread.

This Monday, I completed the final draft and sent to print the gold savings DM single. This work started being designed from last Thursday when the content was confirmed. Inspired by METRO style, I came up with a general style. When I opened it over the weekend to look, I felt it wasn't reasonable. I thought the content should be distinguished more finely, and the color blocks should be smaller. After all, print materials are different from handheld devices—they don't need finger operation. Making the client immediately understand our promotional purpose is the key point of this DM single. So later I redesigned the DM single, added rounded corners to the blocks, emphasized the "membership recruitment content," and conveyed a much more stable message to the client. On Thursday the finished product came out, and the effect was pretty good.

On Tuesday and Wednesday, Zhao Jia asked me to design business cards for the marketing department. I designed six styles. In the end she said the business cards couldn't differ too much from the original ones, so we still used the original style. However, this business card was canceled before being sent to print. After the company's VI system design is completed, our business cards will probably also be replaced. Boss Cao told me to take it slow and instead design this year's Spring Festival "one gram gold" red envelopes.

Thursday and Friday were mainly spent designing the "one gram gold" red envelopes. Additionally, a greeting card needed to be designed inside the envelope, and on the greeting card, a mechanism that could cleverly hold the gold coin needed to be designed. The mechanism for holding the gold coin couldn't use glue—it had to rely on the paper to stay secure. The design difficulty is quite high (production costs will also increase). It's expected that the entire set of red envelope designs can be completed within the next week.

posts.title: Viewpoints
posts.excerpt: It's now 1:11 AM. In my life there have been countless moments like this, all consisting of "1"s. The most infuriating is undoubtedly pulling out my phone and seeing "11:11." It's purely coincidental,...
posts.content: It's now 1:11 AM. In my life there have been countless moments like this, all consisting of "1"s. The most infuriating is undoubtedly pulling out my phone and seeing "11:11." It's purely coincidental, yet makes one feel ashamed. Two or three years ago this feeling was minimal, but over the past year it's grown stronger. Many people I've met, in various vague and invisible ways, exert influence, making me understand that the wild goose's wings are already full. Whether willing or not, it must fly south. However, liking someone with a mindset of washing away blood shame, or relying on someone purely out of physiological need—this kind of "love" makes one doubtful. Last month my roommate asked to borrow money from me to pay rent. Seeing that his life had deteriorated to the point where he couldn't afford basic necessities, I lent it to him. Yesterday I was also penniless, so I asked him to repay the money. I thought after receiving last month's salary he'd be better off, but unexpectedly he hadn't even paid last month's rent, let alone had money to repay the debt. I was speechless, as if seeing my money become his prostitution money. Before he had a girlfriend, he could pull out several thousand yuan anytime. Now after working for several months, he's actually in debt up to his ears. Love also has great and small distinctions. I only feel regret and a trace of schadenfreude about the messy things between them. A girl said she had been dating her boyfriend for over half a year and never used a single cent of his money. Her approach made me respect her very much. Love is a matter between two people—rights and obligations are equal on both sides. Only seeking to give without seeking return is an idiot. Blindly taking without knowing how to repay is scum.

posts.title: 2012 Year-End Summary
posts.excerpt: This is a belated year-end summary, nearly a week late. These past few days I wanted to collect myself and carefully summarize last year's situation, but laziness prevailed. It wasn't until I felt tha...
posts.content:
This is a belated year-end summary, nearly a week late. These past few days I wanted to collect myself and carefully summarize last year's situation, but laziness prevailed. It wasn't until I felt that if I didn't write it now, I never would, that I respectfully picked up the pen.

At the end of 2012, the world-ending day we had all been eagerly anticipating never arrived after all. Optimists and pessimists alike continued forward, welcoming the arrival of the new year. Not long ago I just celebrated my 23rd birthday. When the page of 2012 turned, I suddenly remembered that we don't grow day by day, but rather approach death day by day. Before I knew it, I had grown another year older. With the slightest inattention, another three hundred-plus days and nights had silently slipped away. Most unacceptable of all: looking back on this year, there were no fateful encounters, nothing unforgettable for a lifetime, nothing colorful. In short, this year was still an ordinary, unremarkable year. However, the reason life is life is that whether the days are turbulent or stagnant, we always live in the present.

I only returned to Chengdu in November last year and found a job to continue living. Yesterday while riding the subway, squeezed among the coming and going crowds, I couldn't read anything from their faces. I probably looked the same. Although I've only been back here for less than two months, I feel like I've already lived here for many years, and will have to live with this same expression for many more years. These kinds of days are truly boring. I pity them, and I pity myself too. I once thought about which direction I wanted to live in the future. However, when I think about how insignificant an individual's will is in the face of all humanity, how an individual's life is but a drop in the ocean in the long river of cosmic history—regardless of how much power, how much money, how many desires one can vent, in the end it all vanishes without a trace. So what was I struggling so desperately for? If even an ant's body can barely survive in this world, then the purpose of human life is still for oneself. To eat one's fill requires labor. To have confidence requires giving. To communicate requires emotion... In short, life is an instinct.

From April to November, life wasn't going as hoped. To survive, I worked at an advertising company doing advertising design, which I had previously thought I liked, but only realized after taking the position that it wasn't the case. This period completely destroyed my long-held design ideal that simplicity is beauty. It also made me recognize even more that design is not the freedom and inspiration one might imagine. In reality, neither are necessary—the client is the boss. For a period after that, I also developed a careless attitude toward the service concept. I believed one shouldn't give clients too much freedom of choice. The more choices you give them, the more likely they are to suffer from "choice paralysis." Sometimes delivering work directly makes them feel more comfortable.

During National Day, I went to Shangri-La and Meili Snow Mountain. I went for pilgrimage, and returned with much purity. I thoroughly fell in love with nature, and once again verified the belief that nature makes people more approachable. Afterward, this experience also became my trump card for the second half of the year. I always tell everyone I meet about the air and human warmth under nature—I wonder if these friends have gotten tired of hearing it. Although I'm now in a big city, I truly hate the air here, the colors here, and everyone's expressionless faces.

Regarding my understanding of life, my ideals for living, and my attitude toward work—I hope in 2013, I can live a bit more delicately. It's late. Good night.

posts.title: Chatting
posts.excerpt: A while ago the company held an event requiring every employee to go out on the streets to find target customers, give them a small gift, and then get their phone numbers. I've always hated this kind ...
posts.content:
A while ago the company held an event requiring every employee to go out on the streets to find target customers, give them a small gift, and then get their phone numbers. I've always hated this kind of marketing method, but I never expected to become one of the participants in this event. However, I'm not a sales employee. I've never received language training. During the script practice, I was always stuttering. So Boss Cao specifically asked Wenwen from HR to give language training to me and another colleague who does network maintenance. She wanted the two of us to read text aloud in the company for half an hour. She believed that after a month of training, we would be able to speak smoothly.

Although sometimes I tend to use wrong words and pause when speaking, I've never believed that I stutter or am timid. Sometimes my brain is in a suspended state, but my mouth keeps working. That's exactly why I easily use wrong words, and then pauses appear. But when my brain recovers function, when I'm interested in the conversation content and it's exactly my area of expertise, I won't stutter. For example, when two or three friends sit together chatting, thoughts diverging freely—at those times I can have clear thinking, speak freely and eloquently, feeling unprecedented ease and pleasure.

My favorite way of chatting is sitting in a rattan chair in a teahouse or coffee shop, in a fair and leisurely posture, slowly opening up heart-to-heart amid the rising mist from fragrant tea or coffee. What I hate most is chatting at dinner parties. When everyone is in high spirits, such chatting is mostly based on joking around—extremely casual and useless. It's like in ancient times using sword dancing to liven things up, and now using bragging to aid digestion. For this very reason, when my friends are pointing out the world at dinner parties, I'm secretly wolfing down food. By the time they've only said five-tenths of what they want to say, my stomach is already very bloated and painful.

Today Tingting, Axin, Axin's male friend, and I were chatting in the music park. This friend asked the three of us why we always talk about life when chatting. I didn't know how to answer him. Indeed, each of us lives our own life. There's no connection in work, no common ground in professional knowledge. Besides talking about life, entertainment, and ideals, I don't know what else we can talk about. Among the classmates and friends I've met, the people who love chatting the most are definitely those selling insurance. Their eloquence makes me look up to them with endless admiration like the flowing Yangtze River: they can first talk about all kinds of sparkling experiences in their work. Two hours later they'll talk about all kinds of awesome stories they heard from chatting with others last time. Two more hours and they'll talk about their own pain and ambitions. The final closing line is always: "Bro, when you make it big, don't forget your brother!" All of the above content is secondary.
