The key is that this speech of theirs must be produced at a dinner table where everyone maintains strange and tired postures. Whenever I encounter this kind of chatting, I can't help but feel an urgent need to urinate.

Chatting, so to speak, is communication. Chatting where thoughts are exchanged and souls mutually inspire can score a full ten points. Chatting that only reports one's own situation and leaves both parties feeling bored can probably only score one point.

posts.title: A Perfect Day for a Single Person: The Second Day After Resigning
posts.excerpt: I'm not talking about a book. From gloomy clouds to shallow grass not reaching the hooves, then to bright sun and blooming flowers full of vitality—the transition of seasons from winter to summer seem...
posts.content:
I'm not talking about a book.

From gloomy clouds to shallow grass not reaching the hooves, then to bright sun and blooming flowers full of vitality—the transition of seasons from winter to summer seems like just an instant, so fast that there's no time to take off thick clothes. I slammed on the brakes of my electric bike at the crossroads, feeling the sting of sunlight on my body.

Let me first talk about what happened a few days ago. It was Wednesday morning this week, not long after starting work. I asked administrative assistant Jiajia for a resignation application form. I thought she wouldn't be surprised, because on Monday I was the only one in the entire company still wearing sloppy casual clothes to work and participating in the company business exam decided by President Wang. This was a sign of resignation. I said to her: "If I can't pass the exam, forget it. Setting the resignation line at 40 points is a bit low. 60 points failing and leaving is best. Also, deduct however much for not wearing work uniform—I don't care!" This scoundrel attitude probably hurt her feelings. I felt sorry, but at that time I was also extremely clear that I really didn't like the rigid work system and any conditions the leaders used resignation as a threat to impose. The work uniform system is a rule in the financial industry—at least I can accept that. But "threatening" is a manifestation of ruthlessness and meanness. No matter what, don't have too high expectations of such people. Even a bit earlier, during last Friday's monthly meeting, I had already considered resigning. It was just that the factors constituting resignation weren't numerous enough yet. I had to weigh resignation versus staying to make the best judgment. On Tuesday I went to interview at the next company. The compensation was slightly better, and the atmosphere of having lunch with them was also good. So I decided that same day. The next day was Wednesday, exactly the day I had completed three months of probation. Wenwen from HR sent me a regularization document. It was quite a joke—Jiajia's resignation application form had just been sent over too. One regularization form, one resignation form...

I printed the resignation application form. While filling it out, the sunlight outside the window, refracted through the glass, covered my desk for the first time—a field of brightness. At that moment, how I wished I had religious faith. Whether revelation or omen, my future should be bright and promising. Many years later, if I succeed, I will miss this magical moment immensely. In the afternoon, I also talked with the general manager for over an hour. I told her all the places where I thought the company's business was unreasonable and the systems unsatisfactory. She is a leader full of personal charm and persuasiveness. She hoped I would stay. But she is a person who thinks emotionally and could never clearly understand what I actually needed at that time: future salary. When I left the company yesterday, I felt very reluctant to part with my colleagues here, especially the girls here.

Back to the moment when I was all hot and bothered at the crossroads. After waiting for the red light, I sped forward. It was just past one in the afternoon. I had just gotten off work at my new company. My younger brother hadn't eaten lunch yet. I was going to take him to eat at Dicos.

Afterward, I fell into weekend boredom again. I didn't want to meet up with my buddies—they're all a boring bunch. Besides singing and eating, they can't come up with any new ways to have fun. Lack of money is the main reason. And I feel that if I keep hanging out with this group of men, none of us can solve the single problem. I want to find a girl to ride bikes with or go read books, chat—anything. But this kind of idea is even harder to realize. Better to just go out wandering alone, ride my bicycle like a cat, along the rural fields. I can also be very comfortable by myself. Sitting on the rocks by the lake, feeling the gentle breeze whispering in my ears, watching the rippling water, watching the quiet uncle fishing, watching couples taking photos of willow trees, watching old men exercising, watching young people playing cards... I feel nothing. I am a free cat, enjoying the good weather. Meow~

posts.title: What to Do After Thirty?
posts.excerpt: Our company has a new regional manager, looks to be in his forties, not very tall, sturdy build, dressed quite casually. No matter from which angle, he's an unremarkable passerby on the street. At lun...
posts.content:
Our company has a new regional manager, looks to be in his forties, not very tall, sturdy build, dressed quite casually. No matter from which angle, he's an unremarkable passerby on the street. At lunch in the company, the general manager asked about his family situation and learned he has no children. Asked further, he doesn't even have a wife. Everyone at the table teased him that nights must be lonely, and the whole table laughed. Only he didn't laugh so casually. He still quietly ate his meal and stopped talking.

After eating, several of us returned to the office together. We waited a long time but the colleague still didn't come to open the door. Everyone had to sit on the stairs waiting. Another colleague and I were both bored playing with our phones, while this manager pulled out a book with a tattered cover, grayish-white pages, and a cover that had been ravaged beyond recognition. After finding a wall corner to squat down, he read with great interest. I was very curious what kind of book this was. Looking at its printing standards and weathered appearance, it completely met the form a pornographic novel should present. But after staring for a long time, it seemed to be just some martial arts novel I'd never heard of.

The way he squatted reading made me think of an impression that has appeared countless times in my mind: indeed there are such people who squat by the roadside all day with nothing to do, reading books. They look very focused, but actually only because they have nothing to do and no one asks them to do anything. Regardless of the possible situation, or that the book he reads isn't very refined, or that the way of reading isn't very civilized, or that he's a middle-aged bachelor—in short, to me, being in your forties like this isn't glorious. I even somewhat subjectively think that being in your forties and still working for others is also an embarrassing life. People in their forties should start their own careers. At noon, they should be discussing business with clients, not at this moment squatting in a corner reading martial arts novels with no distracting thoughts.

The ancients had the teachings of "at thirty one stands firm, at forty one is no longer confused." That's because ancient people's lifespans were generally shorter, so "establishing" one's career had to be earlier. In modern times, everyone lives past seventy or eighty. "Standing firm at forty" is also reasonable. But in the ten years before establishing one's career, what are we doing? Between twenty and thirty is the learning stage—learning professional skills, accumulating social experience, building social circles. After thirty, knowledge is mostly learned, social experience is there, personal network is basically stable. At this stage, our energy is probably half on career, half on family, initially accumulating the original capital needed for entrepreneurship at forty.

Design is a job that feeds on youth. I don't believe I'll still be doing the same thing after thirty. Even if I can, I definitely won't. Doing the same thing for 10 years is a great misfortune in life, a waste of life. Either change careers or direct others to do it. That's what I think.

posts.title: When You See Someone Showing Off, You Get Jealous
posts.excerpt: Just like always, merely seeing someone else's LOMO-style photos showcasing vermilion seal carvings printed on paper triggered my artsy pretentiousness without warning. I couldn't wait to buy a seal c...
posts.content:
Just like always, merely seeing someone else's LOMO-style photos showcasing vermilion seal carvings printed on paper triggered my artsy pretentiousness without warning. I couldn't wait to buy a seal carving knife and ten blocks of Kunlun frozen stone online, intent on making a big splash and carving to my heart's content! After placing the order, I started to second-guess myself—first, because Kunlun frozen stone is softer than Shoushan stone and has fewer patterns than Qingtian stone; second, because buying all this material just to carve my own name seemed wasteful. A while back, my mouse's micro-switch broke, and to fix it I spent dozens of yuan on a soldering iron, only to use it once before tossing it in a drawer. This time, with the seal carving, I naturally hoped to produce some decent results—maybe gift one to a girl I liked if it turned out well. If not, no big deal; if I end up struggling in life and have to set up a street stall engraving seals and forging documents, at least I could brag that I started at 24, already an old hand. Like Lu Xun hiding in a small room rubbing steles before *Call to Arms*—he didn't become famous from the rubbings, but at least he gathered some writing material for his readers.

Looking back, I've always enjoyed doing these futile things. The fundamental goal is to learn some skills for future needs, but unfortunately most of these efforts end up fruitless, which is heartbreaking. In college, I had way too much free time. Later, while tinkering with my blog, a small feature could take a whole day to implement. My energy and mind were completely absorbed in it, and I felt there was nothing more fun than writing code. I desperately wanted to become a programmer, but I severely lacked mathematical and logical thinking, so I reluctantly gave up that unrealistic dream. Then I tried focusing on product design to honor my pre-college aspirations, but the university curriculum had no real substance, and the teachers' levels were uneven, which made me dislike my major. Combined with the grim employment prospects for industrial design, I simply gave up. I even told my counselor directly: "What's the point of studying this? I won't be working in this field anyway!" Around senior year, I decided I needed a practical skill—just like my grandfather who became a stonemason and my father who became a carpenter. With a skill in hand, you won't go hungry. So I took up graphic design: spending time in the library reading design theory, taking the train back to Chengdu to see design biennales, visiting design exhibitions. In the end, my theoretical knowledge felt solid, but I still lacked hands-on skills. I drew a few concept sketches, quickly landed a job at an ad agency, and realized advertising design was nothing like graphic design—I just piled up materials, and clients were delighted. Later, at my third company, my hands-on skills still hadn't improved. A designer whose hand-drawing is terrible is an insult to the profession. Although I've long known I lack drawing ability and planned to relearn it, I never took that real step. I wonder if I'll manage it this year. Drawing is what I most want to pursue—if I can do it well, I'll truly understand the relationships of points, lines, and planes, color combinations, and layout construction. In future work, I won't feel anxious and will enjoy peace of mind.

posts.title: Rain
posts.excerpt: Only on days when you forget your umbrella and watch the drizzle after work, seeing female colleagues magically pull out sun umbrellas and float away, do you feel truly lonely and hopeless. After ling...
posts.content:
Only on days when you forget your umbrella and watch the drizzle after work, seeing female colleagues magically pull out sun umbrellas and float away, do you feel truly lonely and hopeless. After lingering in the lobby for a few minutes, muttering "God helps those who help themselves," I stepped into the rain that seemed like it would never stop. A surge of courage and conviction welled up inside: no one is coming to save you—except yourself.

So I hopped on my bike with no fenders and rode slowly through the rain. Getting soaked was inevitable, so I tried to look graceful and let my dermis enjoy the spring weather. What a lover of life I am, I thought—even in this awful weather, if I fall, I'll do it with style.

The first time I rode in the rain was many years ago in summer. A relative had broken a bone and was in the hospital. His son came from Xichong to Chengdu to take care of him and stayed at my place temporarily. Once, he and I were riding one bike back from the hospital. We were almost home when the weather suddenly changed—dark as night in an instant, strong winds kicking up, dust and plastic bags from the market flying everywhere. Then came the heavy rain, and soon sewage overflowed along the streets while manhole covers bubbled with water. I felt like the whole city was about to be flooded. We hid in the market. He asked, "What now?" "Let's charge through!" I hesitated for a moment, thinking no one was coming to save me, and followed him into the rain.

After that, I never got caught in the rain again. But my deepest impression of rain comes from childhood. When I was in elementary school, I lived with my grandparents in the village. One day we were eating lunch under the eaves when the weather changed abruptly again—no wind this time, just rolling dark clouds turning everything pitch black. Around that time, there were rumors the world was ending, and I thought it really was. Suddenly, lightning struck the mountain right in front of us with the most violent thunder—the rice bowl almost cracked. I said to Grandpa, "It's terrifying! Let's go inside to eat!" He calmly picked up a piece of pickled vegetable, took a bite of rice, and said, "What's there to be afraid of? It's not going to hit us!" So I sat under the eaves, facing the roaring thunder and lightning, calmly finished my bowl of rice, and waited until the downpour arrived.

Thinking about it now, my most artsy period was indeed childhood. Later, I never again had the mood to appreciate lightning, thunder, and splashing rain flowers.

posts.title: Extravagant Hopes
posts.excerpt: Maybe many years from now I'll still remember this passage Wang Xiaobo wrote in *The Golden Age*: "That day I was twenty-one, in the golden age of my life. I had many extravagant hopes. I wanted to lo...
posts.content:
Maybe many years from now I'll still remember this passage Wang Xiaobo wrote in *The Golden Age*:

"That day I was twenty-one, in the golden age of my life. I had many extravagant hopes. I wanted to love, to eat, and in an instant become a cloud half-lit, half-dark in the sky. Only later did I realize that life is a slow process of being hammered. People grow old day by day, and their extravagant hopes fade day by day, until they become like a cow that's been hammered. But on my twenty-first birthday, I didn't foresee this. I thought I would stay fierce forever, that nothing could hammer me."

But before I turned twenty-five, I had already understood this—and the truth was even more disheartening than being hammered. I had never been fierce for even a single day, let alone forever.

Looking back at a photo I took last year beneath the cloud-wreathed Meili Snow Mountain in Yunnan, I was so idealistic then—missing no one, thinking about nothing. Standing beneath the towering snow mountain, I imagined myself as a tree in the primeval forest, standing there for centuries upon centuries, desiring nothing.

Later I entered the city and had to live with my parents. Every little thing required reporting to them, which felt helpless. I began longing to buy a house, which meant I had to find a job that could at least cover the mortgage. Then I feared being trapped by the house and losing my freedom in life, so I didn't even dare mention buying one. Unfortunately, I've never been fierce, so these two extravagant hopes are now entangled within me. In such a huge city with so many people, is this a mistake after all? Why do so many people have to come here? Why are so many willing to be buried in the wilderness on the city's outskirts?

Today a friend asked if I had fully devoted myself to work. I said yes. Actually, for me, there's no such thing as "working hard." I'm willing to take a salary that's just enough, I don't want to be a corporate executive, I don't want to travel too far—like those young Japanese people nowadays who are desireless and undemanding. But reality is always crueler than ideals. Like a hammered cow, you'll never be fierce again. Living on in a barely surviving state is the portrait of a lifetime.

posts.title: Someone With Stories
posts.excerpt: It was only when I seriously wanted to share my own or others' stories with someone that I realized I had no stories. For example, last night at dinner, I wanted to share with colleagues a story from ...
posts.content:
It was only when I seriously wanted to share my own or others' stories with someone that I realized I had no stories. For example, last night at dinner, I wanted to share with colleagues a story from Huang Renyu's *1587, a Year of No Significance* to prove that orthodox history and textbook history aren't the same thing. But after searching my mind three times, I couldn't come up with a single concrete argument. I could only keep repeating, "They're different, really different." It felt so pathetic. Where had all the books I'd read gone? Was my judgment at that moment just a flash of insight from back then—remembering only the conclusion, not the process?

I'm naturally a quiet person. The times I desperately want to share my own stories are few and far between—either when I meet someone exceptional, or when I feel I have to say a few words. The first case is when I encounter people more impressive than myself holding forth; I want to join in to show we're kindred spirits or to compete. The second is in public settings where everyone is sharing stories and experiences, and to blend in I have to smear myself with a bit of mud. Either way, it's tough for someone like me who lives more through introspection than communication. Introspective people draw knowledge, beliefs, and energy from books, films, music, and any other cultural resources, which then form their code of conduct—unlike extroverts who can get fired up and confident after one drinking session or one conversation. Therefore, introspective people differ vastly from extroverts in how they chat. My code of conduct and beliefs come mostly from reading, movies, or the internet. Because of information overload, I've developed the habit of forgetting things right after reading them. So my knowledge system doesn't have a clear story database—vague concepts are what I remember most. For these reasons, I'm not qualified to be a storyteller. I never talk about my own stories or emotions. It's not the arrogance people usually assume.

Now that I know what kind of person I am, the next challenge is how to enrich my story bank. There are basically two ways: remember interesting things from life, draw a conclusion, and share it with friends; or remember friends' interesting stories and share them with others. You could even learn from insurance salespeople and tell other people's stories as your own, but never repeat them.

posts.title: The Method to Becoming Erudite
posts.excerpt: Last year I had two large boxes of books shipped home. The outer boxes were battered and torn, so without sorting them, they were casually thrown into a corner. Later my mom couldn't stand it and quie...
posts.content:
Last year I had two large boxes of books shipped home. The outer boxes were battered and torn, so without sorting them, they were casually thrown into a corner. Later my mom couldn't stand it and quietly organized them for me. I didn't pay attention and left them there without knowing. Then for over half a year I didn't read or buy books, and I even forgot how many books I had. Until last month, when I wanted to lend a colleague two books by Peter Hessler to build some revolutionary friendship through conversation. But after searching at home, I turned one big box of books upside down and still couldn't find them. I wondered if they were lost. So that revolutionary friendship never developed—I ended up lending out one Márquez and one India travelogue instead.

My dad loves tea, and we have all kinds at home. I happen to enjoy tea too, but I never buy any—I just drink whatever's at home. After finishing the aged Pu'er that had developed a moldy medicinal taste, I switched to Anxi Tieguanyin. Once I could taste the subtle bitterness in the Tieguanyin, I happened to find a pack of Emei Silver Needle in the refrigerator's cold storage. It felt dry and cold to the touch—good tea. I couldn't wait to brew a cup and set it beside my computer desk. That day I forgot about the cup's existence and accidentally knocked the tea over onto a box full of miscellaneous items next to the desk. I quickly cleaned it up, only to discover there were still many books inside that box! Thankfully they weren't wet, and *River Town* and *Country Driving* were in there too! Who knows why Mom used two boxes for my books—I thought this one was just junk!

I took the books out and lined them up. The sense of loss that had been haunting me was instantly cured. This box contained sociology, history, finance, design, computer programming—even several ancient Chinese texts, not to mention some unfinished novels. I felt it was quite eclectic. Who could tell from this pile what job the owner does? So I came to a conclusion related to my long-standing behavior that probably confuses people—for example, today seeing me tinkering with Photoshop, tomorrow seeing me neglecting meals to edit code, the day after seeing me frantically memorizing vocabulary, and the day after that borrowing history books from the library. The fundamental motivation behind these strange behaviors is my desire to become an erudite person. One hour I can explain to others the positive and negative impacts of the Taiping Rebellion on Chinese history; the next hour I can explain implementing rounded rectangles in HTML5; and after that analyze macroeconomic trends. But I don't actually love this knowledge—it's just what I have to learn to become erudite. That's why I bought these difficult books.

Ideals may be the origin of tragedy. When someone tries to attend to too many things, the most likely outcome is that nothing gets done well. This afternoon I was restless at home, feeling time was being wasted, and waves of panic hit me: I needed to add a special page to the company website; then I remembered the personal business cards I'd been designing for months still weren't done; I also needed to carve a seal; and the books I bought today still weren't read—there were so many things it was infuriating! After agonizing for a while, I first read dozens of pages from the books I bought today, took a long afternoon nap, then studied small seal script and large seal script for carving, and started making my own seal.

When you're immersed in a task, the whole world becomes irrelevant. While carving the seal, I experienced the same feeling I used to have when writing code—my mind grew calmer and calmer, wanting only to focus on this, striving for perfection. Suddenly I realized: with an impatient mind, you can't learn anything. Calmness is the true method to becoming erudite.

posts.title: No Travel in October
posts.excerpt: As usual, every October there's a travelogue—Kunming and Dali in 2010; Qionghai in Xichang in 2011; Meili Snow Mountain, Shangri-La, and Lijiang in 2012. For 2013 I planned to write *A Record of Cycli...
posts.content:
As usual, every October there's a travelogue—Kunming and Dali in 2010; Qionghai in Xichang in 2011; Meili Snow Mountain, Shangri-La, and Lijiang in 2012. For 2013 I planned to write *A Record of Cycling Around Qinghai Lake*—bike, tent, and sleeping bag were all ready. But the company wouldn't give time off, so it got delayed. I'm not particularly regretful though. Money's been tight lately—the prime lens and rMBP still aren't sorted. Plus October's Qinghai Lake is quite cold, not ideal for lakeside camping. Next spring or summer when the rapeseed flowers blanket the lakeshore, if I can go with friends, that would be great.

Although the long trip didn't happen, my OCD mindset forced me to visit some Chengdu attractions to experience "crowds." Here's what happened that day: the night before, I'd arranged with a girl to go to Wenjiang's food festival. But the next morning, my dad suddenly wanted the whole family to go there. What a coincidence—Chengdu is so big, yet everyone had to squeeze into that one spot. How many foodies does the Land of Abundance really have? The girl's family didn't know about it. If she and I went too and we all ran into each other, it would basically turn into a blind date setup. So I had to find an excuse not to go with my family, then discussed changing locations with her. But after thinking it over, we couldn't find anywhere interesting. I really wanted to slap myself—can't even find a place for a date, what a wimp!

In the end, I went out alone with my DSLR. No matter how alone I am, I won't let myself look too lonely. Unless you're a twin, I believe people are born lonely. But happy people find their own ways to entertain themselves—some find friends, some spend money on wine, some spend ten years honing a craft. Their common trait is "staying busy."

I wanted to shoot some street photography, so I went to three places: Kuanzhai Alley, People's Park, and Jinli. The entrance to Kuanzhai Alley was too crowded—I couldn't get in. Then I went to People's Park next door and managed to enter. Right inside the main gate was a small lake with dozens of small boats carrying tourists floating on it—yellow and green boats, tourists chatting and laughing. It was nearing noon and I was a bit hungry, so I bought a bowl of soybean flower and sat on the flower bed by the lake to eat, completely ignoring the passing pedestrians. I remember as a kid secretly flipping through my uncle's photo album in the countryside. He was probably twenty then, standing cautiously in front of the Zhaojue Temple Zoo gate and some park amusement areas, taking many photos. The almost faded photos seemed to have those same yellow-and-green boats. A wave of time-travel nostalgia hit me. Decades from now, People's Park will probably be the same—it's nice that generations share the same memories. Later I went to Jinli. It was also packed. I protected my camera and moved forward with the crowd. Jinli is no different from most ancient streets in China. It's a pity that real historical sites can't be preserved in cities like this—very uninteresting.

After that, I worked three more days, then had another two-day weekend, and it was already the 7th. In Yibin, Brother Jin organized a class reunion and called the eight of us from Chengdu to Shizishan Chuanshi South Gate Food Plaza for a meal. Everyone drank about seven bottles of beer and three people got knocked out on the spot. We've been graduated for over a year, met up two or three times, and each time was more enjoyable than the last. But think about it—besides classmates and friends, who else can you drink so uninhibitedly with?

posts.title: 2013 Year-End Summary
posts.excerpt: I don't know how many people are willing to write their own biography. I feel like I'm writing my own chronicle. The content for 2013 is roughly: "In the first year of the Celestial Empire's dream ful...
posts.content:
I don't know how many people are willing to write their own biography. I feel like I'm writing my own chronicle. The content for 2013 is roughly: "In the first year of the Celestial Empire's dream fulfillment, in March Hongguang entered the room and filled the hall with light, taking it as a sacred sign. Thus he resigned from his post at the East Gate and went to the West Gate to join the wine-making workshop as a painter. He worked diligently for over half a year. That year, the government was harmonious and everything went smoothly—nothing else to report." Okay, just kidding. At least there are a few other things to mention.

First, my financial situation is not optimistic. All children from humble backgrounds definitely realize after entering society that money is a good thing—I really lack money. I thought I'd have ten thousand yuan saved by year's end, but actually ended up with nothing. The money that turned into "fixed asset investments" includes: a DSLR for over seven thousand, a desktop computer host for five thousand, a phone for seventeen hundred, a mountain bike for fourteen hundred, a table for four hundred, a second-hand router, and some odds and ends like a wallet and soy milk maker. My book-hoarding habit has been cured—bought a little over two hundred yuan worth of books all year and basically didn't finish reading them. Although the company provides meals and my parents provide housing, the rest of the money really doesn't know where it went. Also, under family pressure and practical circumstances, my parents put a house on my head. Starting from 2014, I'll have to pay over two thousand yuan in monthly loan repayments. Honestly, after getting the house, I often feel like a bird with its feathers plucked—those wishes that were once within reach now feel so far away. If there's still an economic plan for the new year, I still hope to save ten thousand yuan.

Second, trying to make myself more interesting. This year at the new company, I've met more middle-aged people, especially batches of applicants who come intermittently. In them I see incompetence, helplessness, and the "broken jar, broken throw" mentality. Maybe they weren't like this originally, but society forced them into it with no way out—can't learn skills and can't find connections. If there's still hope for the second half of life, they can only pin their hopes on the welfare lottery. To avoid tragedy, the reality before us is to accumulate more and learn more—for example, learning Python programming, carving leisure seals, observing how leaders handle things, or even preparing a stand-up routine that can fill any occasion or some dirty jokes to amuse girls. These are what I call the内涵 (connotation) of being "interesting" and my life philosophy. I believe if I persist, at eighty I'll still be able to chat and laugh with everyone.

Third, I'm still a good person. Actually, I'd rather be a bad person—if I didn't like you, why the hell would I be good to you? My brain must be waterlogged!

Fourth, becoming an ordinary person. Although I once wanted to walk a bit on the artsy path, seeing some people acting noble, aloof, and detached from worldly affairs gradually made me disgusted. But I don't mean to disparage "artsy"—I just can't stand people using the name of artsiness to pretend sophistication, pretend taste, and play the cultured fool. You were born poor—why pretend to be pretentious? If you love reading, read; if you love rock, rock; if you love coffee, drink it. Why drink ten-yuan red wine and have to swirl it in a stemmed glass for ages? Why eat Western food once and have to reflect on about Britain's warmth? Why see a full moon and stay up all night writing jueju poems? Really, there aren't that many causes and effects, inevitabilities and coincidences. Life doesn't have that many rituals. If you want to laugh, laugh; if you want to cry, cry; if you like it, like it; if you hate it, hate it.

A whole year—probably that's all there is to say.

posts.title: 2014 Vietnam Travelogue
posts.excerpt: I went traveling — to Vietnam, during the Spring Festival. The reason for going was simple: in order to have a passport ready so I could decide to go to Nepal — crossed off as item fourteen on my wish...
posts.content:
I went traveling — to Vietnam, during the Spring Festival.

The reason for going was simple: in order to have a passport ready so I could decide to go to Nepal — crossed off as item fourteen on my wish list — at any moment in the future, I went ahead and applied for one in advance (it took two trips back to my hometown county to finally get it done). After seeing what I'd done, Xiao Zhang also applied for a passport. Coincidentally, our passports became effective on the very same day. Like the impulse to rush into a scenic area the moment you've bought a ticket, she said, "Let's go to Vietnam," and I said, "Sure, it's not expensive anyway." Our fourteen-day Spring Festival holiday finally had a destination, and we'd also make up for missing Qinghai Lake during the National Day holiday.

Going to Vietnam had been her dream since college — perhaps influenced by some artsy film or artistic photo — she said the Vietnamese ao dai was beautiful. Perhaps under the incoherent rhetoric of "what's national is universal," we've all grown accustomed to viewing other ethnic groups' customs with a neutral stance, generally believing that "if you think it's ugly, it's because you don't understand it, you don't understand that people's culture; so you've made a wrong judgment; actually it's very good and beautiful!" — this obsessive-compulsive conclusion. But Xiao Zhang wasn't aesthetically obsessive; I, on the other hand, held firm to my own aesthetic tastes: China's qipao, in every detail — its cut, fabric, and embroidery — already surpasses the ao dai. And a qipao worn by a woman is absolutely not a temptation that can be satisfied merely by gently running one's hands along its clinging curves.

Before October, I had never imagined I'd visit Vietnam in my lifetime. Aside from knowing that Da Nang was beautiful, I had no interest in the country at all. So as departure approached, I hadn't even prepared a decent itinerary. I told Xiao Zhang: "Let's just wander — wherever we end up, that's where we end up."

Before Xiao Zhang returned to her hometown for work in July, we met up; in November there was also Xiao Luo, and we spent half a day together; and then came this trip — on the 28th she arrived in Chengdu, and after a long separation we were warmly catching up, sharing experiences on how to slip away from family during the Spring Festival.

That night we flew to Nanning. I had arranged for a girl I'd met on the Qinghai-Tibet Railway, Qing, to take us to eat laoyoufen (old friend noodles). I felt truly guilty — because of a flight delay, we didn't arrive in downtown Nanning until midnight, ruining her sweet dreams. After that, she took us to Zhongshan Road to experience the local street food. At the T-junction on Zhongshan Road, in front of the honey-glazed pork rib stall that Qing recommended as the best, Xiao Zhang stared at Qing for a good while before saying she looked a lot like Sanmao. I probably wouldn't notice who resembles whom in my entire life, but Qing really did look like her. The honey-glazed ribs were delicious too.

Our first meal was laoyoufen, recommended by Qing. But my college roommate, a Nanning local, had spent four years raving about Guangxi's signature dish being luosifen (snail rice noodles). My goodness — he was recommending Liuzhou's specialty! And he was from Nanning!! Laoyoufen is made by first stir-frying a jumble of ingredients in a wok, then mixing them with the noodles into a bowl. The taste was rather peculiar, with a slightly sour tang. If not for everyone around me eating with such relish, I would've thought the ingredients were unhygienic. Fortunately, after eating it two or three times, I got used to it. Nanning also has pickled fruit, available in sweet and sour varieties. The sweet kind is passable, but the sour kind is truly bizarre.

The next day, we took an international bus from Langdong Bus Station to Hanoi — a seven-hour journey costing 168 yuan, including the half-hour-plus at the Friendship Pass border checkpoint. After clearing customs, we switched to another bus. The coach traveled along a two-lane highway, and through the window I saw lush little hills, colorful Vietnamese-style buildings, and more and more people on motorbikes wearing green conical hats. Occasionally we passed through towns where streetsides were lined with countless kumquats and chrysanthemums for sale — just like the Spring Festival customs in Guangdong.

We didn't arrive in Hanoi until 9 PM. Following other passengers from our bus, we slipped out to Hanoi's iconic landmark — Hoan Kiem Lake. Oh my! It was basically a small pond, surrounded by a ring of neon lights, and yet it was packed with people. Well, this was Hanoi's city center. After Xiao Zhang and I found our accommodation and had a bowl of some unnameable noodle soup, we went back to Hoan Kiem Lake for another walk, chatting about her love life and my crushes, just like the young Vietnamese people around us. We didn't plan to stay long and booked flights to Ho Chi Minh City for the next day — Vietjet Air was only a little over 500 RMB per person. Later we learned this was Vietnam's cheapest airline, and it didn't provide complimentary meals.

Long before the plane approached Ho Chi Minh City, we could already see vast expanses of urban sprawl. The buildings weren't tall but were neatly arranged, stretching from the bay all the way to Tan Son Nhat Airport within the city. Stepping off the plane, we were hit by a heatwave of over 30 degrees. Fortunately I'd checked the weather beforehand and boarded in a T-shirt, while Xiao Zhang's coat draped over her arm clearly betrayed that she'd just flown in from the north.

Ho Chi Minh City's most famous tourist hub is Pham Ngu Lao Street (范五老街). I'm not sure how to spell it in Vietnamese, but when I pronounced it in Chinese, the taxi driver actually understood and dropped us off there. Although it might seem silly to judge this way, Vietnamese does indeed share many similar-sounding words with Chinese.

Please forgive my pretentiousness, nostalgia, and eagerness to show off after reading *The Lover* — from here on I'll refer to "Ho Chi Minh City" by its old name, "Saigon." I also hold a conviction: folk customs habitually violated by politics will eventually be vindicated. Saigon is no exception — once the Communist Party of Vietnam falls, this city will surely reclaim its original name. Here are two examples: Stalingrad was called "Tsaritsyn" before 1925, "Stalingrad" from 1925 to 1961, and "Volgograd" after 1961. In 2013, to commemorate the famous Battle of Tsaritsyn that Stalin led here in 1918, "Volgograd" is renamed "Stalingrad" on six commemorative days each year. Additionally, Zhongshan City, named after the founding father Sun Yat-sen, has only a little over a million residents, and his portrait is only displayed on Tiananmen Square once a year on October 1st. See — six days a year and a small prefecture-level city is the appropriate measure for commemorating a famous historical figure and a revolutionary who led a nation's progress. Ho Chi Minh's significance in Vietnamese history has undoubtedly been exaggerated.

In Saigon, you could rent a motorbike for about five or six US dollars. I originally told the hostel owner I wanted a white Vespa-style scooter, but he quietly left me with an ordinary Honda — the kind you see everywhere on every street. It was disappointing, but I couldn't be bothered to argue. If Chengdu hadn't banned motorcycles, I might have already bought the Vespa knockoff I'd been eyeing for years.

"Any travel not aimed at experiencing natural scenery is a scam" — this has been my declaration on the meaning of travel countless times, and it's also why every trip I take is bound to be arduous. My wish list includes trekking in Nepal and cycling around Qinghai Lake — enjoying the scenery while also enduring physical and mental self-torture. Although a recent odd report suggested that when people are pushed to the brink of endurance and mental breakdown, they are closest to the divine, I don't pursue that.

Xiao Zhang wore a backless spaghetti-strap dress with vertical stripes in red, white, and green, her long hair cascading over her shoulders. Under the sea-blue sunshine at 10 degrees north latitude, she radiated youth and artistic flair, and my heart couldn't help but flutter. At that moment, she was a vacationer who had traveled far; and I, wearing a plain grayish-white T-shirt paired with slightly scuffed jeans and earthy-yellow New Balance suede sneakers, was the picture of a backpacker. To outsiders, we must have looked like an odd travel duo, but I hadn't realized it — and from the start, I hadn't really pondered her travel mindset either.

Only by joining Saigon's motorbike swarm can you truly integrate into their daily life. Riding this Honda, I imagined myself in a *Roman Holiday* scenario, carrying Xiao Zhang careening through Saigon. Unable to read the street names on the map, I simply rode toward Saigon's tallest building, the Bitexco Financial Tower. Although this building wasn't even as tall as my office in Chengdu, it stood out like a crane among chickens in Saigon — visible from anywhere in the city. I thought that must be the city center, but when I got there, it was just like anywhere else in China — no miracle.



Beside the Financial Tower flowed the endlessly surging Mekong River. I leaned on the railing and gazed for a long time — in my entire life I had never seen such a wide river. Even before this, apart from Erhai Lake and Dianchi Lake, I hadn't even seen the sea. The Mekong originates on the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau and is called the Lancang River in China. In October 2012, I had passed through the Lancang River in Deqin County. The river channel was narrow, the water muddy yellow, the current turbulent, and the roaring sound was so powerful it felt like it could suck me in — truly terrifying. After flowing thousands of kilometers and settling its sediment at power stations along the way, the Mekong in Saigon had become broad and calm. There was a port along the bank, and a seagoing vessel was unloading cargo. At that moment I thought of the film *The Lover* — the female protagonist, at a similar spot, forever left Vietnam, left her lover behind. In a few days I too would leave Vietnam, not knowing what feelings I'd carry with me, and a wave of melancholy washed over me.

Looking toward the Mekong River delta, I spotted a tall bridge rising in the distance and suggested we go take a look.

It was a towering bridge, and from it you could see ships slowly gliding across the river in the distance. After crossing the bridge, we suddenly found ourselves in the countryside — the roads were broken and muddy, chickens and ducks and kids playing football were everywhere, and the houses on both sides were low and dilapidated, much like slums in Chinese cities. The people on the road looked at Xiao Zhang and me with indifference. I felt a hint of fear, twisted the throttle harder, and sped through.

As the sun set, we'd been riding for nearly two hours and still hadn't made it back to the city from this rural area. Xiao Zhang was tired from sitting in the back, slumped against my back and making my neck ache. I said, "Don't you think it's heartwarming, watching those young couples on motorbikes rushing home in the setting sun?" She mhm'd, and I continued, "We're heading back to the city too — why don't you learn from those young women on the bikes behind and just hold onto me?" Then Xiao Zhang punched me in the back — hard!

posts.title: 2018 Year in Review
posts.excerpt: Since life hasn't undergone earth-shattering changes, I haven't written a year-end review in quite a few years. Its existence is necessarily a ritual — something worth commemorating. The few reviews I...
posts.content:
Since life hasn't undergone earth-shattering changes, I haven't written a year-end review in quite a few years. Its existence is necessarily a ritual — something worth commemorating.

The few reviews I've written before were nothing more than summaries of what I'd done and what material things I'd acquired, never touching on lessons or wisdom. My family has no sages, no family philosophy, not even any worldly experience to pass down. Fortunately, this year I read a highly valuable bestselling book — *Principles* by Ray Dalio — which, for someone from my upbringing,coincidentally fills those gaps.

This moment in 2018 is slipping away at breakneck speed in a dwindling countdown. As the year comes to a close, in what spirit should I look back on it? And in the coming year, with what expectations should I welcome it? Applying the methods from *Principles*, what life lessons can I distill?

Human behavior is determined by one's own cognition, and human change, at its root, is cognitive change. When my actions reached a dead end, I carefully examined some of my past perspectives, identified their shortcomings, and improved upon them. From many angles, it was finally in 2018 that I got on the right track: choosing one person to grow old with, settling on a lifelong career, and actively exploring things beyond my current abilities.

During the last Spring Festival, my family gathered around the stove for an evening conversation and brought up a past event. Grandpa overheard their discussion and immediately shut it down with a forceful stance. Around that time I had just read *Soft Burial* and recalled a line: "There are always things in this world not worth remembering. Or rather, there are some things and some people you must forget." I immediately understood his intention. The experiences of 2017 had left me mired in prolonged suffering from which I couldn't extricate myself. I drew inspiration from that experience and soon let go of those troubles. There is no return journey in memory — the past cannot be changed. Only by facing the future with a completely new and fearless, optimistic mindset can one move forward.

Writing about "fearlessness" reminds me of something I wrote on Twitter: "The more ignorant you are of history, the more fearless you become; the more thoroughly you understand it, the more it chills you to the bone." This was a comment I made regarding a major event this year. When a person is praised by another, they always feel happy and full of confidence. But when flattered by a crowd, it's easy to become overconfident and blindly arbitrary — especially when that person's power is limitless, so vast that it cannot be openly discussed. Civilization and peace are the products of compromise, and in extreme conditions, certain rulers prefer to exploit, suppress, and plunder. Liu Liangcheng wrote in *One Person's Village*: "In some years, we can only barely survive by becoming beasts of burden — those were not days fit for human beings." I worry deeply, and I don't want to end up in Jiabiangou, so I simply cleared out my Weibo account and became a silent beast.

Clearing Weibo wasn't just out of fear — another reason was that I'd had enough of the excessive and utterly worthless information feed. I spent too much idle time on social media over the years, and I neither monetized any of that information nor became an influencer of any kind. Comparing the time cost invested with the informational value gained, it was a losing proposition. I then deleted the Weibo mobile app, and life seemed not to be affected at all. Now I only use Twitter to record my own thoughts, my blog for long-form essays, and Netflix for TV dramas; occasionally I browse V2EX and tech websites. Return to life, embrace simplicity to its core — this is a result that the younger me, who loved flashy clothes and spirited living, could never have imagined.

When I visited a childhood friend's house in the first half of the year, he said he had no dreams — his only goal now was to make money. What a coincidence — my goal is also to make money! And I set a small target every year. The 2018 target wasn't fully achieved — I reached 94.4%.

The economic outlook for 2019 is widely expected to face major problems. I currently have no savings, only a self-occupied home, so I'm not worried about asset depreciation. My mortgage is one-eighth of household income, far below the one-third safety line. The liquor industry I work in, if the information is accurate, actually performs better when the economy worsens. However, my parents' income in recent years has declined significantly, and my younger brother is still in high school — factors I cannot ignore. If the economy continues to decline in the coming years, my parents may have to return to the countryside. The hometown has enough land for cash crops, which might be a viable path. I've always longed to build an American-style house in my hometown, but I currently lack the opportunity and funding. If my parents go back, I'll work in that direction!

posts.title: Simplicity and Luxury
posts.excerpt: If someone increasingly yearns for a simple life—growing flowers, birds, insects, and fish; strolling through lush, dense parks; buying natural, unpretentious objects; making two simple yet nutritious...
posts.content:
If someone increasingly yearns for a simple life—growing flowers, birds, insects, and fish; strolling through lush, dense parks; buying natural, unpretentious objects; making two simple yet nutritious-looking dishes; arranging a tidy space; preferring a clean computer desktop; always discarding unnecessary, useless items; avoiding noisy environments; listening to quiet music—what kind of mindset is this? And if they also like using expensive brands, what kind of mindset is that?

All of the above are my current symptoms. I hate anything that looks complicated and disorganized, and try to maintain a simple living state. Although simplicity is often associated with plainness, everything I love isn't plain—most of the time it actually seems quite luxurious.

Simple yet luxurious—the literal meaning is truly a contradictory state. Simplicity's imagery includes white, elegance, and lightness; luxury's imagery includes black, complexity, and heaviness. Now these two things are glued together like laminate flooring, appearing quite tangled. But some brands have achieved this—they sell raw wood furniture for more than others' multi-layered lacquered furniture, and sell clothes with no patterns for more than others' elaborately silver-embossed, flocked, or jacquard-woven clothes. Simplicity and luxury in these single products aren't entirely expressed through external form: simplicity shows on the outside, luxury lies in the内涵 (inner quality).

Liking to grow flowers and raise dogs first requires sufficient living space; hobbies of play and entertainment require money first; buying simple furniture means higher design and material costs. The life I like is mostly like this—it looks simple, but is actually harder to achieve than many dreams.

I always have a sense of powerlessness. Those dreams would surely be easy to realize in ancient times. Modern people love new things too much and have almost lost their own pursuits, instead living increasingly complicated lives.

People and things—let's keep them simple.

posts.title: 2025 Vietnam-Guangxi-Yunnan Road Trip
posts.excerpt: August in Chengdu was blazing hot — the ground felt like it was warping from the heat, and being outdoors was no different from ascetic practice. So after my kid graduated from kindergarten, she was s...
posts.content:
August in Chengdu was blazing hot — the ground felt like it was warping from the heat, and being outdoors was no different from ascetic practice. So after my kid graduated from kindergarten, she was stuck at home all day. Whenever she was thoroughly bored, she'd always ask me: "Dad, what else can I play?"

I knew exactly what she meant — she wanted to watch TV. So I said, "How about our whole family goes to the beach!"

And just like that, we went. The route for this trip wasn't specially planned — we had just two goals:

First, burn through the rest of summer vacation outside.
Second, we had to go to the seaside.

No detailed itinerary — wherever was fun, we'd stay and play. Going from Chengdu to the eastern coast was far too distant, and the seawater wasn't clean anyway. Guangxi was a simpler choice. First stop: Fangchenggang. Second stop: we'd decide after we finished the first.

We departed Chengdu at 8:30 AM and arrived in Maotai Town by 1 PM. We spent an hour and a half there eating lamb with the skin on, and also got caught in a sudden thunderstorm. We kept driving until 6:30 PM — it had just gotten dark — and stayed in Luodian County, the last county before leaving Guizhou. The next day we set off again at 8:30 AM and by 2 PM we had arrived in Fangchenggang.



**Dongxing and Mong Cai, Vietnam**

The sky was drizzling, and Fangchenggang's inner sea was murky. I remembered back in '23 when we went to Zhuhai, the kid saw the yellow ocean beside Jinwan Airport and said, "Dad, that's not the sea — seawater should be blue." To show her a truly blue ocean, we first went to the popular Bailangtan (White Wave Beach) scenic area. The overcast weather drained the ocean of its color, but this time the kid wasn't depressed about it at all. She found shells on the beach and happily spent the whole afternoon picking them.

For the next few days we stayed in Dongxing City. We were already at the national border — it would've been a shame not to get an exit stamp in the passport. Getting a temporary visa here was incredibly easy — a little over 200 yuan per person, issued the same day. When entering Vietnam with a temporary visa, you had to surrender your passport to them, and they'd return it when you came back to China. Without our passports, we could only explore within Mong Cai.



Entering a new country, I thought the kid would be at least a little curious to explore. But after a ten-minute City Walk through Mong Cai, she got bored and asked when we were going back to Dongxing — she wanted to play at the beach. So for this brief Mong Cai itinerary, I quickly tried to imprint the image of Vietnam on her: the ubiquitous single-star red flags, tall and narrow houses, brightly painted murals on walls, roads jam-packed with deafening motorbikes, Vietnamese drip coffee, conical hats, and even Chinese horseshoe crabs (a protected species in China) being slaughtered at the market.



At noon we returned to Dongxing and drove to Wanwei Golden Beach. Just as we arrived, the sun was setting. The golden beach bathed in the afterglow fulfilled every imagination we had of the sea. Waves lapped against the extraordinarily long sandy shore, fishing boat motors chugged lazily back to port, and the distant sunset cast dazzling golden edges through dark clouds on the horizon. It was so beautiful we decided to just stay at Golden Beach.



**Dongna Yuwan and Detian Waterfall**

After two days of digging sand at Wanwei Golden Beach, we were getting a bit tired of it. We set off for Guangxi's second attraction, Dongna Yuwan. It's a pastoral landscape located in Chongzuo, not far from the surrounding Mingshi Pastoral area and Detian Waterfall — all visitable together. Dongna Yuwan takes about half a day to explore, so we arrived around noon, played in the afternoon, and then went directly to stay inside the Detian Waterfall scenic area so we could enter early the next morning.



Detian Waterfall belongs to both China and Vietnam. On our side, it's built up with all the amenities you'd expect from a Chinese scenic area — crowded with an endless stream of tourists. On the Vietnamese side, it's like a wild, undeveloped spot — the roads aren't even paved, visitors are sparse, and the few people there looked like they were selling cigarettes.



**Jingxi County Town**

Summer in Guangxi is a paradise for water play. Leaving Detian Waterfall, the road to Jingxi County town passed several natural waterfalls where you could stop and play. We specifically chose a hotel in Jingxi County town right next to Dalongtan Reservoir. This reservoir is particularly interesting — it was originally an underground river over 10 kilometers long that burst to the surface here, forming a natural pool. Later, artificial embankments were built, creating a 750-mu reservoir. Because of the enormous water volume, the overflow from the dam formed a spectacular waterfall. What's even more incredible is that the riverbed downstream of the waterfall is actually tiled — it's a natural waterfall swimming pool!



Perhaps the water was too clean — when I brought the kid to play, I saw some mint leaves floating by. Looking up, I saw the BBQ vendor on the dam washing vegetables right in the river! The swimmers nearby didn't object either — they must have been used to it, after all, this is also the drinking water source for Jingxi County. So when I saw my kid drinking river water and spraying it around while swimming, I gave up on scolding her. Just play, just play!



**Xishuangbanna**

After two days in Jingxi County, we'd already been in Guangxi for a week. I was getting a bit homesick, but Chengdu hadn't cooled down yet, so we couldn't go back. Our next stop was somewhere slightly closer to home — Xishuangbanna.

Jingxi County to Xishuangbanna was over 900 kilometers, requiring an overnight stop in Jianshui Ancient Town before arriving the next day. In August, Jianshui's evening temperature was only 25 degrees — a cool breeze brought a refreshing chill. We found a Yunnan wild mushroom hotpot restaurant in the ancient town, which naturally included jian shou qing (see-hand-green mushrooms). The kid was worried about seeing "little people" (hallucinations) after eating them, and hesitated with every bite. The next day I asked her, "Did you see any little people?" She smiled sheepishly.



The road from Jianshui to Xishuangbanna was mountainous and steep. The expressway had no auxiliary lanes, making autonomous driving virtually impossible the whole way — exhausting. Fortunately, the best reward a journey offers a traveler is knowing that beautiful scenery and delicious food await ahead. Thinking about that, excitement returned!

At the very first checkpoint in Xishuangbanna, the tropical rainforest gave us a little shock: while we were queued up in the car waiting for inspection, a sudden thunderstorm snapped a roadside tree in half — it missed my car by just a few meters. A close call. A few kilometers past the checkpoint, we found the ground was completely dry, with no trace of rain at all.

Arriving in Xishuangbanna near dusk, walking through the bustling streets, seeing car after car with northeastern license plates, I momentarily thought I'd arrived in Dongbei. Later we even checked into a hotel run by people from Dongbei and ate Xishuangbanna's local snacks cooked by Dongbei people.

Xishuangbanna's defining feature might not actually be Dai culture. Take the Starlight Night Market, for instance — its architectural style is no different from Thailand's. Tourists fill the streets and alleys. No passport needed, no need to go abroad, no risk of organ harvesting, and you can still experience Thai vibes — pretty good. But I'd recommend avoiding the local bizarre-looking street food — not sure where we picked something up, but our kid got diarrhea and we had to go to the hospital in the middle of the night.



The most worthwhile place to visit in Xishuangbanna is the Chinese Academy of Sciences Tropical Botanical Garden. It's a directly affiliated public institution of the CAS, serving as a comprehensive research institution integrating scientific research, species conservation, and science popularization education, as well as a renowned scenic destination both domestically and internationally. The garden covers approximately 1,125 hectares, collects over 13,000 species of living plants, has 39 specialized plant zones, and preserves a primeval tropical rainforest of about 250 hectares — one of the largest botanical gardens in China by area, species richness, and number of specialized zones. But because it's simply too large and the temperature was high, we only finished the East Zone before leaving the park.





**Return Journey**

Xishuangbanna was our last stop. After charging up outside the Tropical Botanical Garden around 2-3 PM, we set off toward Kunming — our goal was to get back to Chengdu the next day. We tried to cover as much ground as possible today. Passing Kunming in the evening, the massive Dianchi Lake was bathed in brilliant blue sunlight, so we got off the expressway and lingered by the lake for a while.



It wasn't until past 9 PM that we found a place to stay in Xundian County town. We found the most popular Zhaotong-style BBQ to wrap up the day's journey.



The next day we set off again at 10 AM. Along the way, we stopped at Hejiangmen in Yibin to feed the fish, and finally got home at 8 PM.

**Transportation Summary**

Our vehicle is a Great Wall dual-mode plug-in hybrid DHT-PHEV with four-wheel drive. This trip lasted 12 days total, starting with a zeroed odometer. Total distance driven: 4,891 km. Fuel cost: 1,948 yuan. Charging cost: 223 yuan. Unit fuel cost: 0.398 yuan/km. Comprehensive energy cost: 0.44 yuan/km. Very satisfied with the experience!



Tolls for the entire trip were approximately 2,600 yuan, bringing total transportation costs to 4,771 yuan. Hotel accommodations were roughly 2,000-3,000 yuan. We didn't visit many ticketed attractions — probably around 1,000-2,000 yuan. Total trip cost was under 10,000 yuan.

**Travel Reflections**

I've developed a complete understanding of the Sichuan Basin. It's no longer just elevation data on paper — it's a real, tangible experience. Driving north before, crossing the Daba Mountains and Qinling, I knew the difficulty of the Shu roads. This time heading south, I felt dizzy from the high altitude in Kunming and Zhaotong, and the roads through the twisting Wumeng Mountains were limited to 80-100 km/h. Once we descended into Sichuan territory, suddenly the mountains seemed smaller, the roads straighter, and we could drive faster. To the west of the basin, of course, standing in Chengdu at 500 meters elevation, you can see Yaomei Peak at 6,247 meters. To the east, only the Three Gorges serves as an outlet for the basin's water to flow out. The entire southwest region (Yunnan, Guizhou, Sichuan, Tibet) is like a well-kneaded ball of dough, and the Sichuan Basin is the depression you make when you punch your fist hard into it. Being able to live in such a small, precious piece of land, I feel especially fortunate.

Furthermore, comparing topography, landforms, and climate with the areas surrounding the basin (indeed the entire western China), Chengdu is a uniquely irreplaceable presence across this vast territory. These favorable factors determine Chengdu's eternal economic position in the western region.

posts.title: Buendía
posts.excerpt: > Caption: Buendía is the name of a family in Gabriel García Márquez's famous novel *One Hundred Years of Solitude*. Every member of this family lives in their own world, caring only about the things ...
posts.content:
> Caption: Buendía is the name of a family in Gabriel García Márquez's famous novel *One Hundred Years of Solitude*. Every member of this family lives in their own world, caring only about the things that matter to them.

---

A former colleague shared photos of her kid on WeChat Moments today. For someone around 30, this is a common occurrence. I imagine that as we grow old, our classmates' Moments feeds will gradually and permanently stop updating — and that too will be a common occurrence. As long as we live in this world, none of us can escape birth, and none of us can avoid death.

My own child was also born this year — a lovely little daughter. Sometimes I get annoyed with her too. I hope she's always happy, always giggling at me foolishly. But the moment things don't go her way, she bursts into tears, expressing her demands at maximum volume: hungry, sleepy, bored, wants daddy to hold her... But how could I possibly tell which one it is? I tried a hundred different approaches and none of them satisfied her. Why is she crying? I was helpless! I had to ask my mom to feed her some milk, letting the child drift into a sleep induced by a blood sugar spike.

Last weekend I dropped her and her mother off at my mother-in-law's place. Colleagues at the company congratulated me on getting a break — clearly they'd all been through it. They had been sleeping with me for months, and now the bed felt empty. Late at night, in the quiet hours, I missed them terribly. How much joy had the child's presence brought to an otherwise lifeless room!

It was precisely because the child's noise and commotion enriched the family that I found myself reflecting with regret: during my own upbringing, I never brought my parents peace of mind. Every evening after dinner, I'd shut myself in my room to play on the computer, not saying a word, perfectly quiet. My younger brother has the same habit now. The two of us sons neither enjoy communicating with our parents. But we can't entirely blame ourselves — our father is a taciturn man, never cracking a smile at home. Influenced by his introverted personality from childhood, our own personalities became somewhat similar. We are like the Buendía family — each living our own life, with only blood ties binding us together.

That I can reflect on this is my daughter's good fortune! The emotional atmosphere of a family must not be allowed to affect another generation. I hope my daughter will be more outgoing. If she doesn't come to her dad for a fuss or a play, then her dad will go to her — like a fool, happily accompanying her as she grows up.

posts.title: A Drawing of my daughter
posts.excerpt: This is a drawing my daughter made when she was 4 years old. It depicts our family picking rainbow fruit in a garden. Neither of us has any talent for painting, so we never taught her any drawing skil...
posts.content:
This is a drawing my daughter made when she was 4 years old. It depicts our family picking rainbow fruit in a garden. Neither of us has any talent for painting, so we never taught her any drawing skills, but we always encouraged her to paint what she sees and thinks.

During childhood, children have many questions about the world that need to be explained by us. But can you answer them all? Never! Absolutely not! Rather than teaching them incorrect knowledge, it's better to keep quiet and let the children explore the answers on their own.

posts.title: Journey to Western Yunnan: Shangri-La — Deqin
posts.excerpt: **D1: Shangri-La** One hour before the holiday, the boss said we'd return to work on the 6th of National Day. My heart immediately sank—did the planned 8-day itinerary have to be scrapped? I wasn't wi...
posts.content:
**D1: Shangri-La**

One hour before the holiday, the boss said we'd return to work on the 6th of National Day. My heart immediately sank—did the planned 8-day itinerary have to be scrapped? I wasn't willing! They could deduct my salary however they wanted—more wouldn't make me a rich man, less wouldn't turn me into a poor ghost. But life experiences can't be bought with any amount of money.

The day before departure was Mid-Autumn Festival. When I woke up, there was heavy rain outside the window. Because there were too many uncertainties, after eating a piece of mooncake the company gave, I went to the bus station to buy a ticket to Shangri-La for October 1st. I originally wanted to take a more comfortable hard-seat bus to Lijiang first, then transfer to Shangri-La, but the buses to Lijiang were 20-30 yuan more expensive than usual, while the direct sleeper bus to Shangri-La was only a few yuan cheaper. So considering both economy and time, I went direct to Shangri-La. That's how traveling goes—lots of Schrödinger's cats.

Twelve years ago, Shangri-La was called Zhongdian. For tourism economic development, it was renamed to this now world-famous name, but to this day many people still call it Zhongdian County. There's only one sleeper bus to Shangri-La per day, taking 12 hours total. This was my first time on a sleeper bus. Besides the dirty, slightly smelly blanket, it was quite comfortable at first. After a few hours out of the city limits, the bus was crawling along mountain streams—roaring river on the right, rugged cliffs on the left. Although the driver must know the road conditions very well, "those who walk by the river often will eventually get their shoes wet." I couldn't control the bus's direction, but at least I might witness my own death, so I didn't dare close my eyes the whole way. Besides, the scenery along the way is also part of the journey. Later I couldn't stand viewing the scenery while lying down and wanted to sit up to look, only to discover how anti-human a sleeper bus is as a passenger vehicle—my head almost touched the ceiling, windows were below eye level, even more uncomfortable than lying down. In the end, I could only lie on my side to view the scenery. This posture was very strange—besides the ancient people in landscape paintings lying on the ground with feather fans and silk scarves admiring mountains, I really couldn't think of anyone else who appreciated the motherland's beautiful rivers and mountains while lying flat.

The bus passed through Huaping and Yongsheng counties before reaching Lijiang. If it were road conditions in the eastern regions, it would be ordinary, but unfortunately this is the western mountainous area—we spent six hours to get there. The more dangerous section was Jin'an Bridge Hydropower Station. After the bus followed the mountain stream to Jin'an Bridge, it wound along the mountain road for another 45 minutes and we could still see the river valley. It seemed that besides the altitude increasing, the straight-line distance to Lijiang hadn't changed at all. After Jin'an Bridge, we arrived at Yongsheng County seat in the river valley plain. Just outside the county seat, we saw tobacco planting bases for Zhonghua and Huanghelou brands. From this flat area, after walking a few hundred meters, the view suddenly opened up—as if suddenly suspended in mid-air. Below was a huge sedimentary plain. Sunlight pierced through gaps in distant clouds, stabbing straight light columns onto the golden dam, dotted with villages here and there. If the drop weren't so great, if we could still hear chickens and dogs, this dam would resemble Tao Yuanming's peach blossom spring.

The bus passed Lijiang at dusk. City lights twinkled, various out-of-town vehicles on the roadside—no interest at all. Good thing I was just passing through. Further ahead it became night. I couldn't see anything, lying on the bunk, watching the clear starlight outside the window and the moon just emerging over the eastern mountains. Memory took me back to childhood—spreading a mat in the pitch-black backyard, with some fear of the dark, propping my head on my arm to look up at the bright moon and stars in the sky, just as clear as tonight's Lijiang sky. Later I lived almost entirely in cities and couldn't see the starry sky at night. I completely forgot those strange childhood sensations and almost forgot who I was.

By the time we arrived in Shangri-La it was already midnight. The temperature was slightly cold, and my head throbbed faintly—maybe due to the high altitude, or more likely the exhaustion from the long journey causing some hysteria. Anyway, on my first night in Shangri-La, I found a guesthouse right across from the bus station and went to sleep.

**D2: Shangri-La Songzanlin Temple — Deqin Feilai Temple**

My first night sleeping in Shangri-La, my brain stayed in light sleep the whole night, feeling like I was flying close to the ground the entire time, drifting through many unfamiliar places in a daze. With anticipation for a wonderful journey on the Diqing Plateau, I woke up a little after 7 AM, couldn't wait to pull open the curtains to see what it looked like outside. My first glance was disappointing—the sky was predictably overcast with clouds, and since the cloud layer wasn't high, it felt quite oppressive. Plus, the streets below the window were covered in dust, and combined with the chaotic shops and cold morning, the city looked very rundown. But a minute or two later I cheered up—because the guesthouse I stayed at was on the edge of the county seat. Isn't that what the urban-rural fringe of Chinese cities looks like? I had asked the guesthouse owner the night before—he was from Guang'an, so we were technically fellow townsmen. He told me in Shangri-La I could visit Songzanlin Temple and Pudacuo. Considering my itinerary originally had no Shangri-La stop, but since I was here, not visiting one or two attractions would waste the 12-hour hardship, so I went to the nearby Songzanlin Temple.

Yunnan tourist tickets are already rip-offs. With the massive influx of National Day tourists, if they didn't discount, public resentment would surely rise. They probably knew this themselves, so the day before Mid-Autumn Festival, Yunnan tourist sites urgently cut ticket prices by 25%. Songzanlin Temple's ticket after discount was 91 yuan. I hadn't done any Shangri-La travel research beforehand and had no idea about Songzanlin Temple. Given such an expensive ticket, I assumed it must have magnificent architecture and boundless spiritual power. Looking at the ticket in my hand, it showed a very familiar, grand golden-roofed temple. It turned out to be the temple I'd seen over and over online called Songzanlin Temple. I came here without planning—it was fate.

Songzanlin Temple was much smaller than imagined, but from a distance its imposing presence was extremely grand. Up close, I realized it was built on a small hilltop, with small monk residences built all around it. With these monk quarters surrounding it like stars around the moon, the three golden-roofed main halls of Songzanlin Temple appeared splendid and moving.

Out of respect for faith, photography wasn't allowed inside the temple. I followed the temple's tour guide wandering inside. Unfortunately, even the sacred Sakyamuni Buddha Hall couldn't accommodate so much worldly noise—I heard nothing. Above the Buddha hall were eternal lamps for praying for family safety. Devotees had to give monks 10 yuan to light one. I requested one to be lit beneath the Arhat statue. Although I've grown up around Buddhism, I'm not a believer. Still, I fell victim to the Chinese habit of worshipping Buddha when seeing one and ghosts when seeing one. If I dig deeper, I was lighting it on behalf of my grandma—she's never left Sichuan. I wanted to tell her that in sacred Shangri-La, I lit a butter lamp for the family on her behalf.

After leaving Songzanlin Temple, I went straight back to the guesthouse where I'd slept the night before to pick up my backpack left with the owner, then caught a van to Deqin. There were seven passengers on the van—including me, only three tourists going to Feilai Temple, one Chongqing guy going to Benzilan on the Sichuan border to build a matsutake cold storage, and the rest going to Deqin County seat. On this Yunnan trip, I thank every driver who gave me a ride—they kept one hand on the wheel and one on the phone on narrow mountain roads without sending the car into the valley. The driver who took us to Feilai Temple was a twenty-something Tibetan guy. I chose his car because it was in good condition. On the way, we encountered a checkpoint. The traffic police asked him to show documents, and he could only produce the purchase certificate and driver's license—no other insurance. It turned out to be a new car. *facepalm* But he still drove while on the phone and safely delivered us to Feilai Temple.

My first sight at Feilai Temple, I didn't see Meili Snow Mountain. Despite being fully mentally prepared, I was still disappointed. I prayed for a strong wind to sweep away the clouds shrouding the peak. After sitting restlessly for a long time, I finally accepted reality. Then I wandered along National Highway 214 in the Feilai Temple scenic area, trying to find the Feilai Temple that isn't a place name. After walking one or two kilometers with no sign, I had to turn back. I ran into a girl who asked me how to get to Feilai Temple. I said I was also looking for it—let's find it together. Since we couldn't see the snow mountain anyway. Later we chatted about everything while walking slowly and wandering around. I discovered I wasn't as dull and speechless as I am in the city. It must be the freedom—on the road, I don't have to take responsibility for what I say, don't have to lie and cover up, don't have to have evil thoughts about people's money. I'm just a traveler who came specifically to see the scenery—nothing else. Later we actually found Feilai Temple—it was a small temple, a local people's Bodhisattva temple. Then we ate together—ordered a plate of stir-fried yak meat and a plate of green pepper potato shreds. After splitting the bill, we said goodbye. At that time I thought I'd never run into her again in this lifetime—she was returning to Shangri-La tomorrow, I was going to Yubeng.

posts.title: 2014 Strawberry Music Festival (Chengdu) Live
posts.excerpt: This was the Strawberry Music Festival's first time in Chengdu. I usually listen to music on Xiami and knew a few familiar artists, so I went with some friends. Chengdu had just stepped into summer, a...
posts.content:
This was the Strawberry Music Festival's first time in Chengdu. I usually listen to music on Xiami and knew a few familiar artists, so I went with some friends. Chengdu had just stepped into summer, and the atmosphere at the venue was electric. But the girls' outfits were refreshingly cool — hard to resist. This year's theme was "SNS life is rubbish, get a real life." I couldn't agree more — with so many girls around, who the hell wants to hang out online!

I have to admit Song Dongye was incredibly popular, but this kind of hot, intense music really needs more rhythm. Re-TROS had an amazing live presence. Lao Lang played all nostalgic songs: *Model Love Letter*, *The Brother Who Slept in the Bunk Above Me*, and of course the inevitable full-audience singalong of *My Deskmate*.

posts.title: Never Make Decisions When Angry
posts.excerpt: The period before the New Year is peak season for liquor sales, and every packaging supplier's production schedule is stretched to the limit. Yesterday, one supplier delayed a batch of bottle labels t...
posts.content:
The period before the New Year is peak season for liquor sales, and every packaging supplier's production schedule is stretched to the limit. Yesterday, one supplier delayed a batch of bottle labels that should have been delivered to us by one day. My boss tore into me over the phone, warning that if the distributor refused the goods, I'd be held jointly liable and would have to compensate the company out of my own pocket.
